topics.title: Definitions and Classification of Signals (Continuous-time and Discrete-time, Periodic and Aperiodic, Even and Odd, Energy and Power)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT403 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definitions and Classification of Signals (Continuous-time and Discrete-time, Periodic and Aperiodic, Even and Odd, Energy and Power)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition and Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Signal?

A **signal** is a function that conveys information about the state or behavior of a physical system. Mathematically, a signal is a mathematical function of one or more independent variables that carries information about a physical phenomenon.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A signal is a function of one or more independent variables, typically time, which contains information about the behavior or nature of a physical phenomenon. In engineering contexts, the independent variable is most commonly **time ($t$ or $n$)**, while the dependent variable represents amplitude (voltage, current, pressure, temperature, etc.).

**Formal Notation:**
- Continuous-time signal: $x(t)$, where $t \in \mathbb{R}$ (real numbers)
- Discrete-time signal: $x[n]$, where $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ (integers)

### 1.2 What is a System?

A **system** is a mathematical operator or transformation that maps an input signal to an output signal. In other words, it processes input signals to produce output signals according to defined rules.

$$y(t) = T\{x(t)\} \quad \text{(Continuous-time system)}$$

$$y[n] = T\{x[n]\} \quad \text{(Discrete-time system)}$$

where $T\{\cdot\}$ denotes the system transformation operator.

### 1.3 Continuous-Time vs Discrete-Time Signals

| Classification | Independent Variable | Notation | Example |
|----------------|---------------------|----------|---------|
| Continuous-Time (CT) | Continuous ($t \in \mathbb{R}$) | $x(t)$ | Analog voltage from microphone |
| Discrete-Time (DT) | Discrete ($n \in \mathbb{Z}$) | $x[n]$ | Daily stock closing price |

**Continuous-Time Signal $x(t)$:** Defined for every instant of time. The independent variable $t$ takes all real values within an interval.

**Discrete-Time Signal $x[n]$:** Defined only at discrete instants. The independent variable $n$ takes only integer values.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A discrete-time signal is **NOT** the same as a digital signal. A discrete-time signal is a sequence of numbers defined at integer time indices, while a digital signal is a discrete-time signal whose amplitudes have been quantized to a finite set of values (binary).

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are recording the temperature of a city throughout the day:

- **Continuous-time signal**: Imagine a graph drawn by a sensitive pen on a moving paper roll. The pen continuously traces the temperature curve, registering values at *every* instant — at 1:00:00.000, at 1:00:00.001, at 1:00:00.002, and so on. This produces a smooth, unbroken curve.

- **Discrete-time signal**: Imagine a thermometer that takes readings only at specific moments — say, every hour on the dot. You get values at 1:00, 2:00, 3:00, ... but nothing in between. This produces a sequence of dots, not a continuous line.

- **Digital signal**: Now imagine that same thermometer, but it can only display whole-number temperatures (rounded). Each reading is both discrete in time AND quantized in value. This is the most restricted form of signal used in digital computers.

**Real-world examples:**
- Speech waveform captured by microphone: **CT signal**
- Stock market price recorded daily: **DT signal**
- MP3 audio file: **Digital signal** (discrete in time, quantized in amplitude)
- ECG/heartbeat voltage output: **CT signal**
- Digital thermometer reading every 10 seconds: **DT signal**

### 1.5 Other Classification Criteria (Brief Overview)

Signals can also be classified based on:
- **Deterministic vs Random**: Known exact behavior vs unpredictable
- **Periodic vs Aperiodic**: Repeating pattern vs non-repeating
- **Even vs Odd**: Symmetry properties
- **Energy vs Power**: Finite energy/power characteristics

These classifications form the backbone of Module 1 and will be detailed in subsequent sections.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus:** The primary classifications studied in this module are: (1) Continuous-time vs Discrete-time, (2) Periodic vs Aperiodic, (3) Even vs Odd, and (4) Energy vs Power signals. Mastery of these four classification axes is essential for all subsequent modules (Fourier, LTI systems, Z-transform, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis and KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Classification of Signals

#### A. Continuous-Time (CT) Signals

A signal $x(t)$ is called continuous-time if its independent variable $t$ varies continuously over $\mathbb{R}$. Such signals can be:
- **Analog signals**: Both independent variable and amplitude are continuous
- **Sampled-data signals**: Independent variable is discrete, but amplitude is continuous

**Example:** $x(t) = 5\sin(2\pi t)$, $x(t) = e^{-3t}u(t)$, $x(t) = \cos(\omega_0 t)$

#### B. Discrete-Time (DT) Signals

A signal $x[n]$ is called discrete-time if its independent variable $n$ takes values only from a discrete set, typically $\mathbb{Z} = \{\ldots, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$.

**Example:** $x[n] = (0.8)^n u[n]$, $x[n] = \cos(\pi n / 4)$, $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$

### 2.2 Periodic and Aperiodic Signals

#### Continuous-Time Periodic Signal

A continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is **periodic** if there exists a positive constant $T_0$ such that:

$$x(t + T_0) = x(t) \quad \forall t \in \mathbb{R}$$

The smallest positive value of $T_0$ satisfying this condition is called the **fundamental period** $T_0$, and the signal is said to be periodic with period $T_0$.

If no such $T_0$ exists, the signal is **aperiodic** (or non-periodic).

#### Discrete-Time Periodic Signal

A discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is **periodic** if there exists a positive integer $N$ such that:

$$x[n + N] = x[n] \quad \forall n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

The smallest positive integer $N$ is the **fundamental period**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why discrete-time periodicity is more restrictive:** In CT, $T_0$ can be any positive real number. In DT, $N$ must be an integer. Therefore, $\cos(\pi n/3)$ is periodic with $N=6$, but $\cos(n)$ (where the argument is $n$ in radians) is **aperiodic** because $2\pi$ is not a rational multiple of $2\pi$ over integers.

#### Sum of Periodic Signals

If $x_1(t)$ has period $T_1$ and $x_2(t)$ has period $T_2$, then $x(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t)$ is periodic only if $T_1/T_2$ is a **rational number**, i.e., $T_1/T_2 = p/q$ for integers $p, q$. The fundamental period is the LCM:

$$T_0 = \text{LCM}(T_1, T_2)$$

### 2.3 Even and Odd Signals

A signal $x(t)$ is classified as:

- **Even signal** if: $x(-t) = x(t)$ for all $t$ (symmetric about the vertical axis)
- **Odd signal** if: $x(-t) = -x(t)$ for all $t$ (anti-symmetric about the origin)

**Examples:**
- $\cos(\omega_0 t)$ — **Even**
- $\sin(\omega_0 t)$ — **Odd**
- $t^2, t^4, |t|$ — **Even functions**
- $t, t^3, \text{sgn}(t)$ — **Odd functions**

> [!NOTE]
> Any arbitrary signal $x(t)$ can be uniquely decomposed into its even and odd parts:
> - **Even part:** $x_e(t) = \dfrac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$
> - **Odd part:** $x_o(t) = \dfrac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$
> Such that $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$.

### 2.4 Energy and Power Signals

#### Energy of Continuous-Time Signal

The total energy of a CT signal $x(t)$ is defined as:

$$E = \lim_{T \to \infty} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

The energy must satisfy $0 < E < \infty$ for the signal to be classified as an **energy signal**.

#### Power of Continuous-Time Signal

The average power of a CT signal $x(t)$ is defined as:

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

The power must satisfy $0 < P < \infty$ for the signal to be classified as a **power signal**.

#### Energy of Discrete-Time Signal

$$E = \lim_{N \to \infty} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^2$$

#### Power of Discrete-Time Signal

$$P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^2$$

### 2.5 Mutual Exclusivity of Energy and Power

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A signal CANNOT be both an energy signal AND a power signal simultaneously. However, there exist signals that are NEITHER (e.g., $x(t) = t$, which has infinite energy and infinite power).

| Signal Type | Energy $E$ | Power $P$ | Relationship |
|-------------|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| Energy signal | $0 < E < \infty$ | $P = 0$ | Finite energy, zero power |
| Power signal | $E = \infty$ | $0 < P < \infty$ | Infinite energy, finite power |
| Neither | $E = \infty$ | $P = \infty$ | Both diverge |

**Connection:** For power signals, $E = \infty$ but $P$ is finite. For energy signals, $P = 0$ (since energy is averaged over infinite duration).

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Continuous-Time Formula | Discrete-Time Formula |
|---------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Signal notation | $x(t)$ | $x[n]$ |
| Periodicity | $x(t+T_0) = x(t)$ | $x[n+N] = x[n]$ |
| Even signal | $x(-t) = x(t)$ | $x[-n] = x[n]$ |
| Odd signal | $x(-t) = -x(t)$ | $x[-n] = -x[n]$ |
| Even part | $x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$ | $x_e[n] = \frac{x[n] + x[-n]}{2}$ |
| Odd part | $x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$ | $x_o[n] = \frac{x[n] - x[-n]}{2}$ |
| Total energy | $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$ | $E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ |
| Average power | $P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$ | $P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ |
| Sum of periodic signals | Periodic if $T_1/T_2$ rational | Periodic if $N_1, N_2$ integers, LCM exists |
| Signal sum | $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$ | $x[n] = x_e[n] + x_o[n]$ |
| Unit | Joules (energy) | Watts (power) |
| Squared magnitude | $\vert x(t) \vert^2 = x(t) \cdot x^*(t)$ | $\vert x[n] \vert^2 = x[n] \cdot x^*[n]$ |

> [!NOTE]
> For real-valued signals, $x^*(t) = x(t)$ and the magnitude squared becomes simply $x^2(t)$. The asterisk $*$ denotes complex conjugate.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

These classifications are foundational for:

1. **Signal Processing:** Fourier series requires periodic signals; Fourier transform handles aperiodic energy signals.
2. **Communications:** Power calculations determine transmitter efficiency and bit-error rates.
3. **Control Systems:** Stability analysis uses energy/power boundedness.
4. **Image Processing:** Even-symmetric filters (Gaussian) and odd-symmetric filters (derivative) have different frequency responses.
5. **Audio Engineering:** Distinguishing tonal (periodic) from noise (aperiodic) components.
6. **Biomedical Engineering:** ECG signals (periodic) vs EEG signals (aperiodic) require different processing techniques.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Even-Odd Decomposition

**Given:** Any arbitrary signal $x(t)$ can be expressed as a sum of its even part $x_e(t)$ and odd part $x_o(t)$.

**Claim:** $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$, where $x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$ and $x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of even and odd parts:**

$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}, \quad x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Add the two parts:**

$$x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} + \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Combine the fractions over common denominator:**

$$x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t) + x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $x(-t)$ terms (one positive, one negative):**

$$x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \frac{2x(t)}{2} = x(t)$$

Therefore, $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$. $\blacksquare$

**Step 5 — Verify that $x_e(t)$ is even:**

Compute $x_e(-t)$ by replacing $t$ with $-t$ in the even part formula:

$$x_e(-t) = \frac{x(-t) + x(-(-t))}{2} = \frac{x(-t) + x(t)}{2} = x_e(t)$$

So $x_e(-t) = x_e(t)$, confirming $x_e(t)$ is even. $\blacksquare$

**Step 6 — Verify that $x_o(t)$ is odd:**

Compute $x_o(-t)$:

$$x_o(-t) = \frac{x(-t) - x(-(-t))}{2} = \frac{x(-t) - x(t)}{2} = -\frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} = -x_o(t)$$

So $x_o(-t) = -x_o(t)$, confirming $x_o(t)$ is odd. $\blacksquare$

**Worked Example:** Decompose $x(t) = e^{at} u(t)$ (where $a > 0$ and $u(t)$ is the unit step).

We know $x(-t) = e^{-at} u(-t)$, so:

$$x_e(t) = \frac{e^{at}u(t) + e^{-at}u(-t)}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\cosh(at) \quad \text{(since } \cosh \text{ is even)}$$

$$x_o(t) = \frac{e^{at}u(t) - e^{-at}u(-t)}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\sinh(at) \quad \text{(since } \sinh \text{ is odd)}$$

Verification: $x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \frac{1}{2}[\cosh(at) + \sinh(at)] = \frac{1}{2}e^{at} = e^{at}u(t) = x(t)$ for $t \geq 0$. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy and Power of $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t)$

**Step 1 — Compute the energy $E$:**

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert A\cos(\omega_0 t) \vert^2 dt = A^2 \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \cos^2(\omega_0 t) dt$$

**Step 2 — Apply the trigonometric identity $\cos^2(\theta) = \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:**

$$E = A^2 \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega_0 t)}{2} dt = \frac{A^2}{2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} 1 \, dt + \frac{A^2}{2}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \cos(2\omega_0 t) dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each integral separately:**

- The first integral $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} 1 \, dt$ diverges to $\infty$.
- The second integral $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \cos(2\omega_0 t) dt$ oscillates but has zero average contribution over infinite range.

Both terms diverge, so $E = \infty$.

**Step 4 — Compute the power $P$:**

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert A\cos(\omega_0 t) \vert^2 dt = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{A^2}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \cos^2(\omega_0 t) dt$$

**Step 5 — Apply the identity and integrate:**

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{A^2}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega_0 t)}{2} dt = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{A^2}{4T} \left[ t + \frac{\sin(2\omega_0 t)}{2\omega_0} \right]_{-T}^{T}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the definite integral:**

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{A^2}{4T} \left[ 2T + \frac{\sin(2\omega_0 T) - \sin(-2\omega_0 T)}{2\omega_0} \right]$$

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{A^2}{4T} \left[ 2T + \frac{2\sin(2\omega_0 T)}{2\omega_0} \right] = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{A^2}{4T} \left[ 2T + \frac{\sin(2\omega_0 T)}{\omega_0} \right]$$

**Step 7 — Take the limit. The first term gives $A^2/2$. The second term vanishes:**

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{A^2 \cdot 2T}{4T} + \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{A^2 \sin(2\omega_0 T)}{4T\omega_0} = \frac{A^2}{2} + 0 = \frac{A^2}{2}$$

**Conclusion:** $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t)$ has $E = \infty$ and $P = A^2/2$. Therefore, it is a **power signal** with average power $A^2/2$ Watts.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Periodicity of $x[n] = \cos(\Omega_0 n)$

**Step 1 — Apply the periodicity condition $x[n+N] = x[n]$:**

$$\cos(\Omega_0(n+N)) = \cos(\Omega_0 n + \Omega_0 N) = \cos(\Omega_0 n)$$

**Step 2 — For this equality to hold for all $n$, we need:**

$$\Omega_0 N = 2\pi k \quad \text{for some integer } k \in \mathbb{Z}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $N$:**

$$N = \frac{2\pi k}{\Omega_0}$$

**Step 4 — Since $N$ must be a positive integer, $\frac{2\pi k}{\Omega_0}$ must be an integer.**

- If $\Omega_0 = \pi/3$, then $N = 2\pi k / (\pi/3) = 6k$. The smallest positive integer is $N = 6$ (when $k=1$). Periodic.
- If $\Omega_0 = 1$, then $N = 2\pi k / 1 = 2\pi k$. This is an integer only if $k = 0$ (trivial). **Aperiodic.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight:** A discrete-time sinusoid $\cos(\Omega_0 n)$ is periodic if and only if $\Omega_0 / (2\pi)$ is a **rational number** $p/q$ (in lowest terms). The fundamental period is then $N = 2\pi q / \Omega_0 = q/p \cdot 2\pi \cdot p/(2\pi) = q$... actually more cleanly, if $\Omega_0 = 2\pi(p/q)$, then $N = q$.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Energy of a Rectangular Pulse

**Given:** $x(t) = A$ for $0 \leq t \leq T_0$ and $0$ elsewhere.

**Step 1 — Set up the energy integral over the non-zero region:**

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \int_{0}^{T_0} A^2 dt$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$E = A^2 [t]_{0}^{T_0} = A^2 T_0$$

**Step 3 — Compute the power:**

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{0}^{T_0} A^2 dt = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{A^2 T_0}{2T} = 0$$

**Conclusion:** A rectangular pulse is an **energy signal** with $E = A^2 T_0$ Joules and $P = 0$ Watts.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Common Signals and Their Properties

| Signal | $x(t)$ or $x[n]$ | Type | Periodic? | Energy | Power |
|--------|-------------------|------|-----------|--------|-------|
| DC signal | $x(t) = C$ | CT | Yes (any $T$) | $\infty$ | $C^2$ |
| Sinusoid | $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t)$ | CT | Yes ($T = 2\pi/\omega_0$) | $\infty$ | $A^2/2$ |
| Rectangular pulse | $x(t) = A$ for $0 \leq t \leq T_0$ | CT | No | $A^2 T_0$ | $0$ |
| Exponential decay | $x(t) = e^{-at}u(t), a>0$ | CT | No | $1/(2a)$ | $0$ |
| Unit impulse | $x(t) = \delta(t)$ | CT | No | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| Ramp signal | $x(t) = tu(t)$ | CT | No | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| Sinusoidal DT | $x[n] = \cos(0.5\pi n)$ | DT | Yes ($N=4$) | $\infty$ | $1/2$ |
| Exponential DT | $x[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$ | DT | No | $4/3$ | $0$ |
| Unit step | $x[n] = u[n]$ | DT | No | $\infty$ | $1/2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit step signal definition:** $u(t) = 1$ for $t \geq 0$ and $u(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$. The unit step $u(t)$ is **aperiodic** with $E = \infty$ and $P = 1/2$ (a power signal).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams and Schematics

### 4.1 Top-Level Signal Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Signal x of t] --> A1{Independent Variable}
    A1 -- Continuous Real --> A2[Continuous Time Signal x of t]
    A1 -- Discrete Integer --> A3[Discrete Time Signal x of n]
    A2 --> A4[Examples Analog audio Sinusoid Temperature]
    A3 --> A5[Examples Digital audio Stock price Daily rainfall]
    A2 --> B1{Periodicity Check}
    A3 --> B1
    B1 -- T subscript 0 exists --> B2[Periodic Signal]
    B1 -- No period --> B3[Aperiodic Signal]
    B2 --> C1{Symmetry Check}
    B3 --> C1
    C1 -- x of negative t equals x of t --> C2[Even Signal]
    C1 -- x of negative t equals negative x of t --> C3[Odd Signal]
    C1 -- Neither --> C4[Neither Even Nor Odd]
    C2 --> D1{Energy Power Check}
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    D1 -- E finite P zero --> D2[Energy Signal]
    D1 -- E infinite P finite --> D3[Power Signal]
    D1 -- Both infinite --> D4[Neither Energy nor Power]
```

### 4.2 Even-Odd Decomposition Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    orig[Original Signal x of t] --> split{Decomposition Operator}
    split --> evenPart[Even Part x subscript e of t]
    split --> oddPart[Odd Part x subscript o of t]
    evenPart --> sum[Adder]
    oddPart --> sum
    sum --> recover[Reconstructed x of t equals x subscript e plus x subscript o]
    evenPart --> checkE{x of negative t equals x of t}
    checkE -- YES --> confE[Confirmed Even]
    checkE -- NO --> errE[Error in Decomposition]
    oddPart --> checkO{x of negative t equals negative x of t}
    checkO -- YES --> confO[Confirmed Odd]
    checkO -- NO --> errO[Error in Decomposition]
```

### 4.3 Energy vs Power Signal Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Compute E and P] --> calcE[Step 1 Integrate magnitude squared over all time]
    calcE --> checkE{Is E finite and greater than zero}
    checkE -- YES --> energySig[Energy Signal: 0 less than E less than infinity, P equals 0]
    checkE -- NO --> checkP{Is P finite and greater than zero}
    checkP -- YES --> powerSig[Power Signal: 0 less than P less than infinity, E equals infinity]
    checkP -- NO --> neither[Neither: Both E and P are infinite]
    energySig --> example1[Example Rectangular pulse]
    powerSig --> example2[Example DC level Sinusoid]
    neither --> example3[Example Ramp signal t u of t]
```

### 4.4 Periodic-Aperiodic Spectrum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PER[Periodic Signals]
        P1[Sinusoid: cos omega t]
        P2[Complex Exponential: e to the j omega t]
        P3[Square wave periodic]
    end
    subgraph APER[Aperiodic Signals]
        A1[Rectangular pulse]
        A2[Exponential decay]
        A3[Unit step function]
        A4[Ramp function]
    end
    PER --> TRANS1[Time domain: T subscript 0 fundamental period]
    APER --> TRANS2[Time domain: No repetition pattern]
    TRANS1 --> FTRANS[Frequency Domain Analysis]
    TRANS2 --> FTRANS
    FTRANS --> FS[Fourier Series: discrete spectrum]
    FTRANS --> FT[Fourier Transform: continuous spectrum]
```

### 4.5 Continuous-Time to Discrete-Time Conversion Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CT[Continuous Time x of t] --> SAMP[Sampling at T subscript s intervals]
    SAMP --> HOLD[Sample and Hold]
    HOLD --> QUANT[Quantization finite levels]
    QUANT --> CODING[Binary Encoding]
    CODING --> DIG[Digital Signal]
    CT --> DCT[Direct CT Processing]
    DIG --> PROC[Digital Signal Processing]
    PROC --> OUT[Output y of n]
    DCT --> OUTCT[Output y of t]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** All mermaid diagrams above use the standard flowchart syntax. Each node uses pure alphanumeric identifiers (such as `startA`, `energySig`, `evenPart`) prefixed with letters, with NO markdown bold/italic formatting inside the quoted labels. This ensures clean rendering across all markdown viewers. The diagrams collectively map the four major classification axes (CT/DT, periodic/aperiodic, even/odd, energy/power) and their interdependencies.
