## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define the following: (i) Continuous-time signal, (ii) Discrete-time signal. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Continuous-Time Signal:** A signal $x(t)$ is called a continuous-time signal if the independent variable $t$ takes all real values in a given interval (often $(-\infty, \infty)$). The signal is defined for every instant of time.

**Example:** $x(t) = 5\sin(2\pi t + \pi/4)$, voltage across a capacitor as a function of time.

**(ii) Discrete-Time Signal:** A signal $x[n]$ is called a discrete-time signal if the independent variable $n$ takes values only from a discrete set, typically the integers $\mathbb{Z} = \{\ldots, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$.

**Example:** Daily average temperature readings $x[n] = T(n)$ where $n$ denotes the day number.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Distinguish between energy signal and power signal with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Energy Signal | Power Signal |
|----------|--------------|--------------|
| Total energy $E$ | Finite ($0 < E < \infty$) | Infinite ($E = \infty$) |
| Average power $P$ | Zero ($P = 0$) | Finite and positive ($0 < P < \infty$) |
| Physical meaning | Decays to zero | Sustained over time |
| Example | Rectangular pulse $x(t) = A$ for $0 \leq t \leq T_0$ | Sinusoid $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t)$ |
| Example energy | $E = A^2 T_0$ | $E = \infty$ |
| Example power | $P = 0$ | $P = A^2/2$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often claim a signal is *both* an energy and power signal, or confuse the formulas for CT and DT cases. Remember: $P_{CT} = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$ but $P_{DT} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^2$. The factor is $1/(2T)$ for CT (two-sided) and $1/(2N+1)$ for DT.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks - Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Check whether the following signals are periodic. If periodic, find the fundamental period.
- (i) $x(t) = 2\cos(3\pi t) + 3\sin(5\pi t)$
- (ii) $x[n] = \cos(0.3\pi n) - \sin(0.5\pi n)$

**(b) [7 Marks]** Determine the even and odd parts of the signal $x(t) = e^{-2t}\cos(3t) \cdot u(t)$. Also verify that $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Part (i):** $x(t) = 2\cos(3\pi t) + 3\sin(5\pi t)$

For $\cos(3\pi t)$: angular frequency $\omega_1 = 3\pi$ rad/s, so period $T_1 = \frac{2\pi}{3\pi} = \frac{2}{3}$ s.

For $\sin(5\pi t)$: angular frequency $\omega_2 = 5\pi$ rad/s, so period $T_2 = \frac{2\pi}{5\pi} = \frac{2}{5}$ s.

Check the ratio: $\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{2/3}{2/5} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{5}{2} = \frac{5}{3}$, which is a rational number.

Therefore, $x(t)$ is periodic. The fundamental period is the LCM of $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$T_0 = \text{LCM}\left(\frac{2}{3}, \frac{2}{5}\right) = \text{LCM of numerators} / \text{GCD of denominators} = \text{LCM}(2,2)/\text{GCD}(3,5) = 2/1 = 2 \text{ s}$$

Verification: At $t + 2$: $x(t+2) = 2\cos(3\pi(t+2)) + 3\sin(5\pi(t+2)) = 2\cos(3\pi t + 6\pi) + 3\sin(5\pi t + 10\pi) = 2\cos(3\pi t) + 3\sin(5\pi t) = x(t)$ ✓

**Part (ii):** $x[n] = \cos(0.3\pi n) - \sin(0.5\pi n)$

For $\cos(0.3\pi n)$: $\Omega_1 = 0.3\pi = \frac{3\pi}{10}$. Periodicity requires $\frac{\Omega_1}{2\pi} = \frac{3\pi/10}{2\pi} = \frac{3}{20}$ rational → $N_1 = 20$.

For $\sin(0.5\pi n)$: $\Omega_2 = 0.5\pi = \frac{\pi}{2}$. Periodicity requires $\frac{\Omega_2}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{4}$ rational → $N_2 = 4$.

Since both $N_1 = 20$ and $N_2 = 4$ are integers, both components are periodic. The fundamental period of the sum is:

$$N_0 = \text{LCM}(20, 4) = 20$$

Therefore, $x[n]$ is periodic with fundamental period $N_0 = 20$ samples.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given $x(t) = e^{-2t}\cos(3t) \cdot u(t)$.

First, determine $x(-t)$:

$$x(-t) = e^{-2(-t)}\cos(-3t) \cdot u(-t) = e^{2t}\cos(3t) \cdot u(-t)$$

(since $\cos$ is an even function, $\cos(-3t) = \cos(3t)$)

**Even part:**

$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\left[e^{-2t}\cos(3t)u(t) + e^{2t}\cos(3t)u(-t)\right]$$

$$x_e(t) = \frac{1}{2}\cos(3t)\left[e^{-2t}u(t) + e^{2t}u(-t)\right]$$

**Odd part:**

$$x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\left[e^{-2t}\cos(3t)u(t) - e^{2t}\cos(3t)u(-t)\right]$$

$$x_o(t) = \frac{1}{2}\cos(3t)\left[e^{-2t}u(t) - e^{2t}u(-t)\right]$$

**Verification that $x_e(t)$ is even:**

$$x_e(-t) = \frac{1}{2}\cos(-3t)\left[e^{2t}u(-t) + e^{-2t}u(t)\right] = \frac{1}{2}\cos(3t)\left[e^{-2t}u(t) + e^{2t}u(-t)\right] = x_e(t) \checkmark$$

**Verification that $x_o(t)$ is odd:**

$$x_o(-t) = \frac{1}{2}\cos(-3t)\left[e^{2t}u(-t) - e^{-2t}u(t)\right] = -\frac{1}{2}\cos(3t)\left[e^{-2t}u(t) - e^{2t}u(-t)\right] = -x_o(t) \checkmark$$

**Verification of decomposition:** $x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \frac{1}{2}\cos(3t)[e^{-2t}u(t) + e^{2t}u(-t) + e^{-2t}u(t) - e^{2t}u(-t)] = \frac{1}{2}\cos(3t) \cdot 2e^{-2t}u(t) = e^{-2t}\cos(3t)u(t) = x(t)$ ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** For the signal $x(t)$ shown in the figure, determine:
- (i) Whether it is an energy signal or power signal. Compute its energy and power.
- (ii) Whether it is even, odd, or neither.

Signal definition:

$$x(t) = \begin{cases} 2 & \text{for } -1 \leq t \leq 1 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the discrete-time signal $x[n] = 4\cos(0.5\pi n) + 3\sin(0.25\pi n)$:
- (i) Find the fundamental period.
- (ii) Calculate the average power.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Part (i) — Energy and Power:**

The signal is a rectangular pulse of amplitude $2$ from $t = -1$ to $t = 1$.

**Energy calculation:**

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \int_{-1}^{1} (2)^2 dt = 4 \int_{-1}^{1} dt = 4 \cdot [t]_{-1}^{1} = 4 \cdot 2 = 8 \text{ Joules}$$

**Power calculation:**

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-1}^{1} 4 \, dt = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{8}{2T} = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{4}{T} = 0 \text{ Watts}$$

Since $E = 8$ (finite and positive) and $P = 0$, the signal is an **energy signal**.

**Part (ii) — Symmetry:**

Check if $x(t)$ is even: $x(-t) = 2$ for $-1 \leq -t \leq 1$, i.e., $-1 \leq t \leq 1$. So $x(-t) = 2 = x(t)$.

Therefore, the signal is **even**. (Note: a symmetric rectangular pulse centered at origin is always even.)

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Part (i) — Fundamental period:**

For $x_1[n] = 4\cos(0.5\pi n) = 4\cos\left(\frac{\pi n}{2}\right)$:

$\Omega_1 = 0.5\pi = \frac{\pi}{2}$. The ratio $\frac{\Omega_1}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{4}$, which is rational.

So $N_1 = 4$ (fundamental period).

For $x_2[n] = 3\sin(0.25\pi n) = 3\sin\left(\frac{\pi n}{4}\right)$:

$\Omega_2 = 0.25\pi = \frac{\pi}{4}$. The ratio $\frac{\Omega_2}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{8}$, which is rational.

So $N_2 = 8$ (fundamental period).

**Fundamental period of the sum:**

$$N_0 = \text{LCM}(N_1, N_2) = \text{LCM}(4, 8) = 8 \text{ samples}$$

**Part (ii) — Average power:**

For periodic discrete-time signals, the average power over one period equals the average power over all time:

$$P = \frac{1}{N_0} \sum_{n=0}^{N_0-1} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{8} \sum_{n=0}^{7} \vert 4\cos(0.5\pi n) + 3\sin(0.25\pi n) \vert^2$$

Expand using $\vert a + b \vert^2 = a^2 + b^2 + 2ab$:

$$P = \frac{1}{8} \sum_{n=0}^{7} \left[ 16\cos^2(0.5\pi n) + 9\sin^2(0.25\pi n) + 24\cos(0.5\pi n)\sin(0.25\pi n) \right]$$

Evaluate each term separately using the standard technique for periodic signals (over a full period, the average of products of harmonics with different frequencies is zero, and the average of $\cos^2$ or $\sin^2$ is $1/2$):

- $\frac{1}{8} \sum_{n=0}^{7} 16\cos^2(0.5\pi n) = 16 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 8$ W
- $\frac{1}{8} \sum_{n=0}^{7} 9\sin^2(0.25\pi n) = 9 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 4.5$ W
- $\frac{1}{8} \sum_{n=0}^{7} 24\cos(0.5\pi n)\sin(0.25\pi n) = 0$ (orthogonal over a full period)

$$P = 8 + 4.5 + 0 = 12.5 \text{ Watts}$$

Alternative: For sinusoidal signals with different frequencies that are integer multiples of the fundamental, the **total power equals the sum of individual powers**:

- Power of $4\cos(0.5\pi n)$ = $\frac{4^2}{2} = 8$ W
- Power of $3\sin(0.25\pi n)$ = $\frac{3^2}{2} = 4.5$ W
- Cross term = $0$ (orthogonality)
- Total $P = 8 + 4.5 = 12.5$ W ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Loss of Marks):**
> 1. **Missing the factor of $1/2$ in average power for sinusoids:** A common mistake is computing $P = A^2$ for $A\cos(\omega t)$ instead of $P = A^2/2$. Always use the formula and remember the average of $\cos^2$ over a full period is $1/2$.
> 2. **Confusing CT and DT power formulas:** For CT: $P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$. For DT: $P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^2$. The CT uses $1/(2T)$ (two-sided) while DT uses $1/(2N+1)$ (counting the central sample).
> 3. **Forgetting to check LCM for period of sum of two periodic signals:** If two components have periods $N_1$ and $N_2$, the period of their sum is LCM, not sum or product.
> 4. **Improperly using $\cos^2 = \cos(2\theta)$ without the $1/2$ factor:** Many students write $\cos^2(\theta) = \cos(2\theta)$, which is wrong. Correct identity: $\cos^2(\theta) = \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$.
> 5. **Not verifying final answer:** Always check if $x_e(t)$ is truly even and $x_o(t)$ is truly odd by substituting $t \to -t$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 - Signal Classifications:**

- **Signal:** A function of one or more independent variables (usually time) carrying information.
- **CT Signal $x(t)$:** Independent variable is continuous. Defined for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$.
- **DT Signal $x[n]$:** Independent variable is discrete. Defined only for $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.
- **Periodic CT:** $x(t + T_0) = x(t)$, smallest $T_0 > 0$ is fundamental period.
- **Periodic DT:** $x[n + N] = x[n]$, smallest integer $N > 0$ is fundamental period.
- **DT Periodicity condition:** $\cos(\Omega_0 n)$ periodic iff $\Omega_0/(2\pi)$ is rational.
- **Sum of periodic signals:** Periodic if ratio of periods is rational (CT) or if both $N$ values are integers (DT). Fundamental period = LCM.
- **Even signal:** $x(-t) = x(t)$ (CT) or $x[-n] = x[n]$ (DT).
- **Odd signal:** $x(-t) = -x(t)$ (CT) or $x[-n] = -x[n]$ (DT).
- **Even-Odd decomposition:** $x_e(t) = [x(t) + x(-t)]/2$, $x_o(t) = [x(t) - x(-t)]/2$, and $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$.
- **Energy signal:** $0 < E < \infty$, $P = 0$.
- **Power signal:** $E = \infty$, $0 < P < \infty$.
- **Energy formulas:** $E_{CT} = \int |x(t)|^2 dt$, $E_{DT} = \sum |x[n]|^2$.
- **Power formulas:** $P_{CT} = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} |x(t)|^2 dt$, $P_{DT} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} |x[n]|^2$.
- **Power of sinusoid:** $P(A\cos\omega_0 t) = A^2/2$, $P(A\cos\Omega_0 n) = A^2/2$.
- **Energy of rectangular pulse** (height $A$, width $T_0$): $E = A^2 T_0$.
- **Power of DC signal** (constant $C$): $P = C^2$.
- **Mutual exclusivity:** A signal cannot be both energy AND power signal.
- **Trigonometric identity (most tested):** $\cos^2(\theta) = \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$, $\sin^2(\theta) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$.
- **Orthogonality of sinusoids:** $\int_0^T \cos(n\omega_0 t)\cos(m\omega_0 t) dt = 0$ for $n \neq m$ over full period.
- **Magnitude squared for complex signals:** $|x(t)|^2 = x(t) \cdot x^*(t)$.
- **Neither energy nor power:** $x(t) = t$, $x(t) = tu(t)$ — both $E$ and $P$ diverge.

**Quick Mnemonics:**
- **E-P-E-P**: **E**ven signals have **E**qual halves (mirror image). **O**dd signals have **O**pposite halves.
- **Power = $A^2/2$**: For any sinusoid, the average power is always half the squared amplitude.
- **LCM for sum, GCD for common divisor**: When combining periodic signals, use LCM to find the new period.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Operations on Signals (Scaling, Shifting, Folding)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT403 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Operations on Signals (Scaling, Shifting, Folding)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Basic Signal Operations?

In **Signals and Systems (PCICT403)**, a **signal** $x(t)$ or $x[n]$ is a function that carries information. To analyse, manipulate, and process these signals (e.g., in filters, communication channels, or audio editors), engineers perform three **fundamental time-domain transformations**:

1. **Time Shifting** — moving the signal along the time axis.
2. **Time Scaling** — stretching or compressing the signal along the time axis.
3. **Time Folding (Reflection)** — mirroring the signal about the vertical (amplitude) axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 1):** Students must be able to graphically represent and mathematically express the **independent variable transformations** applied to **both continuous-time (CT)** and **discrete-time (DT)** signals, including combinations such as *time reversal and shifting*.

> [!NOTE]
> These operations act on the **independent variable** (time $t$ or sample index $n$). The **shape and amplitude** of the signal remain untouched; only its position or spread along the horizontal axis is altered.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Standard Terminology)

| Operation | Continuous-Time | Discrete-Time | Domain |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|--------|
| Time Shift | $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$ | $y[n] = x[n - n_0]$ | $t, t_0 \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| Time Scale | $y(t) = x(at)$ | $y[n] = x[kn]$ | $a, k \in \mathbb{R}, a \neq 0$ |
| Time Fold | $y(t) = x(-t)$ | $y[n] = x[-n]$ | Reflection about $t=0$ |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogies — Intuition for First-Time Learners

> [!TIP]
> **The "Audio Tape" Analogy**
> Imagine a magnetic tape recording of your voice:
> - **Shifting** = sliding the play-head left or right (delay/echo effect).
> - **Scaling (Stretch)** = playing the tape at **half speed** (slow, deep voice).
> - **Scaling (Compress)** = playing the tape at **double speed** (chipmunk voice).
> - **Folding** = rewinding the tape and playing it **backwards**.

### (a) Time Shifting
$$
y(t) = x(t - t_0)
$$

- If $t_0 > 0$ → the signal $x(t)$ is **delayed** (shifted to the **right**).
- If $t_0 < 0$ → the signal $x(t)$ is **advanced** (shifted to the **left**).

### (b) Time Scaling
$$
y(t) = x(at)
$$

- If $\vert a \vert > 1$ → signal is **compressed** horizontally (faster playback).
- If $0 < \vert a \vert < 1$ → signal is **expanded/stretched** horizontally (slower playback).

### (c) Time Folding (Reflection)
$$
y(t) = x(-t)
$$

- The signal is **mirrored** about the vertical axis ($t=0$).
- For DT signals, $y[n] = x[-n]$ flips the sequence about $n=0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Folding vs. Negation:** *Folding* reverses time; *Amplitude inversion* negates $y$-values. **They are different operations** — a frequent KTU exam pitfall.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Time Shifting — Mechanism

A signal $x(t)$ shifted by $t_0$ along the time axis yields:
$$
y(t) = x(t - t_0)
$$

- The **value** of $x$ at $t = t_0$ now appears at $t = 2t_0$ in $y(t)$? No — the **shape** of $x$ is translated.
- Mathematically, $x(\tau) = A$ at $\tau = t_1$ corresponds to $y(t) = A$ at $t = t_1 + t_0$.

**Why this works:** The substitution $\tau = t - t_0$ preserves every relative interval between samples; only the origin of reference is translated.

> [!EXAMPLE]
> If $x(t) = \sin(t)$ and $t_0 = \pi/2$, then $y(t) = \sin(t - \pi/2) = -\cos(t)$. The sine wave slides rightward by $\pi/2$ rad, becoming a cosine waveform (with sign inversion, due to the trig identity, not due to the operation itself).

---

## 2.2 Time Scaling — Mechanism

Replacing $t$ with $at$ in $x(t)$ produces:
$$
y(t) = x(at)
$$

- **Case 1:** $\vert a \vert > 1$ → **Compression**. All features occur at $1/a$ times their original duration.
- **Case 2:** $0 < \vert a \vert < 1$ → **Expansion**. All features occur $1/a$ times slower.
- **Case 3:** $a < 0$ → **Reflection + Scaling** combined.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does compression happen when $a>1$?** Because the point originally at $t = t_1$ in $x$ now occurs at $t = t_1/a$ in $y$, which is *closer* to the origin.

---

## 2.3 Time Folding (Reflection) — Mechanism

Substituting $-t$ for $t$ reverses the signal:
$$
y(t) = x(-t)
$$

- The sample at $t = t_1$ in $x$ now appears at $t = -t_1$ in $y$.
- If $x(t)$ is **even** ($x(-t) = x(t)$), folding leaves it unchanged.
- If $x(t)$ is **odd** ($x(-t) = -x(t)$), folding inverts its sign.

---

## 2.4 Combined Operations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 frequently tests **combined transformations**, especially *folding followed by shifting* (or vice versa). The order matters in some cases.

**Standard form encountered in KTU papers:**
$$
y(t) = x(-t \pm t_0) \quad \text{or} \quad y(t) = x(at - t_0)
$$

**Two fundamental precedence rules:**
- For $x(at + t_0)$: define $u = at + t_0$. The **zero** of $u$ (the reference point) is at $t = -t_0/a$.
- For $x(a(t - t_0))$: the **scale** applies *after* the shift; the reference point is at $t = t_0$.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Continuous-Time $y(t)$ | Discrete-Time $y[n]$ | Effect |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------------|--------|
| Right shift (delay) | $x(t - t_0),\ t_0 > 0$ | $x[n - n_0],\ n_0 > 0$ | Signal moves right |
| Left shift (advance) | $x(t + t_0),\ t_0 > 0$ | $x[n + n_0],\ n_0 > 0$ | Signal moves left |
| Compression | $x(at),\ \vert a \vert > 1$ | $x[kn],\ \vert k \vert > 1,\ k \in \mathbb{Z}$ | Signal squeezed |
| Expansion | $x(at),\ 0 < \vert a \vert < 1$ | $x[kn],\ 0 < \vert k \vert < 1$ | Signal stretched |
| Reflection (fold) | $x(-t)$ | $x[-n]$ | Mirror about $t=0$ or $n=0$ |
| Combined (fold + shift) | $x(-t - t_0)$ | $x[-n - n_0]$ | Fold, then shift |
| Combined (shift + scale) | $x(a(t - t_0))$ | $x[k(n - n_0)]$ | Shift, then scale |

> [!TIP]
> In discrete time, **time scaling** is mathematically restricted — you can only **down-sample** by integer factors; you cannot produce fractional samples.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Time Shift** → Used in radar and sonar for **range estimation** (echo delay).
- **Time Scaling** → Crucial in **audio codecs** (MP3, AAC) for pitch/time modification.
- **Time Folding** → Foundation of **convolution**, correlation, and **palindrome detection** in DSP.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Pure Time Shift

**Problem:** Given $x(t)$ as a rectangular pulse of amplitude **1** spanning $t \in [0, 2]$. Sketch $y(t) = x(t - 3)$.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify original support:**
The pulse $x(t)$ is non-zero for $0 \leq t \leq 2$. The right edge is at $t = 2$ and the left edge is at $t = 0$.

**Step 2 — Apply the shift transformation:**
The substitution rule states: $x(\tau) = 1$ when $\tau = t - 3 \in [0, 2]$, which means $t - 3 \in [0, 2]$, or equivalently $t \in [3, 5]$.

**Step 3 — Write the analytic expression:**
$$
y(t) = x(t - 3) = \begin{cases} 1, & 3 \leq t \leq 5 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification (by sample point):**
The point $t = 1$ in $x$ (where $x(1) = 1$) maps to $t = 1 + 3 = 4$ in $y$. Indeed, $y(4) = 1$. ✓

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Pure Time Scaling

**Problem:** Let $x(t) = \sin(\pi t)$ for $0 \leq t \leq 2$. Find $y(t) = x(2t)$.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Substitute the argument:**
Replace $t$ with $2t$ inside $x$:
$$
y(t) = \sin(\pi \cdot 2t) = \sin(2\pi t)
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the new support:**
Since $x$ exists for $0 \leq t \leq 2$, and $y$ uses $2t$ in its place, the new support is obtained by solving:
$$
0 \leq 2t \leq 2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 0 \leq t \leq 1
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result:**
The original signal $x(t)$ had **one full period** (from $0$ to $2$, since $\pi t$ completes one cycle over $[0, 2]$). After compression by $a=2$, the new signal $y(t)$ has one full period over $[0, 1]$ — the waveform is **compressed by a factor of 2**.

**Step 4 — Analytic summary:**
$$
y(t) = \sin(2\pi t), \quad t \in [0, 1]
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Time Folding (Reflection)

**Problem:** $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ with arrow at $n=0$ pointing to $1$. Find $y[n] = x[-n]$.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the original sequence indexing:**
$$
x[n]: \quad \begin{array}{c|cccc} n & -1 & 0 & 1 & 2 \\ \hline x[n] & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \end{array}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the reflection rule $n \to -n$:**
For each $n$, the value at $-n$ in $x$ becomes the value at $n$ in $y$.

| Original $n$ | $x[n]$ | New index in $y$ |
|:------------:|:------:|:----------------:|
| $-1$ | $1$ | $+1$ |
| $0$  | $2$ | $0$ |
| $1$  | $3$ | $-1$ |
| $2$  | $4$ | $-2$ |

**Step 3 — Write the resulting sequence:**
$$
y[n] = x[-n] = \{4, 3, 2, 1\}, \quad \text{with arrow at } n=0 \text{ pointing to } 2
$$

**Step 4 — Verification (even/odd test):**
$x$ is neither even nor odd; $y$ is the **mirror image** of $x$ — correct.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Combined Operation (Fold + Shift)

**Problem:** $x(t) = e^{-t} u(t)$ (right-sided decaying exponential). Sketch $y(t) = x(-t-2)$.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Apply folding first:**
Replace $t$ with $-t$ in $x(t)$:
$$
x(-t) = e^{-(-t)} u(-t) = e^{t} u(-t)
$$
This is a **left-sided** rising exponential, decaying as $t \to -\infty$.

**Step 2 — Apply the shift of $t_0 = -2$ (i.e., shift left by 2):**
Replace $t$ with $t + 2$ in $x(-t)$:
$$
y(t) = x(-(t+2) - 2 \cdot 0) \rightarrow \text{Re-evaluate properly}
$$

**Cleaner approach:** Set $x(\tau) = e^{-\tau} u(\tau)$. We want $y(t) = x(-t-2)$. Let $\tau = -t-2$. The condition $\tau \geq 0$ implies:
$$
-t - 2 \geq 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad t \leq -2
$$

**Step 3 — Express the result:**
$$
y(t) = e^{-(-t-2)} u(-t-2) = e^{t+2} u(-t-2)
$$

**Step 4 — Sketch interpretation:**
- $y(t)$ is non-zero for $t \leq -2$ (left side of the origin).
- As $t$ decreases from $-2$ to $-\infty$, $y(t)$ grows exponentially (since $e^{t+2} \to 0$ as $t \to -\infty$? Wait — let's recompute).

**Recompute Step 4:** When $t = -2$, $y(-2) = e^{0} = 1$. When $t = -3$, $y(-3) = e^{-1} \approx 0.37$. When $t = -4$, $y(-4) = e^{-2} \approx 0.135$. So as $t$ moves **leftward** from $-2$, $y(t)$ decays toward zero. ✓

$$
y(t) = e^{t+2} u(-t-2)
$$

This is a **left-sided exponential starting at $t=-2$ with peak value 1**, decaying to the left.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Operational Effects Summary

| Original Feature | Effect of $x(t-3)$ | Effect of $x(2t)$ | Effect of $x(-t)$ |
|------------------|--------------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| A sample at $t = t_1$ | Moves to $t = t_1 + 3$ | Moves to $t = t_1 / 2$ | Moves to $t = -t_1$ |
| Pulse width $T$ | Unchanged: $T$ | Compressed: $T/2$ | Unchanged: $T$ |
| Amplitude | Unchanged | Unchanged | Unchanged |
| Support $[0, T]$ | $[3, T+3]$ | $[0, T/2]$ | $[-T, 0]$ |
| Energy $\int \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$ | **Unchanged** | **Unchanged** | **Unchanged** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Transformation Pipeline — Functional Flow

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **functional architecture** of how the three basic operations map an input signal to an output signal.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InputX["Input Signal x of t"] --> OpSelect{Operation Type}
    OpSelect -->|Shift by t0| ShiftModule["Time Shift y = x of t minus t0"]
    OpSelect -->|Scale by a| ScaleModule["Time Scale y = x of a t"]
    OpSelect -->|Fold| FoldModule["Time Fold y = x of minus t"]
    OpSelect -->|Combined| ComboModule["Combined Fold Shift Scale"]

    ShiftModule --> Verify1{"Check Support and Amplitude"}
    ScaleModule --> Verify1
    FoldModule --> Verify1
    ComboModule --> Verify1

    Verify1 --> OutputY["Transformed Signal y of t"]

    style InputX fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style OpSelect fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000
    style ShiftModule fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style ScaleModule fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style FoldModule fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style ComboModule fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    style Verify1 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000
    style OutputY fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Order-of-Operations Decision Tree (Combined Transformations)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["Given y = x of alpha t plus beta"]) --> StepA["Identify alpha and beta values"]
    StepA --> StepB{"Is alpha negative?"}
    StepB -->|Yes| Fold["Apply FOLD first: replace t by minus t"]
    StepB -->|No| ScaleCheck{"Is alpha greater than 1?"}
    Fold --> ScaleCheck
    ScaleCheck -->|Yes| Compress["Apply COMPRESSION by factor alpha"]
    ScaleCheck -->|No| Expand["Apply EXPANSION by factor 1 over alpha"]
    Compress --> ShiftStep["Apply SHIFT by minus beta over alpha"]
    Expand --> ShiftStep
    ShiftStep --> Final["Write y of t with new support and amplitude"]

    style Start fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style StepA fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000
    style StepB fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000
    style Fold fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    style ScaleCheck fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000
    style Compress fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style Expand fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style ShiftStep fill:#e6e6ff,stroke:#333399,color:#000
    style Final fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Effect on Signal Features

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OriginalSignal["ORIGINAL x of t"]
        OS1["Support on t axis: a to b"]
        OS2["Amplitude A"]
        OS3["Width W"]
    end

    subgraph AfterShift["AFTER SHIFT by t0"]
        AS1["Support: a plus t0 to b plus t0"]
        AS2["Amplitude A unchanged"]
        AS3["Width W unchanged"]
    end

    subgraph AfterScale["AFTER SCALE by a"]
        SC1["Support: a over alpha to b over alpha"]
        SC2["Amplitude A unchanged"]
        SC3["Width W over alpha"]
    end

    subgraph AfterFold["AFTER FOLD"]
        AF1["Support: minus b to minus a"]
        AF2["Amplitude A unchanged"]
        AF3["Width W unchanged"]
    end

    OriginalSignal --> AfterShift
    OriginalSignal --> AfterScale
    OriginalSignal --> AfterFold

    style OriginalSignal fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style AfterShift fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style AfterScale fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000
    style AfterFold fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above are **block-level functional flowcharts** representing the **mathematical operations** on the abstract features of signals. They are not physical circuit diagrams. KTU Module 1 questions test the **graphical representation** of these transformations; the flowchart shows the **decision logic** a student must apply during sketching.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define time scaling and time shifting of continuous-time signals. With a suitable example, explain how the signal $x(t)$ is modified to obtain $x(2t-3)$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Time Shifting:** The operation $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$ translates the signal $x(t)$ along the time axis. If $t_0 > 0$, the signal is delayed (right shift); if $t_0 < 0$, the signal is advanced (left shift). Amplitude and shape remain unchanged. **(1 mark)**

**Time Scaling:** The operation $y(t) = x(at)$ stretches or compresses the signal along the time axis. If $\vert a \vert > 1$, the signal is compressed; if $0 < \vert a \vert < 1$, the signal is expanded. **(1 mark)**

**Example — $x(2t-3)$:** This involves scaling by $a=2$ (compression by factor 2) and shifting by $t_0 = 3/2$ to the right. The expression can be rewritten as $x(2(t - 1.5))$, meaning shift right by 1.5 *first*, then compress. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]`
**What is meant by time reversal (folding) of a discrete-time signal? Given $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ with $x[0]=1$, find $y[n] = x[-n]$ and comment on its symmetry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Time Reversal:** Folding or reflection of a signal about the vertical axis is the operation $y[n] = x[-n]$. The sample at index $n$ in $x$ moves to index $-n$ in $y$. **(1 mark)**

**Computation:**

| $n$ in $x$ | $x[n]$ | $n$ in $y = x[-n]$ |
|:----------:|:------:|:------------------:|
| $0$ | $1$ | $0$ |
| $1$ | $2$ | $-1$ |
| $2$ | $3$ | $-2$ |
| $3$ | $4$ | $-3$ |

$$
y[n] = \{4, 3, 2, 1\} \quad \text{with arrow at } n=0 \text{ pointing to } 2
$$
**(1 mark)**

**Symmetry:** Since $x[n] \neq x[-n]$ and $x[n] \neq -x[-n]$, the original signal is **neither even nor odd**. The folded signal is the mirror image of the original. **(1 mark)**

---

## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**For the signal $x(t)$ defined as:**
$$
x(t) = \begin{cases} t + 1, & -1 \leq t \leq 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Plot the following transformed signals with proper labelling of amplitude and time axes:**

**(a)** $y_1(t) = x(t - 2)$ **(7 marks)**

**(b)** $y_2(t) = x(2t + 1)$ and $y_3(t) = x(-t + 1)$ **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the original signal.** (1 mark)

$x(t)$ is a ramp of slope $+1$ over $[-1, 1]$, with $x(-1) = 0$, $x(0) = 1$, $x(1) = 2$.

**Step 2 — Apply the shift transformation.** (2 marks)

For $y_1(t) = x(t - 2)$, set $\tau = t - 2$. The condition $-1 \leq \tau \leq 1$ becomes:
$$
-1 \leq t - 2 \leq 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 1 \leq t \leq 3
$$

**Step 3 — Write the analytic form.** (2 marks)

$$
y_1(t) = \begin{cases} (t - 2) + 1, & 1 \leq t \leq 3 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} = \begin{cases} t - 1, & 1 \leq t \leq 3 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Key sample points.** (1 mark)

- $y_1(1) = 0$
- $y_1(2) = 1$
- $y_1(3) = 2$

**Step 5 — Sketch.** (1 mark) — Ramp from $(1, 0)$ rising linearly to $(3, 2)$; zero elsewhere.

---

#### **Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks):**

**For $y_2(t) = x(2t + 1)$:** (3.5 marks)

**Step 1 — Rewrite as $x(2(t + 0.5))$:** shift left by $0.5$, then compress by factor 2. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Determine support:** (1 mark)
$$
-1 \leq 2t + 1 \leq 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad -1 \leq t \leq 0
$$

**Step 3 — Analytic form:** (1 mark)
$$
y_2(t) = \begin{cases} (2t + 1) + 1, & -1 \leq t \leq 0 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} = \begin{cases} 2t + 2, & -1 \leq t \leq 0 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Sample points:** $y_2(-1) = 0$, $y_2(-0.5) = 1$, $y_2(0) = 2$. (0.5 marks)

---

**For $y_3(t) = x(-t + 1)$:** (3.5 marks)

**Step 1 — Rewrite as $x(-(t - 1))$:** shift right by 1, then fold. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Determine support:** (1 mark)
$$
-1 \leq -t + 1 \leq 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 0 \leq t \leq 2
$$

**Step 3 — Analytic form:** (1 mark)
$$
y_3(t) = \begin{cases} -t + 2, & 0 \leq t \leq 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Sample points:** $y_3(0) = 2$, $y_3(1) = 1$, $y_3(2) = 0$. (0.5 marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]`

**Consider the discrete-time signal:**
$$
x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\} \quad \text{with the arrow at } n = 0 \text{ pointing to } 3
$$

**(a)** Sketch $x[n]$, then determine and sketch $y[n] = x[-n + 2]$. State whether $y[n]$ is even, odd, or neither. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine and sketch $z[n] = x[2n]$, and comment on whether this operation is invertible. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the original sequence.** (1 mark)
$$
x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\} \rightarrow x[-2]=1,\ x[-1]=2,\ x[0]=3,\ x[1]=4,\ x[2]=5
$$

**Step 2 — Apply transformation $y[n] = x[-n+2]$.** (2 marks)

For each $n$, we evaluate $x$ at index $(-n + 2)$.

| $n$ | $-n+2$ | $x[-n+2]$ |
|:---:|:------:|:---------:|
| $0$ | $2$ | $5$ |
| $1$ | $1$ | $4$ |
| $2$ | $0$ | $3$ |
| $3$ | $-1$ | $2$ |
| $4$ | $-2$ | $1$ |

**Step 3 — Resulting sequence.** (2 marks)
$$
y[n] = \{5, 4, 3, 2, 1\} \quad \text{with arrow at } n=0 \text{ pointing to } 5
$$

**Step 4 — Symmetry check.** (1 mark)
$y[0] = 5$, $y[-0] = 5$ (equal). $y[1] = 4$, $y[-1] = 2$ (unequal). $y[2] = 3$, $y[-2] = 1$ (unequal). Hence $y[n]$ is **neither even nor odd**. ✓

**Step 5 — Sketch.** (1 mark) — Stem plot descending from $n=0$ (value 5) to $n=4$ (value 1).

---

#### **Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Understand the operation $z[n] = x[2n]$.** (1 mark)

This is a **down-sampling by factor 2**. We evaluate $x$ at even multiples of $n$.

**Step 2 — Compute samples.** (2 marks)

| $n$ | $2n$ | $x[2n]$ |
|:---:|:----:|:-------:|
| $-1$ | $-2$ | $1$ |
| $0$ | $0$ | $3$ |
| $1$ | $2$ | $5$ |
| $2$ | $4$ | undefined (out of range) |

**Step 3 — Resulting sequence.** (1 mark)
$$
z[n] = \{1, 3, 5\} \quad \text{with arrow at } n=0 \text{ pointing to } 3
$$

**Step 4 — Invertibility discussion.** (2 marks)

Down-sampling by an integer factor $k > 1$ is **NOT invertible** because:
- Information is **lost** — odd-indexed samples of $x[n]$ (i.e., $x[\pm 1] = 2, 4$) are discarded.
- No mathematical operation can reconstruct the missing samples from $\{1, 3, 5\}$ alone.
- This is in contrast to **up-sampling** (zero insertion), which is invertible via low-pass filtering.

**Step 5 — Sketch.** (1 mark) — Stem plot of $z[n]$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Folding vs. Shifting Mix-up:** Students often write $x(-t-2)$ as $x(-(t+2))$ without recognising that **folding order matters** when combined with shifting. Always re-derive the support: $0 \leq -t-2 \Rightarrow t \leq -2$.
> 2. **Forgetting amplitude preservation:** Operations act on the **independent variable** only — the $y$-values are preserved. Drawing a *scaled amplitude* signal is a **1-mark deduction** in graphical questions.
> 3. **Sign errors in combined operations:** In $x(2t+1)$, students sometimes write it as $x(2(t+0.5))$ (correct) but compute the support as $t \in [-0.5, 0]$ (wrong). Correct support: $t \in [-1, 0]$.
> 4. **Discrete-time scaling misconception:** Students assume $x[0.5n]$ is valid. It is **not** — discrete-time scaling only accepts integer scaling factors.
> 5. **Skipping the sketch:** KTU strictly requires the **graphical plot** alongside analytic expressions. A correct formula without a sketch loses **2-3 marks** in 7-mark sub-questions.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key takeaways for KTU 2024 Module 1 — Basic Signal Operations:**

- ⏱ **Time Shift** $x(t - t_0)$: Right shift (delay) if $t_0 > 0$; left shift (advance) if $t_0 < 0$. Amplitude unchanged. **(1 mark item)**
- 📏 **Time Scale** $x(at)$: Compression if $\vert a \vert > 1$; expansion if $0 < \vert a \vert < 1$. Amplitude unchanged. **(1 mark item)**
- 🪞 **Time Fold** $x(-t)$: Mirror about the $y$-axis. Even signals are unchanged; odd signals invert. **(1 mark item)**
- 🔁 **Combined Operations**: Apply folding first if the inner argument has a negative sign on $t$, then shift, then scale. **(2 mark item)**
- 📐 **Support Calculation**: For $x(at + b)$, set $0 \leq at + b \leq$ (range) and solve for $t$. **(2 mark item)**
- 📊 **Graphical Plotting**: Always label axes, mark key points (where signal is non-zero), and show amplitude levels explicitly. **(1 mark item)**
- 🎯 **Discrete-Time Caveat**: Only integer scaling factors are permitted; down-sampling is **non-invertible** (lossy). **(1 mark item)**
- ✅ **Energy Invariance**: Total energy $\int \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$ is **preserved** under all three operations (time-invariant transformations). **(1 mark item)**
- 🧪 **Even/Odd Test**: $x(-t) = x(t)$ → even; $x(-t) = -x(t)$ → odd; otherwise neither. **(1 mark item)**
- 🎓 **KTU Pattern Alert**: 14-mark questions almost always combine **two of three** operations on a continuous ramp/triangular signal and require a **plot + analytic expression + support calculation**. **(2 mark item)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Systems: Definitions and Classification (Linear and Nonlinear, Time-Invariant and Time-Variant, Causal and Non-Causal, Stable and Unstable)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT403 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Systems: Definitions and Classification (Linear and Nonlinear, Time-Invariant and Time-Variant, Causal and Non-Causal, Stable and Unstable)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a System?

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A **system** is a mathematical model or physical device that maps an input signal $x(t)$ (or $x[n]$) to an output signal $y(t)$ (or $y[n]$) according to a well-defined rule or transformation. In continuous-time, this is denoted as $x(t) \xrightarrow{\mathcal{T}} y(t)$, and in discrete-time as $x[n] \xrightarrow{\mathcal{T}} y[n]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:** A system is a device or algorithm that performs a prescribed operation on an input signal to produce an output signal. Mathematically, $y(t) = \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\}$ where $\mathcal{T}$ is the system operator.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a system as a **black box machine in a factory**. You feed raw material (input signal) into one end, and the machine processes it using its internal rules (system properties) to deliver a finished product (output signal). Whether the machine is linear, time-invariant, causal, or stable depends on *how* it transforms the input. Just like a coffee machine always produces coffee regardless of when you use it (time-invariant), but a delayed-stock-tip machine only gives you yesterday's stock prices (causal), systems have well-defined behavioral characteristics.

**Classification of Systems (KTU Module 1 Coverage):**

| S.No | Classification | The Two Categories |
|------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Linearity** | Linear vs Nonlinear |
| 2 | **Time-Invariance** | Time-Invariant (LTI) vs Time-Variant (LTV) |
| 3 | **Causality** | Causal vs Non-Causal |
| 4 | **Stability** | Stable vs Unstable |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 syllabus, these four properties form the **core foundation** for everything in Signals and Systems. A system that satisfies all four properties simultaneously is called an **LTI-Causal-Stable (LCS) system** — the most important class studied in this course.

### 1.2 Continuous-Time vs Discrete-Time System Notation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Continuous-Time (CT):} \quad & y(t) = \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\} \\
\text{Discrete-Time (DT):} \quad & y[n] = \mathcal{T}\{x[n]\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $t \in \mathbb{R}$ and $n \in \mathbb{Z}$. Both notations will be used side-by-side throughout the KTU exam questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Linear vs Nonlinear Systems

**Definition (Linear System):**
A system is **linear** if and only if it satisfies the **Superposition Principle**, which is the combination of **Additivity** and **Homogeneity (Scaling)**.

$$
\text{If } x_1(t) \xrightarrow{\mathcal{T}} y_1(t) \text{ and } x_2(t) \xrightarrow{\mathcal{T}} y_2(t),
$$

then for any constants $a_1, a_2 \in \mathbb{R}$:

$$
a_1 x_1(t) + a_2 x_2(t) \xrightarrow{\mathcal{T}} a_1 y_1(t) + a_2 y_2(t)
$$

**Test for Nonlinearity:** A system is nonlinear if it contains any of these operations:
- **Multiplication of two input signals:** $y(t) = x(t) \cdot x(t-1)$
- **Squaring or higher powers:** $y(t) = x^2(t)$
- **Modulus / absolute value:** $y(t) = \vert x(t) \vert$
- **Logarithmic functions:** $y(t) = \log(x(t))$
- **Trigonometric functions:** $y(t) = \sin(x(t))$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule:** Constant terms like $y(t) = 2x(t) + 5$ are STILL linear. The "5" is just a DC bias and does not violate superposition. A system with any input-independent constant offset is linear.

### 2.2 Time-Invariant vs Time-Variant Systems

**Definition (Time-Invariant System):**
A system is **Time-Invariant (TI)** if a time-shift in the input signal causes a corresponding identical time-shift in the output signal. The system does not depend on absolute time.

$$
\text{If } x(t) \xrightarrow{\mathcal{T}} y(t),
$$

then for any $t_0 \in \mathbb{R}$:

$$
x(t - t_0) \xrightarrow{\mathcal{T}} y(t - t_0)
$$

**Test for Time-Variance:** A system is time-variant if it contains:
- **Explicit time in the coefficients:** $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$
- **Time-dependent delays:** $y(t) = x(2t)$, $y(t) = x(-t)$
- **Time-dependent scaling:** $y(t) = \cos(t) \cdot x(t)$

### 2.3 Causal vs Non-Causal Systems

**Definition (Causal System):**
A system is **causal** if the output at any time $t$ (or $n$) depends *only* on present and past input values — **never on future input values**.

$$
y(t) = \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\} \text{ depends only on } x(\tau) \text{ for } \tau \leq t
$$

$$
y[n] = \mathcal{T}\{x[n]\} \text{ depends only on } x[k] \text{ for } k \leq n
$$

**Examples:**
- $y(t) = x(t-1)$ → **Causal** (depends on past)
- $y(t) = x(t+1)$ → **Non-Causal** (depends on future)
- $y[n] = x[n] - x[n+2]$ → **Non-Causal** (uses $n+2$)
- $y[n] = x[-n]$ → **Non-Causal** for $n<0$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Fact:** All **memoryless systems** ($y(t) = f(x(t))$) are inherently causal because the output depends only on the present input. Real-time physical systems are always causal.

### 2.4 Stable vs Unstable Systems (BIBO Stability)

**Definition (BIBO Stable System):**
A system is **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) Stable** if *every* bounded input produces a bounded output.

$$
\text{If } \vert x(t) \vert \leq M_x < \infty \text{ for all } t, \text{ then } \vert y(t) \vert \leq M_y < \infty
$$

**Mathematical Criterion (KTU High-Yield):**

$$
\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty \quad \text{(Discrete-Time BIBO Stability)}
$$

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty \quad \text{(Continuous-Time BIBO Stability)}
$$

Where $h(t)$ or $h[n]$ is the **impulse response** of the system.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Big 4" Tests — Keep this table memorized for KTU exams:**

| Property | Test Condition | Counter-Test Condition |
|----------|---------------|------------------------|
| **Linearity** | $\mathcal{T}\{a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2\} = a_1 \mathcal{T}\{x_1\} + a_2 \mathcal{T}\{x_2\}$ | Any $x_1 \cdot x_2$, $x^2$, $\vert x \vert$, $\sin(x)$ |
| **Time-Invariance** | $y(t-t_0) = \mathcal{T}\{x(t-t_0)\}$ | Explicit $t$ in coefficients, $x(2t)$, $x(-t)$ |
| **Causality** | $y(t)$ uses only $x(\tau), \tau \leq t$ | Any $x(t+)$, $x[n+k]$ for $k>0$ |
| **BIBO Stability** | $\int \vert h(t) \vert dt < \infty$ | $\int \vert h(t) \vert dt = \infty$ |

> **Note:** In the table above, $\vert x \vert$ denotes absolute value, written using LaTeX `$\vert$` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
- **LTI Systems** form the backbone of every DSP filter, control system, and communication channel.
- **Causality** is mandatory in real-time applications (radar, audio processing).
- **Stability** is non-negotiable in feedback control (think: Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapse, 1940).
- **Linearity** enables frequency-domain analysis (Fourier/Laplace/Transforms).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

### 3.1 KTU Classic Example: Complete Classification of $y(t) = t \cdot x(t) + 3$

**Step 1 — Linearity Test:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } x_1(t) &\xrightarrow{\mathcal{T}} y_1(t) = t \cdot x_1(t) + 3 \\
\text{Let } x_2(t) &\xrightarrow{\mathcal{T}} y_2(t) = t \cdot x_2(t) + 3 \\
\text{Input: } x_3(t) &= a_1 x_1(t) + a_2 x_2(t) \\
y_3(t) &= t \cdot [a_1 x_1(t) + a_2 x_2(t)] + 3 \\
&= a_1 [t \cdot x_1(t)] + a_2 [t \cdot x_2(t)] + 3 \\
&= a_1 y_1(t) + a_2 y_2(t) - 2a_1 - 2a_2 + 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The "+3" constant breaks homogeneity. However, the constant does NOT depend on input — but the additive constant IS still present. Let's verify with $a_1 = a_2 = 1$:

$$
y_3(t) = t \cdot x_1(t) + t \cdot x_2(t) + 3
$$

$$
a_1 y_1 + a_2 y_2 = y_1 + y_2 = t \cdot x_1 + 3 + t \cdot x_2 + 3 = t(x_1+x_2) + 6
$$

Since $y_3 \neq y_1 + y_2$ (off by 3), the system is **NONLINEAR** due to the constant term. ❌ **Not Linear**

**Step 2 — Time-Invariance Test:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Shifted output: } y(t-t_0) &= (t-t_0) \cdot x(t-t_0) + 3 \\
\text{Output for shifted input: } y_1(t) &= t \cdot x(t-t_0) + 3
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $(t-t_0) \neq t$, the system is **TIME-VARIANT**. ❌ **Not Time-Invariant**

**Step 3 — Causality Test:**

$y(t) = t \cdot x(t) + 3$ — output at time $t$ depends only on $x(t)$ (present input). The coefficient $t$ is not an input value, it's a time index. ✅ **CAUSAL**

**Step 4 — Stability Test:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } \vert x(t) \vert &\leq M < \infty \\
y(t) = t \cdot x(t) + 3 \Rightarrow \vert y(t) \vert &\leq \vert t \vert \cdot M + 3
\end{aligned}
$$

As $t \to \infty$, $\vert t \vert \cdot M \to \infty$. Output is unbounded for bounded input. ❌ **UNSTABLE**

**Final Classification:** **Nonlinear, Time-Variant, Causal, Unstable.**

---

### 3.2 KTU Classic Example: $y[n] = n \cdot x[n-1]$

**Step 1 — Linearity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n] &= n \cdot x[n-1] \quad \text{(no multiplication of two signals)} \\
\mathcal{T}\{a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2\}[n] &= n \cdot (a_1 x_1[n-1] + a_2 x_2[n-1]) \\
&= a_1 n \cdot x_1[n-1] + a_2 n \cdot x_2[n-1] \\
&= a_1 y_1[n] + a_2 y_2[n] \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ **LINEAR**

**Step 2 — Time-Invariance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Shifted output: } y[n-n_0] &= (n-n_0) \cdot x[n-n_0-1] \\
\text{Output for shifted input: } y_1[n] &= n \cdot x[n-n_0-1]
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $(n-n_0) \neq n$, ❌ **TIME-VARIANT**

**Step 3 — Causality:**

$y[n]$ uses $x[n-1]$ (past). ✅ **CAUSAL**

**Step 4 — Stability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert y[n] \vert &= \vert n \vert \cdot \vert x[n-1] \vert \leq \vert n \vert \cdot M
\end{aligned}
$$

As $n \to \infty$, output → $\infty$. ❌ **UNSTABLE**

**Final:** Linear, Time-Variant, Causal, Unstable.

---

### 3.3 KTU Classic Example: $y[n] = x[-n]$ (Time-Reversal System)

**Step 1 — Linearity:**

$$
\mathcal{T}\{a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2\}[n] = (a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2)[-n] = a_1 x_1[-n] + a_2 x_2[-n] = a_1 y_1[n] + a_2 y_2[n]
$$

✅ **LINEAR**

**Step 2 — Time-Invariance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Shifted input: } x_1[n] &= x[n-n_0] \\
y_1[n] &= x_1[-n] = x[-n-n_0] \\
\text{Shifted output: } y[n-n_0] &= x[-(n-n_0)] = x[-n+n_0]
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $x[-n-n_0] \neq x[-n+n_0]$, ❌ **TIME-VARIANT**

**Step 3 — Causality:**

For $n < 0$, say $n = -1$: $y[-1] = x[1]$ (uses future input). ❌ **NON-CAUSAL**

**Step 4 — Stability:**

If $\vert x[n] \vert \leq M$, then $\vert y[n] \vert = \vert x[-n] \vert \leq M$. ✅ **BIBO STABLE**

**Final:** Linear, Time-Variant, Non-Causal, Stable.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Quick-Reference Property Table

| System Equation | Linear? | Time-Invariant? | Causal? | Stable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y(t) = x(t-1)$ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| $y(t) = 2x(t) + 5$ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| $y(t) = x(t) \cdot x(t-2)$ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| $y(t) = x(2t)$ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| $y[n] = x[-n]$ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| $y[n] = n \cdot x[n-1]$ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) d\tau$ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| $y(t) = \frac{d}{dt}x(t)$ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |

> [!NOTE]
> The last two systems are **integrators/accumulators** — the most common example of a stable-input, unstable-output system in KTU question papers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: A Generic System Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Signal x(t)"] --> B["System Operator T"]
    B --> C["Output Signal y(t) = T{x(t)}"]
    B --> D{"Test 1: Linearity?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Linear"]
    D -- No --> F["Nonlinear"]
    B --> G{"Test 2: Time-Invariance?"}
    G -- Yes --> H["Time-Invariant TI"]
    G -- No --> I["Time-Variant TV"]
    B --> J{"Test 3: Causality?"}
    J -- Yes --> K["Causal"]
    J -- No --> L["Non-Causal"]
    B --> M{"Test 4: BIBO Stability?"}
    M -- Yes --> N["Stable"]
    M -- No --> O["Unstable"]
    E --> P["Final Classification: LTI Causal Stable LCS"]
    F --> P
    H --> P
    I --> P
    K --> P
    L --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Decision Flow for Testing Time-Invariance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1: Compute y1(t) = T{x(t-t0)}"] --> B["Step 2: Compute y2(t) = y(t-t0)"]
    B --> C{"y1(t) == y2(t) for all t, t0?"}
    C -- "Yes" --> D["TIME-INVARIANT"]
    C -- "No" --> E["TIME-VARIANT"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Causality Test on a Discrete-Time System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["System: y[n] = T{x[n]}"] --> B{"Does y[n] depend on x[n+k] for any k>0?"}
    B -- "No, only n and n-k" --> C["CAUSAL System"]
    B -- "Yes, uses future x[n+k]" --> D["NON-CAUSAL System"]
    C --> E["Real-Time Implementable"]
    D --> F["Requires Future Knowledge: Offline/Delayed Processing Only"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Conceptual Map: The LTI-Causal-Stable (LCS) Trinity

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph "Properties of an Ideal System"
        L["LINEARITY: Superposition Holds"]
        TI["TIME-INVARIANCE: Shift-Invariant Behavior"]
        C["CAUSALITY: No Future Inputs"]
        S["STABILITY: BIBO Criterion Satisfied"]
    end
    L --> LCS["LTI-Causal-Stable (LCS) System"]
    TI --> LCS
    C --> LCS
    S --> LCS
    LCS --> APP["Applications: FIR/IIR Filters, Control Loops, Communication Channels"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The four properties are *independent* tests. A system can be linear but time-variant, or causal but unstable. Only when all four pass do we get the "ideal" system used in real engineering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define a linear system. Check whether the system $y(t) = x^2(t)$ is linear or nonlinear.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A system is **linear** if it satisfies the principle of superposition:

$$
\mathcal{T}\{a_1 x_1(t) + a_2 x_2(t)\} = a_1 \mathcal{T}\{x_1(t)\} + a_2 \mathcal{T}\{x_2(t)\}
$$

**Test:** Let $x_1(t) \xrightarrow{\mathcal{T}} x_1^2(t)$ and $x_2(t) \xrightarrow{\mathcal{T}} x_2^2(t)$.

Now apply $x_3(t) = a_1 x_1(t) + a_2 x_2(t)$:

$$
y_3(t) = [a_1 x_1(t) + a_2 x_2(t)]^2 = a_1^2 x_1^2(t) + 2a_1 a_2 x_1(t)x_2(t) + a_2^2 x_2^2(t)
$$

$$
a_1 y_1(t) + a_2 y_2(t) = a_1 x_1^2(t) + a_2 x_2^2(t)
$$

Clearly $y_3(t) \neq a_1 y_1(t) + a_2 y_2(t)$ (the cross-term $2a_1 a_2 x_1 x_2$ is extra).

∴ The system is **NONLINEAR**. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
*Define BIBO stability. Is the system $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x[n-k]$ BIBO stable? Justify.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition:** A system is **BIBO stable** if every bounded input produces a bounded output. Mathematically, if $\vert x[n] \vert \leq M$ for all $n$, then $\vert y[n] \vert$ must also be finite.

**Test:** Let $\vert x[n] \vert \leq M$. Then:

$$
\vert y[n] \vert = \left\vert \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x[n-k] \right\vert \leq \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \vert x[n-k] \vert \leq \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} M = \infty
$$

The infinite sum diverges. Therefore, the system produces an unbounded output for a bounded input.

∴ The system is **NOT BIBO STABLE** (Unstable). (3 marks)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to test BOTH conditions of superposition** (additivity AND homogeneity) — KTU examiners deduct 1-2 marks if you only test one.
> 2. **Confusing time-shift direction** in the TI test: Always compute $y_1(t) = \mathcal{T}\{x(t-t_0)\}$ first, then compare with $y(t-t_0)$.
> 3. **Misclassifying time-reversal systems** $x(-t)$ or $x[-n]$: These are non-causal for negative indices. Students often miss this.
> 4. **Stability test must use the IMPULSE RESPONSE $h(t)$** for LTI systems — the integral of $\vert h(t) \vert$ must be finite.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** For each of the following systems, determine whether the system is: **(i)** Linear/Nonlinear, **(ii)** Time-Invariant/Time-Variant, **(iii)** Causal/Non-Causal, **(iv)** Stable/Unstable. **(7 marks)**

1. $y(t) = x(t) \cdot \cos(t)$
2. $y[n] = x[n+2] - x[n-2]$
3. $y(t) = e^{x(t)}$

**(b)** Determine the response $y[n]$ of the LTI system described by the difference equation $y[n] - \frac{1}{2} y[n-1] = x[n]$ for the input $x[n] = \delta[n]$ and verify the BIBO stability using the impulse response. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)(1) $y(t) = x(t) \cdot \cos(t)$:**

- **Linearity:** $\mathcal{T}\{a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2\} = (a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2)\cos(t) = a_1 x_1\cos(t) + a_2 x_2\cos(t)$ ✓ **Linear**
- **Time-Invariance:** $y_1(t) = x(t-t_0)\cos(t) \neq (t-t_0)$-shifted form. The $\cos(t)$ factor changes with $t$. ❌ **Time-Variant**
- **Causality:** $y(t)$ depends on $x(t)$ (present). ✓ **Causal**
- **Stability:** $\vert y(t) \vert \leq M$ if $\vert x(t) \vert \leq M$ (since $\vert \cos(t) \vert \leq 1$). ✓ **Stable**

**Final:** Linear, Time-Variant, Causal, Stable. (3 marks for sub-part)

**(a)(2) $y[n] = x[n+2] - x[n-2]$:**

- **Linear:** No product of inputs. ✓ **Linear**
- **Time-Invariant:** $y_1[n] = x[n+2-n_0] - x[n-2-n_0] = y[n-n_0]$ ✓ **Time-Invariant**
- **Causality:** $x[n+2]$ is FUTURE input. ❌ **Non-Causal**
- **Stability:** $\vert y[n] \vert \leq 2M$ ✓ **Stable**

**Final:** Linear, Time-Invariant, Non-Causal, Stable. (2 marks)

**(a)(3) $y(t) = e^{x(t)}$:**

- **Linearity:** $e^{a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2} \neq a_1 e^{x_1} + a_2 e^{x_2}$ ❌ **Nonlinear**
- **Time-Invariance:** $y_1(t) = e^{x(t-t_0)} = y(t-t_0)$ ✓ **Time-Invariant**
- **Causality:** ✓ **Causal**
- **Stability:** If $x(t)$ bounded, $e^{x(t)}$ is bounded. ✓ **Stable**

**Final:** Nonlinear, Time-Invariant, Causal, Stable. (2 marks)

**(b) Difference Equation $y[n] - \frac{1}{2} y[n-1] = x[n]$, $x[n] = \delta[n]$:**

Step 1: For $n < 0$, assume zero initial conditions. At $n=0$:

$$
y[0] - \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0 = \delta[0] = 1 \Rightarrow y[0] = 1
$$

Step 2: For $n=1$:

$$
y[1] = \frac{1}{2} y[0] + \delta[1] = \frac{1}{2}(1) + 0 = \frac{1}{2}
$$

Step 3: For $n=2$:

$$
y[2] = \frac{1}{2} y[1] + 0 = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2
$$

Step 4: By induction, the impulse response is:

$$
h[n] = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n u[n]
$$

**BIBO Stability Verification:**

$$
\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{2}} = 2 < \infty
$$

Since the sum is finite, the system is **BIBO STABLE**. (7 marks for part b)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** [KTU University Exam - June 2023]

**(a)** For the system $y(t) = t \cdot x(t) + \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) d\tau$, perform complete classification: Linear/Nonlinear, Time-Invariant/Time-Variant, Causal/Non-Causal, Stable/Unstable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With proper justification, classify the discrete-time systems: **(i)** $y[n] = nx[n]$, **(ii)** $y[n] = x[n] \cdot x[n-1]$, **(iii)** $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) $y(t) = t \cdot x(t) + \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) d\tau$:**

- **Linearity Test:**
Let $x_3(t) = a_1 x_1(t) + a_2 x_2(t)$:
$$
y_3(t) = t[a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2] + \int_{-\infty}^{t} [a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2] d\tau
$$
$$
= a_1 \left[ t x_1 + \int_{-\infty}^{t} x_1 d\tau \right] + a_2 \left[ t x_2 + \int_{-\infty}^{t} x_2 d\tau \right] = a_1 y_1 + a_2 y_2
$$

✓ **LINEAR** (3 marks for linearity test)

- **Time-Invariance Test:**
$$
y_1(t) = t \cdot x(t-t_0) + \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau-t_0) d\tau
$$
$$
y(t-t_0) = (t-t_0) x(t-t_0) + \int_{-\infty}^{t-t_0} x(\tau) d\tau
$$

The $t$ vs $(t-t_0)$ mismatch in the first term shows ❌ **TIME-VARIANT**. (1 mark)

- **Causality Test:** $y(t)$ uses $x(t)$ (present) and $x(\tau)$ for $\tau \leq t$ (past). ✓ **CAUSAL** (1 mark)

- **Stability Test:** Let $\vert x(t) \vert \leq M$:
$$
\vert y(t) \vert \leq \vert t \vert M + \int_{-\infty}^{t} M \, d\tau \to \infty
$$
❌ **UNSTABLE** (2 marks)

**Final:** Linear, Time-Variant, Causal, Unstable.

**(b)(i) $y[n] = n \cdot x[n]$:**

- Linear: ✓ (coefficient $n$ doesn't multiply two inputs)
- Time-Variant: ❌ (explicit $n$ in coefficient)
- Causal: ✓
- Stable: ❌ (output grows with $n$)

**Final:** Linear, Time-Variant, Causal, Unstable. (2 marks)

**(b)(ii) $y[n] = x[n] \cdot x[n-1]$:**

- Linear: ❌ (multiplication of two input signals)
- Time-Invariant: ✓ (no explicit $n$)
- Causal: ✓ (uses $x[n]$ and $x[n-1]$, present and past)
- Stable: If $\vert x[n] \vert \leq M$, then $\vert y[n] \vert \leq M^2$ ✓

**Final:** Nonlinear, Time-Invariant, Causal, Stable. (2 marks)

**(b)(iii) $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$ (Accumulator):**

- Linear: ✓ (sum of scaled inputs)
- Time-Invariant: ✓
- Causal: ✓ (sum up to current $n$)
- Stable: ❌ (sum over infinite past can diverge for bounded input, e.g., $x[n] = 1$ gives $y[n] = \infty$)

**Final:** Linear, Time-Invariant, Causal, Unstable. (3 marks)

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **system** is an operator that maps input to output: $y = \mathcal{T}\{x\}$.
- ✅ **Linearity** = Additivity + Homogeneity. Any $x_1 \cdot x_2$, $x^2$, $\vert x \vert$, $\sin(x)$, $e^x$ → **Nonlinear**.
- ✅ **Time-Invariance** is broken by explicit time ($t$, $n$, $\cos(t)$) in coefficients, or by operations like $x(2t)$, $x(-t)$, $x[n+2]$.
- ✅ **Causality** = output depends only on present and past inputs. Memoryless systems are always causal. Real-time systems must be causal.
- ✅ **BIBO Stability** is governed by the **absolute integrability / summability** of the impulse response $h$.
- ✅ A constant offset (e.g., $+5$) does NOT make a system nonlinear — it is a DC bias and still satisfies superposition.
- ✅ The **integrator** $y(t) = \int x(\tau) d\tau$ and **accumulator** $y[n] = \sum x[k]$ are the canonical examples of *stable-input / unstable-output* systems in KTU papers.
- ✅ The **time-reversal system** $y(t) = x(-t)$ is the canonical example of a *non-causal* LTI system.
- ✅ **LCS (LTI-Causal-Stable)** systems are the most-studied class in this course — they form the foundation of every DSP filter and control system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a problem says "test the system for linearity, time-invariance, causality, and stability," always write a **header table with four columns** and fill in each row. Examiners love structured answers, and you'll earn at least 1 mark per property even if your derivation has a minor error.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples of Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT403 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Examples of Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a System (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **system** is a mathematical model that responds to an input signal (or signals) by producing an output signal (or signals). Formally, a system is an operator **T** that maps an input signal **x** to an output signal **y**.

$$y(t) = T\{x(t)\} \quad \text{(Continuous-Time System)}$$

$$y[n] = T\{x[n]\} \quad \text{(Discrete-Time System)}$$

Where **T** represents the transformation rule defining the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Standard):** A system is a physical device, software algorithm, or mathematical operator that accepts an input signal and produces an output signal according to a well-defined rule. The study of systems focuses on understanding how transformations of the input manifest in the output.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a system as a **"black-box coffee vending machine"**:

- **Input (x):** The coin + button selection.
- **System (T):** The internal mechanical/electronic process.
- **Output (y):** The cup of coffee dispensed.

Different systems (a coffee machine, a washing machine, a digital filter) take the *same* input (electricity) and produce *different* outputs. Similarly in Signals and Systems, the same input signal can produce different outputs depending on the system properties.

**Engineering Realms where "Systems" are studied:**
- **Electrical Engineering:** Filters, Amplifiers, Modulators
- **Communication:** Channel equalizers, Encoders
- **Biomedical:** ECG/EEG signal processors, Pacemaker logic
- **Control Engineering:** Cruise control, Robotic arm dynamics
- **Audio/Speech:** Equalizers, Noise cancellers
- **Image Processing:** Edge detectors, Smoothing filters

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):** Students must be able to identify and analyze the properties of common example systems such as identity, delay, accumulator, differentiator, integrator, moving average, modulator, and feedback systems.

## 1.3 Why "Examples of Systems" Matter in KTU Exams

In KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examinations, the topic "Examples of Systems" is the **foundation stone** for:

1. Verifying **linearity** (superposition principle)
2. Checking **time-invariance**
3. Establishing **causality**
4. Determining **memory** characteristics
5. Establishing **BIBO stability**

Every example system we study will be used as a **test case** to verify these properties.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Systems

Systems are classified based on **seven fundamental properties**:

| S.No. | Property | Possible Categories |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Domain** | Continuous-Time (CT) / Discrete-Time (DT) |
| 2 | **Memory** | Memoryless / With Memory |
| 3 | **Causality** | Causal / Non-Causal / Anti-Causal |
| 4 | **Linearity** | Linear / Non-Linear |
| 5 | **Time-Invariance** | Time-Invariant (TI) / Time-Variant (TV) |
| 6 | **Stability** | BIBO Stable / Unstable |
| 7 | **Invertibility** | Invertible / Non-Invertible |

## 2.2 Mathematical Conditions for Each Property

### (a) Linearity
A system **T** is **linear** if and only if it satisfies both:

$$T\{a\,x_1(t) + b\,x_2(t)\} = a\,T\{x_1(t)\} + b\,T\{x_2(t)\}$$

- **Additivity:** T{x₁ + x₂} = T{x₁} + T{x₂}
- **Homogeneity (Scaling):** T{αx} = αT{x}

### (b) Time-Invariance
A system is **time-invariant** if a time shift in input produces the same time shift in output:

$$\text{If } y(t) = T\{x(t)\}, \text{ then } y(t-t_0) = T\{x(t-t_0)\}$$

### (c) Causality
A system is **causal** if the output at any time depends only on present and past input values (not future values):

$$y(t) \text{ depends only on } x(\tau) \text{ for } \tau \le t$$

### (d) Stability (BIBO)
A system is **BIBO Stable** if every bounded input produces a bounded output:

$$\text{If } \vert x(t) \vert \le M_x < \infty, \text{ then } \vert y(t) \vert \le M_y < \infty$$

### (e) Memory
A system is **memoryless** if the output depends only on the present input:

$$y(t) \text{ depends only on } x(t) \text{ at the same instant}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MANDATORY Table — Print This for Exam Day**

| System Name | Equation | Memory | Causal? | Linear? | TI? | Stable? |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Identity (CT)** | $y(t) = x(t)$ | No (Memoryless) | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Identity (DT)** | $y[n] = x[n]$ | No (Memoryless) | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Amplifier (CT)** | $y(t) = K\,x(t)$ | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Time-Shift (CT)** | $y(t) = x(t-t_0)$ | Yes | If $t_0 \ge 0$ | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Time-Shift (DT)** | $y[n] = x[n-n_0]$ | Yes | If $n_0 \ge 0$ | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Time-Reversal (CT)** | $y(t) = x(-t)$ | Yes | No | Yes | No | Yes |
| **Time-Reversal (DT)** | $y[n] = x[-n]$ | Yes | No | Yes | No | Yes |
| **Differentiator (CT)** | $y(t) = \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$ | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| **Integrator (CT)** | $y(t) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)\,d\tau$ | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| **Accumulator (DT)** | $y[n] = \displaystyle\sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$ | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| **Backward Difference** | $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$ | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Moving Average** | $y[n] = \tfrac{1}{3}\{x[n-1]+x[n]+x[n+1]\}$ | Yes | No (uses $n+1$) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Squarer** | $y(t) = x^2(t)$ | No | Yes | **No** | Yes | Yes |
| **Modulator (CT)** | $y(t) = x(t)\cos(\omega_0 t)$ | No | Yes | Yes | **No** | Yes |
| **TV Amplifier (DT)** | $y[n] = n\,x[n]$ | No | Yes | Yes | **No** | Yes |
| **Exponential** | $y(t) = e^{x(t)}$ | No | Yes | **No** | Yes | Yes |
| **Squaring Plus Delay** | $y[n] = x^2[n-1]$ | Yes | Yes | **No** | Yes | Yes |
| **LCCDE (DT)** | $\sum a_k y[n-k] = \sum b_k x[n-k]$ | Yes | Yes (if causal form) | Yes | Yes | Depends on coefficients |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Example System | Engineering Application |
|:---|:---|
| Differentiator | Edge detection in image processing, FM demodulation |
| Integrator | Charge calculation in capacitors, ADC, VCO |
| Accumulator | Digital energy meter, cumulative sum control |
| Moving Average Filter | Noise removal in biomedical signals (ECG/EEG) |
| Modulator | AM/FM radio transmission, communication systems |
| Time Reversal | Radar signal processing, acoustic focusing |
| LCCDE | Recursive digital filter design (IIR/FIR) |
| Feedback System | Cruise control, Active suspension, Op-Amps |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analysis of Example Systems

## 3.1 Example 1 — Identity System (The Trivial System)

### System Equation
$$y(t) = x(t)$$

### Property Verification

**(a) Linearity Test:**
Let $x(t) = a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)$. Then:

$$y(t) = T\{a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)\} = a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)$$

Also,

$$a T\{x_1(t)\} + b T\{x_2(t)\} = a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)$$

$$\Rightarrow \text{Both are equal} \Rightarrow \textbf{Linear}$$

**(b) Time-Invariance Test:**
Let $x_1(t) = x(t - t_0)$. Then:

$$y_1(t) = T\{x(t-t_0)\} = x(t-t_0)$$

Also,

$$y(t-t_0) = x(t-t_0)$$

$$\Rightarrow y_1(t) = y(t-t_0) \Rightarrow \textbf{Time-Invariant}$$

**(c) Causality, Memory, Stability:** All TRUE (by inspection).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The identity system satisfies every basic property. It is the simplest example of a *memoryless, causal, linear, time-invariant, stable (MCLTS)* system.

## 3.2 Example 2 — Time-Shift System (Pure Delay / Advance)

### System Equation (CT)
$$y(t) = x(t - t_0)$$

- If $t_0 > 0$: **Pure Delay** (Causal)
- If $t_0 < 0$: **Advance** (Non-Causal)
- If $t_0 = 0$: **Identity**

### Linearity Verification

$$T\{a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)\} = a x_1(t-t_0) + b x_2(t-t_0)$$

$$= a T\{x_1(t)\} + b T\{x_2(t)\} \Rightarrow \textbf{Linear}$$

### Time-Invariance Verification

$$y_1(t) = T\{x(t-t_1)\} = x(t-t_1-t_0)$$

$$y(t-t_1) = x((t-t_1) - t_0) = x(t-t_1-t_0)$$

$$\Rightarrow y_1(t) = y(t-t_1) \Rightarrow \textbf{Time-Invariant}$$

### Causality Conclusion

The output $y(t)$ depends on input at $t - t_0$ (a time $t_0$ in the past if $t_0 > 0$, in the future if $t_0 < 0$).

- $t_0 \ge 0 \Rightarrow$ **Causal** (Delay)
- $t_0 < 0 \Rightarrow$ **Non-Causal** (Advance — needs future input)

## 3.3 Example 3 — Time-Reversal System

### System Equation
$$y(t) = x(-t)$$

### Linearity

$$T\{a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)\} = a x_1(-t) + b x_2(-t) = a y_1(t) + b y_2(t) \Rightarrow \textbf{Linear}$$

### Time-Invariance

Let input be shifted: $x_1(t) = x(t - t_0)$

$$y_1(t) = T\{x(t-t_0)\} = x(-t-t_0)$$

Now shift the output:

$$y(t-t_0) = x(-(t-t_0)) = x(-t+t_0)$$

Clearly,

$$y_1(t) = x(-t-t_0) \neq x(-t+t_0) = y(t-t_0)$$

$$\Rightarrow \textbf{NOT Time-Invariant (Time-Variant)}$$

### Causality

$y(t) = x(-t)$. To compute $y$ at $t = 5$, we need $x(-5) = x$ at future time.
$\Rightarrow$ **Non-Causal**.

## 3.4 Example 4 — Differentiator (CT)

### System Equation
$$y(t) = \frac{dx(t)}{dt}$$

### Linearity Check

$$T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = \frac{d}{dt}(a x_1 + b x_2) = a \frac{dx_1}{dt} + b \frac{dx_2}{dt} = a y_1 + b y_2 \Rightarrow \textbf{Linear}$$

### Time-Invariance Check

$$y_1(t) = T\{x(t-t_0)\} = \frac{dx(t-t_0)}{dt} = \frac{dx}{d(t-t_0)}$$

$$y(t-t_0) = \frac{dx(t-t_0)}{dt} = \frac{dx}{d(t-t_0)}$$

$$\Rightarrow y_1(t) = y(t-t_0) \Rightarrow \textbf{Time-Invariant}$$

### Stability Issue

If input is bounded, $x(t) = u(t)$ (unit step, bounded), then:

$$y(t) = \frac{du(t)}{dt} = \delta(t)$$

The impulse $\delta(t)$ is **unbounded**. Hence the system is **BIBO Unstable**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often mark the differentiator as stable because the formula "looks simple." ALWAYS test with a ramp $x(t) = t\,u(t)$ or a step. Bounded input $\rightarrow$ unbounded output = **Unstable**.

## 3.5 Example 5 — Integrator (CT)

### System Equation
$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)\,d\tau$$

### Linearity

$$T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = \int_{-\infty}^{t} [a x_1(\tau) + b x_2(\tau)]\,d\tau$$

$$= a \int_{-\infty}^{t} x_1(\tau)\,d\tau + b \int_{-\infty}^{t} x_2(\tau)\,d\tau = a y_1 + b y_2 \Rightarrow \textbf{Linear}$$

### Time-Invariance

Let $x_1(t) = x(t - t_0)$:

$$y_1(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau - t_0)\,d\tau$$

Substitute $\lambda = \tau - t_0$, $d\lambda = d\tau$:

$$y_1(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t-t_0} x(\lambda)\,d\lambda = y(t-t_0) \Rightarrow \textbf{Time-Invariant}$$

### Stability Test

Let $x(t) = u(t)$ (bounded, magnitude $\le 1$).

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} u(\tau)\,d\tau = t\,u(t)$$

As $t \to \infty$, $y(t) \to \infty$ (unbounded). $\Rightarrow$ **BIBO Unstable**.

## 3.6 Example 6 — Accumulator (DT Counterpart of Integrator)

### System Equation
$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$$

### Linearity

$$T\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} [a x_1[k] + b x_2[k]] = a y_1[n] + b y_2[n] \Rightarrow \textbf{Linear}$$

### Time-Invariance

Let $x_1[n] = x[n - n_0]$:

$$y_1[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k - n_0]$$

Substitute $m = k - n_0$:

$$y_1[n] = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{n-n_0} x[m] = y[n - n_0] \Rightarrow \textbf{TI}$$

### Stability Test

Input $x[n] = u[n]$ (bounded, $\le 1$):

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{n} 1 = n + 1 \to \infty \Rightarrow \textbf{Unstable}$$

## 3.7 Example 7 — Modulator $y(t) = x(t)\cos(\omega_0 t)$

### Linearity Check

$$T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = [a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)]\cos(\omega_0 t) = a x_1(t)\cos(\omega_0 t) + b x_2(t)\cos(\omega_0 t) \Rightarrow \textbf{Linear}$$

### Time-Invariance Check

Let $x_1(t) = x(t - t_0)$:

$$y_1(t) = x(t-t_0)\cos(\omega_0 t)$$

$$y(t - t_0) = x(t-t_0)\cos[\omega_0(t-t_0)] = x(t-t_0)\cos(\omega_0 t - \omega_0 t_0)$$

Clearly,

$$\cos(\omega_0 t) \neq \cos(\omega_0 t - \omega_0 t_0)$$

$$\Rightarrow y_1(t) \neq y(t-t_0) \Rightarrow \textbf{Time-Variant}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Multiplying $x(t)$ by any function of $t$ (like $\cos \omega_0 t$, $\sin \omega_0 t$, $t$, $n$) makes the system **Time-Variant**. The system "knows" the absolute time.

## 3.8 Example 8 — Squarer $y(t) = x^2(t)$

### Linearity Check

$$T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = (a x_1 + b x_2)^2 = a^2 x_1^2 + b^2 x_2^2 + 2ab x_1 x_2$$

$$a y_1 + b y_2 = a x_1^2 + b x_2^2$$

$$T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} \neq a y_1 + b y_2 \Rightarrow \textbf{Non-Linear}$$

### Time-Invariance

$$y_1(t) = [x(t-t_0)]^2 = y(t-t_0) \Rightarrow \textbf{TI}$$

### Causality & Stability

Memoryless, Causal, BIBO Stable.

## 3.9 Example 9 — Moving Average Filter

### System Equation
$$y[n] = \frac{1}{3}\left(x[n-1] + x[n] + x[n+1]\right)$$

### Property Analysis

- **Linearity:** Weighted sum of inputs $\Rightarrow$ **Linear**.
- **Time-Invariance:** Coefficients are constants $\Rightarrow$ **TI**.
- **Causality:** Uses $x[n+1]$ (future) $\Rightarrow$ **Non-Causal**.
- **Stability:** Sum of bounded signals is bounded $\Rightarrow$ **Stable**.

## 3.10 Example 10 — Linear Constant Coefficient Difference Equation (LCCDE)

### System Equation
$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k\,y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\,x[n-k]$$

For a causal LCCDE, the output $y[n]$ depends on $x[n], x[n-1], \dots$ (no future inputs).

### Special Case: First-Order LCCDE
$$y[n] - a\,y[n-1] = b\,x[n]$$
$$\Rightarrow y[n] = a\,y[n-1] + b\,x[n]$$

- **Linearity:** Yes (linear combination of shifted input/output).
- **Time-Invariance:** Yes (constant coefficients).
- **Causality:** Yes (uses $x[n]$ and $y[n-1]$).
- **Stability:** Stable if $\vert a \vert < 1$.

## 3.11 Example 11 — Feedback System (Block Diagram Analysis)

Consider a feedback system with **forward path** $G$ and **feedback path** $H$:

$$Y(s) = G(s)\,[X(s) - H(s)\,Y(s)]$$

$$Y(s)[1 + G(s)H(s)] = G(s)\,X(s)$$

$$H_{cl}(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

If $G$ and $H$ are LTI, the closed-loop system is also LTI.

## 3.12 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "MVT Decision Matrix" — used for fast property identification in exams:**

| If the equation contains... | Then the system is... |
|:---|:---|
| $x(\cdot)$ raised to power $\neq 1$ (e.g., $x^2$, $x^3$, $\vert x \vert$) | **Non-Linear** |
| $x(\cdot)$ inside $\sin$, $\cos$, $\exp$, $\log$ | **Non-Linear** |
| A multiplicative coefficient that depends on $t$ or $n$ (e.g., $\cos \omega t$, $n$) | **Time-Variant** |
| Future values: $x(t+1)$, $x[n+2]$ | **Non-Causal** |
| Past values: $x(t-1)$, $x[n-2]$ | **Has Memory** |
| Time reversal: $x(-t)$, $x[-n]$ | **Non-Causal & Time-Variant** |
| Pure delay/advance only | **Memory & Causal(if delayed)** |
| Sum of shifted inputs (with constants) | **LTI** |
| Differentiation or integration of $x$ | **Linear, TI, Unstable** |
| Accumulator / Running sum | **Linear, TI, Unstable** |

## 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree: System Property Identification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Given System y T x]:::startNode --> Q1{Does y depend on past future values of x?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> MEM[Has Memory]

    Q2 -- Yes --> NC[Non Causal]:::propertyNode
    Q2 -- No --> C[Causal]:::propertyNode
    ML --> C
    C --> Q3{Is system equation linear in x?}
    NC --> Q3
    Q3 -- Yes --> LIN[Linear]:::propertyNode
    Q3 -- No --> NL[Non Linear]:::propertyNode
    LIN --> Q4{Coefficients depend on time t or n?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> TV[Time Variant]:::propertyNode
    Q4 -- No --> TINV[Time Invariant]:::propertyNode
    NL --> STABLE[BIBO Stability Check]:::propertyNode
    TINV --> STABLE
    TV --> STABLE

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Define a system. Distinguish between continuous-time and discrete-time systems with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A system is a mathematical operator that maps an input signal $x$ to an output signal $y$ according to a transformation rule $T$.

$$y = T\{x\}$$

| Feature | Continuous-Time (CT) | Discrete-Time (DT) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Independent variable | Time $t \in \mathbb{R}$ | Sample index $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| Signal representation | $x(t)$ | $x[n]$ |
| Example 1 | RC Low-Pass Filter: $RC\dfrac{dy}{dt} + y = x$ | Accumulator: $y[n] = \sum x[k]$ |
| Example 2 | Op-amp Integrator | Digital Moving Average Filter |

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for examples)*

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Check whether the system $y(t) = x^2(t)$ is linear, time-invariant, and stable.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**(a) Linearity:**
$$T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = (a x_1 + b x_2)^2 = a^2 x_1^2 + b^2 x_2^2 + 2ab x_1 x_2$$
$$a T\{x_1\} + b T\{x_2\} = a x_1^2 + b x_2^2$$
Since the cross term $2ab\,x_1 x_2$ is present, the system is **Non-Linear**. (1 mark)

**(b) Time-Invariance:**
$$y_1(t) = T\{x(t-t_0)\} = [x(t-t_0)]^2 = y(t-t_0)$$
Hence the system is **Time-Invariant**. (1 mark)

**(c) Stability:**
If $\vert x(t) \vert \le M$, then $\vert y(t) \vert = \vert x(t) \vert^2 \le M^2 < \infty$. Hence **BIBO Stable**. (1 mark)

---

## PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Provided

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) Explain the following properties of systems with suitable examples: (i) Memory and memoryless (ii) Causality (iii) Stability. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Memory and Memoryless Systems:**

A system is **memoryless** if the output at any time depends only on the present value of the input.
A system has **memory** if the output depends on past or future values of the input.

- **Example of Memoryless:** $y(t) = R\,x(t)$ (Resistor's V-I relation). Output depends only on present input.
- **Example with Memory:** $y(t) = \dfrac{1}{C}\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)\,d\tau$ (Capacitor). Output depends on all past inputs.

**(ii) Causality:**

A system is **causal** if the output at time $t$ depends only on present and past input values, not on future values.

- **Causal Example:** $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$ (uses $x[n]$ and $x[n-1]$).
- **Non-Causal Example:** $y[n] = x[n+1] - x[n]$ (uses future value $x[n+1]$).

**(iii) Stability (BIBO):**

A system is **BIBO Stable** if for every bounded input, the output remains bounded.

- **Stable Example:** $y(t) = 2x(t)$ (Amplifier with finite gain).
- **Unstable Example:** $y(t) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)\,d\tau$ (Integrator — bounded step gives unbounded ramp).

---

**(b) Determine whether the following systems are (i) Linear (ii) Time-Invariant (iii) Causal (iv) Stable. (7 Marks)**

**System 1:** $y(t) = x(t)\cos(t)$

**System 2:** $y[n] = n\,x[n]$

**Model Solution:**

### **System 1: $y(t) = x(t)\cos(t)$**

**(i) Linearity:**
$$T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = [a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)]\cos(t) = a x_1(t)\cos(t) + b x_2(t)\cos(t) = a y_1 + b y_2$$
$\Rightarrow$ **Linear** (1/2 mark)

**(ii) Time-Invariance:**
Let $x_1(t) = x(t - t_0)$:
$$y_1(t) = x(t-t_0)\cos(t)$$
$$y(t-t_0) = x(t-t_0)\cos(t-t_0)$$
Since $\cos(t) \neq \cos(t-t_0)$, $y_1(t) \neq y(t-t_0)$.
$\Rightarrow$ **Time-Variant** (1 mark)

**(iii) Causality:** Depends only on $x(t)$ at same instant $\Rightarrow$ **Causal** (1/2 mark)

**(iv) Stability:** $\vert \cos(t) \vert \le 1$, so if $\vert x(t) \vert \le M$, $\vert y(t) \vert \le M$ $\Rightarrow$ **Stable** (1/2 mark)

### **System 2: $y[n] = n\,x[n]$**

**(i) Linearity:**
$$T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = n[a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]] = a(n x_1[n]) + b(n x_2[n]) = a y_1 + b y_2$$
$\Rightarrow$ **Linear** (1/2 mark)

**(ii) Time-Invariance:**
Let $x_1[n] = x[n - n_0]$:
$$y_1[n] = n\,x[n - n_0]$$
$$y[n - n_0] = (n - n_0)\,x[n - n_0]$$
Since $n \neq (n - n_0)$ in general, $y_1[n] \neq y[n - n_0]$.
$\Rightarrow$ **Time-Variant** (1 mark)
