- Decode the hidden oscillatory + decaying behavior of a signal.
- Solve otherwise unsolvable differential equations algebraically.
- Diagnose the "health" (stability) of a control system.

## 1.3 Region of Convergence (ROC) — The "Valid Address" of the Transform

The Laplace integral converges only for a specific range of complex $s$-values. This range is called the **Region of Convergence (ROC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition of ROC:** The set of all complex numbers $s = \sigma + j\omega$ for which the integral $X(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-st} dt$ yields a finite value.

**Key Properties of ROC (KTU High-Yield):**
- The ROC is a **strip** (or half-plane) in the complex $s$-plane parallel to the $j\omega$-axis.
- For **right-sided** signals (causal, $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$): ROC is $\text{Re}(s) > \sigma_{max}$, a right-half plane.
- For **left-sided** signals: ROC is $\text{Re}(s) < \sigma_{min}$, a left-half plane.
- For **two-sided** signals: ROC is a vertical strip $\sigma_1 < \text{Re}(s) < \sigma_2$.
- The ROC **never** includes any pole of $X(s)$ (poles are singularities where $X(s) \to \infty$).
- If $x(t)$ is of **finite duration and absolutely integrable**, the ROC is the **entire $s$-plane**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic: Why the Laplace Transform Works

The Laplace Transform is essentially the **Fourier Transform multiplied by a convergence factor** $e^{-\sigma t}$. When the signal $x(t)$ grows exponentially (e.g., $e^{at}$ with $a > 0$), the Fourier Transform fails to converge. The factor $e^{-\sigma t}$ counteracts this growth, forcing the integral to converge.

**Three-Step Operational Logic:**

1. **Multiplication by $e^{-\sigma t}$:** Suppresses diverging portions of $x(t)$.
2. **Integration from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$:** Accumulates the weighted signal energy across all time.
3. **Domain Shift:** Maps the time-domain $t$-axis onto the complex $s$-plane, converting differential equations into algebraic equations.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **definitive KTU 2024 reference** for all standard Laplace transform pairs and properties. Memorize every row.

### Table 2.A: Standard Laplace Transform Pairs

| **Time Domain Signal $x(t)$** | **Laplace Transform $X(s)$** | **Region of Convergence (ROC)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\delta(t)$ (Unit Impulse) | $1$ | All $s$ |
| $u(t)$ (Unit Step) | $\dfrac{1}{s}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| $e^{-at} u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{s+a}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |
| $\cos(\omega_0 t) \, u(t)$ | $\dfrac{s}{s^{2} + \omega_0^{2}}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| $\sin(\omega_0 t) \, u(t)$ | $\dfrac{\omega_0}{s^{2} + \omega_0^{2}}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| $t \, u(t)$ (Ramp) | $\dfrac{1}{s^{2}}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| $t^{n} u(t)$ | $\dfrac{n!}{s^{n+1}}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| $e^{-at}\cos(\omega_0 t) \, u(t)$ | $\dfrac{s+a}{(s+a)^{2} + \omega_0^{2}}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |
| $e^{-at}\sin(\omega_0 t) \, u(t)$ | $\dfrac{\omega_0}{(s+a)^{2} + \omega_0^{2}}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |
| $\delta'(t)$ (Doublet) | $s$ | All $s$ |

### Table 2.B: Properties of the Unilateral Laplace Transform

| **Property** | **Time Domain** | **Laplace (s) Domain** | **Initial Conditions** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Linearity** | $a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)$ | $a X_1(s) + b X_2(s)$ | None |
| **Time Shifting** | $x(t - t_0) u(t-t_0)$ | $e^{-s t_0} X(s)$ | $t_0 \geq 0$ |
| **Frequency Shifting (s-shift)** | $e^{-at} x(t)$ | $X(s+a)$ | None |
| **Time Scaling** | $x(at)$ | $\dfrac{1}{\vert a \vert} X\!\left(\dfrac{s}{a}\right)$ | $a > 0$ |
| **Differentiation in Time** | $\dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$ | $sX(s) - x(0^{-})$ | Requires $x(0^-)$ |
| **Second Derivative** | $\dfrac{d^{2}x(t)}{dt^{2}}$ | $s^{2} X(s) - s x(0^{-}) - x'(0^{-})$ | Requires $x(0^-), x'(0^-)$ |
| **Integration in Time** | $\displaystyle\int_{0^-}^{t} x(\tau) d\tau$ | $\dfrac{X(s)}{s}$ | None |
| **Convolution in Time** | $x_1(t) * x_2(t)$ | $X_1(s) \, X_2(s)$ | None |
| **Multiplication by $t$** | $t x(t)$ | $-\dfrac{d X(s)}{d s}$ | None |
| **Initial Value Theorem (IVT)** | $\displaystyle\lim_{t \to 0^{+}} x(t)$ | $\lim_{s \to \infty} s X(s)$ | Only if limit exists |
| **Final Value Theorem (FVT)** | $\displaystyle\lim_{t \to \infty} x(t)$ | $\lim_{s \to 0} s X(s)$ | Poles of $sX(s)$ in LHP only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Initial and Final Value Theorems — KTU Examiner's Favourite!**
> - **IVT** assumes $x(t)$ contains no impulses or higher-order singularities at $t = 0$.
> - **FVT** is **only valid** if all poles of $sX(s)$ lie strictly in the **left-half plane (LHP)**. If any pole is on the $j\omega$-axis or in the right-half plane (RHP), the FVT is **invalid** and the limit does not exist.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Laplace Transform is the **mathematical backbone** of:
- **Control Systems:** Designing PID controllers for robotics and aerospace (e.g., Boeing 787 autopilot).
- **Electrical Circuits:** Solving RLC transient response without solving differential equations directly.
- **Signal Processing:** Analyzing filter stability in the $s$-plane.
- **Mechanical Vibrations:** Predicting resonance and damping in vehicle suspension systems.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Modeling cardiac pacemaker dynamics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Laplace Transform of the Unit Step Function

We need to derive $X(s) = \mathcal{L}\{u(t)\}$ from first principles to satisfy KTU's rigorous valuation standards.

**Step 1 — Substitute into the bilateral definition:**

$$X(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} u(t) \, e^{-st} \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Apply the definition of the unit step:**

Since $u(t) = 1$ for $t \geq 0$ and $u(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$, the integral reduces to:

$$X(s) = \int_{0}^{+\infty} (1) \cdot e^{-st} \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$X(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-st} \, dt = \left[ \frac{e^{-st}}{-s} \right]_{0}^{\infty}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the limits:**

- At upper limit $t \to \infty$: $e^{-s \cdot \infty} = 0$ if and only if $\text{Re}(s) > 0$.
- At lower limit $t = 0$: $e^{0} = 1$.

Therefore:

$$X(s) = 0 - \left( \frac{1}{-s} \right) = \frac{1}{s}$$

with ROC: $\text{Re}(s) > 0$.

$$\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \frac{1}{s}, \quad \text{Re}(s) > 0}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Laplace Transform of $e^{-at} u(t)$ (Exponential Decay)

**Step 1 — Substitute:**

$$X(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-at} \cdot e^{-st} \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s+a)t} \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$X(s) = \left[ \frac{e^{-(s+a)t}}{-(s+a)} \right]_{0}^{\infty}$$

**Step 3 — Apply convergence condition:**

For convergence, we need $\text{Re}(s+a) > 0 \implies \text{Re}(s) > -a$.

$$X(s) = 0 - \left( \frac{1}{-(s+a)} \right) = \frac{1}{s+a}$$

$$\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at} u(t)\} = \frac{1}{s+a}, \quad \text{Re}(s) > -a}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Laplace Transform of $\cos(\omega_0 t) u(t)$

**Step 1 — Use Euler's identity:**

$$\cos(\omega_0 t) = \frac{e^{j\omega_0 t} + e^{-j\omega_0 t}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply linearity of the transform:**

$$X(s) = \frac{1}{2} \mathcal{L}\{e^{j\omega_0 t} u(t)\} + \frac{1}{2} \mathcal{L}\{e^{-j\omega_0 t} u(t)\}$$

**Step 3 — Use the result of Derivation 2** with $a = -j\omega_0$ and $a = +j\omega_0$:

$$X(s) = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{1}{s - j\omega_0} + \frac{1}{s + j\omega_0} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Combine over a common denominator:**

$$X(s) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{(s + j\omega_0) + (s - j\omega_0)}{(s - j\omega_0)(s + j\omega_0)}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$X(s) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2s}{s^2 + \omega_0^2} = \frac{s}{s^2 + \omega_0^2}$$

$$\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{\cos(\omega_0 t) u(t)\} = \frac{s}{s^2 + \omega_0^2}, \quad \text{Re}(s) > 0}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Laplace Transform of the First Derivative (Critical for KTU)

The differentiation property is the workhorse for solving differential equations.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition:**

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{dx(t)}{dt}\right\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{dx(t)}{dt} \, e^{-st} \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Apply integration by parts** with $u = e^{-st}$ and $dv = \frac{dx}{dt} dt$:

$$\int u \, dv = u v - \int v \, du$$

This gives:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{dx}{dt} e^{-st} dt = \left[ x(t) e^{-st} \right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} x(t) (-s e^{-st}) \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the boundary term:**

As $t \to \infty$, $e^{-st} \to 0$ (assuming ROC: $\text{Re}(s) > \sigma_{\max}$), so:

$$\left[ x(t) e^{-st} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - x(0^-) \cdot e^{0} = -x(0^-)$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the integral term:**

$$- \int_{0}^{\infty} x(t) (-s e^{-st}) \, dt = s \int_{0}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-st} \, dt = s X(s)$$

**Step 5 — Combine:**

$$\boxed{\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{dx(t)}{dt}\right\} = sX(s) - x(0^-)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The term $x(0^-)$ is the value of the signal **just before** $t = 0$. This is why the unilateral Laplace transform naturally handles **non-zero initial conditions** in RLC circuits — a major advantage over the Fourier Transform.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Solving a Typical KTU Differential Equation

**Problem:** Solve $y''(t) + 5y'(t) + 6y(t) = x(t)$ with zero initial conditions, given $x(t) = e^{-t} u(t)$.

**Step 1 — Take the unilateral Laplace transform of both sides:**

Using linearity and zero initial conditions ($y(0^-) = 0$, $y'(0^-) = 0$):

$$s^2 Y(s) + 5s Y(s) + 6 Y(s) = \frac{1}{s+1}$$

**Step 2 — Factor the left-hand side (characteristic equation):**

$$(s^2 + 5s + 6) Y(s) = (s+2)(s+3) Y(s) = \frac{1}{s+1}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $Y(s)$ — the Transfer Function Approach:**

$$Y(s) = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE):**

$$Y(s) = \frac{A}{s+1} + \frac{B}{s+2} + \frac{C}{s+3}$$

Using the cover-up method:

$$A = \left. \frac{1}{(s+2)(s+3)} \right|_{s=-1} = \frac{1}{(1)(2)} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$B = \left. \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+3)} \right|_{s=-2} = \frac{1}{(-1)(1)} = -1$$

$$C = \left. \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} \right|_{s=-3} = \frac{1}{(-2)(-1)} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Take the inverse Laplace transform using linearity:**

$$y(t) = \left( \frac{1}{2} e^{-t} - e^{-2t} + \frac{1}{2} e^{-3t} \right) u(t)$$

**Step 6 — Apply Initial and Final Value Theorems as verification:**

$$\lim_{t \to 0^+} y(t) = \frac{1}{2} - 1 + \frac{1}{2} = 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ (matches } y(0^-) = 0\text{)}$$

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} y(t) = 0 \quad \text{(stable system, all poles in LHP)} \quad \checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Complex $s$-Plane — Poles, Zeros, and Stability

The complex $s$-plane is the canvas on which we visualize Laplace-domain behavior. Below is a detailed schematic of the $s$-plane with critical markers used in KTU 2024 problems.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Complex s-plane Diagram"]
    B["Imaginary Axis j omega (Oscillation Frequency)"]
    C["Real Axis sigma (Decay or Growth Rate)"]
    D["Right Half Plane RHP sigma greater than 0 (Unstable Zone)"]
    E["Left Half Plane LHP sigma less than 0 (Stable Zone)"]
    F["Origin s equals 0 (Marginal Stability)"]
    G["Pole at s equals minus 1 Marked with X"]
    H["Pole at s equals minus 3 Marked with X"]
    I["Zero at s equals 2 Marked with O"]
    J["ROC Strip Re s greater than minus 1 Shaded Region"]

    A --- B
    A --- C
    A --- D
    A --- E
    A --- F
    A --- G
    A --- H
    A --- I
    A --- J

    B --- C
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram above: **Pole locations** (marked with **X**) determine natural system responses. **Zeros** (marked with **O**) determine which modes are suppressed. **ROC** (shaded region) must exclude poles.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: LTI System Analysis Using Laplace Transform

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN1["Input x of t"] --> SUM1["Summing Junction"]
    NOISE["Noise n of t"] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> SYS["LTI System H of s"]
    SYS --> OUT1["Output y of t"]
    OUT1 --> FB1["Feedback H fb of s"]
    FB1 --> SUM1
```

**Description of the Block Diagram:**
- **Input $x(t)$:** External excitation signal (causal, e.g., a step or impulse).
- **Summing Junction:** Combines input and feedback (with sign for negative feedback).
- **LTI System $H(s)$:** Linear Time-Invariant subsystem characterized by its **Transfer Function** $H(s) = Y(s)/X(s)$.
- **Output $y(t)$:** System response.
- **Feedback $H_{fb}(s)$:** Returns a portion of the output (used in control systems).

## 4.3 Workflow Diagram: Solving a Laplace Transform Problem (KTU Exam Strategy)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["Step 1 Identify the Input x of t and Output y of t"] --> P2["Step 2 Take Laplace Transform of Both Sides of the Equation"]
    P2 --> P3["Step 3 Apply Differentiation and Integration Properties Include Initial Conditions"]
    P3 --> P4["Step 4 Rearrange to Solve for Y of s"]
    P4 --> P5["Step 5 Perform Partial Fraction Expansion PFE of Y of s"]
    P5 --> P6["Step 6 Apply Inverse Laplace Transform to Obtain y of t"]
    P6 --> P7["Step 7 Verify Using IVT and FVT Theorems"]

    P1 -.-> P2
    P2 -.-> P3
    P3 -.-> P4
    P4 -.-> P5
    P5 -.-> P6
    P6 -.-> P7
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define the bilateral and unilateral Laplace transforms. State the condition for the existence of the Laplace transform.** **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The **bilateral Laplace Transform** of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is defined as:

$$X(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) \, e^{-st} \, dt, \quad s = \sigma + j\omega$$

The **unilateral Laplace Transform** is defined as:

$$X(s) = \int_{0^-}^{+\infty} x(t) \, e^{-st} \, dt$$

The Laplace transform exists (converges) only for those values of $s$ where the integral yields a finite value. This set of values forms the **Region of Convergence (ROC)**. A sufficient condition is that $x(t)$ is of exponential order, i.e., there exist finite constants $M$ and $a$ such that $\vert x(t) \vert \leq M e^{at}$ for all $t \geq 0$, in which case the ROC is $\text{Re}(s) > a$. **[3 Marks]**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**Q2. What is the Region of Convergence (ROC)? Why is the ROC important in the Laplace transform?** **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Region of Convergence (ROC)** is the set of all complex values $s = \sigma + j\omega$ in the complex $s$-plane for which the Laplace transform integral converges to a finite value.

**Importance of ROC:**
1. **Uniqueness:** Two different signals can have identical $X(s)$ expressions but different ROCs. The ROC uniquely determines which time-domain signal corresponds to $X(s)$.
2. **Causality:** For a causal (right-sided) signal, the ROC is to the right of the rightmost pole.
3. **Stability:** A causal LTI system is **BIBO stable** if and only if its ROC includes the $j\omega$-axis ($\text{Re}(s) = 0$).
4. **Pole-Zero Insight:** The ROC is bounded by the poles of $X(s)$ and never contains them. **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Find the Laplace transform of the following signals and sketch their ROCs: **[7 Marks]**
1. $x_1(t) = e^{-3t} u(t)$
2. $x_2(t) = e^{2t} u(-t)$
3. $x_3(t) = e^{-3t} u(t) + e^{2t} u(-t)$

**(b)** Using the Initial and Final Value Theorems, determine the initial and final values of $x(t)$ whose Laplace transform is: **[7 Marks]**

$$X(s) = \frac{10(s+2)}{s(s+1)(s+3)}$$

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Signal 1:** $x_1(t) = e^{-3t} u(t)$ is a right-sided decaying exponential.

Using the standard pair $\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at} u(t)\} = \frac{1}{s+a}$ with $a = 3$:

$$X_1(s) = \frac{1}{s+3}, \quad \text{ROC: } \text{Re}(s) > -3$$

**Signal 2:** $x_2(t) = e^{2t} u(-t)$ is a left-sided growing exponential.

The bilateral Laplace transform gives:

$$X_2(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{0} e^{2t} e^{-st} \, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{0} e^{(2-s)t} \, dt = \left[ \frac{e^{(2-s)t}}{2-s} \right]_{-\infty}^{0}$$

For convergence as $t \to -\infty$, we need $\text{Re}(2-s) > 0 \implies \text{Re}(s) < 2$.

$$X_2(s) = \frac{1}{2-s} = \frac{-1}{s-2}, \quad \text{ROC: } \text{Re}(s) < 2$$

**Signal 3:** $x_3(t) = e^{-3t} u(t) + e^{2t} u(-t)$ is a two-sided signal.

$$X_3(s) = X_1(s) + X_2(s) = \frac{1}{s+3} - \frac{1}{s-2}$$

ROC is the intersection: $-3 < \text{Re}(s) < 2$. This is a vertical strip in the $s$-plane.

**ROC Sketch:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    SK["ROC Strip Visualisation"]
    AX["Real Axis sigma"]
    L1["Boundary sigma equals minus 3 (Pole of X1)"]
    L2["Boundary sigma equals 2 (Pole of X2)"]
    SH["Shaded ROC Strip minus 3 less than Re s less than 2"]
    IM["Imaginary Axis j omega"]

    SK --> AX
    SK --> L1
    SK --> L2
    SK --> SH
    SK --> IM
```

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the poles of $X(s)$:**

$$X(s) = \frac{10(s+2)}{s(s+1)(s+3)}$$

Poles are at $s = 0$, $s = -1$, $s = -3$. All poles are in the LHP (or on the $j\omega$-axis at $s=0$).

**Step 2 — Apply the Initial Value Theorem (IVT):**

$$x(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} s X(s) = \lim_{s \to \infty} \frac{10s(s+2)}{s(s+1)(s+3)} = \lim_{s \to \infty} \frac{10(s+2)}{(s+1)(s+3)}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $s^2$:

$$x(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} \frac{10(1 + 2/s)}{(1 + 1/s)(1 + 3/s)} = \frac{10 \cdot 1}{1 \cdot 1} = 10$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Final Value Theorem (FVT):**

Check FVT validity: Poles of $sX(s) = \frac{10(s+2)}{(s+1)(s+3)}$ are at $s = -1$ and $s = -3$, both strictly in LHP. The pole at $s = 0$ cancels with the $s$-multiplier. **FVT is valid.**

$$x(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} s X(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{10s(s+2)}{s(s+1)(s+3)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{10(s+2)}{(s+1)(s+3)}$$

$$x(\infty) = \frac{10(0+2)}{(0+1)(0+3)} = \frac{20}{3} \approx 6.67$$

**Final Answers:** $x(0^+) = 10$ and $x(\infty) = \dfrac{20}{3}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Determine the inverse Laplace transform of: **[7 Marks]**

$$Y(s) = \frac{2s + 5}{s^2 + 4s + 13}$$

**(b)** Solve the differential equation using Laplace transforms: **[7 Marks]**

$$\frac{d^2 y(t)}{dt^2} + 4 \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + 3y(t) = e^{-2t} u(t), \quad y(0^-) = 1, \quad y'(0^-) = 0$$

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Step 1 — Complete the square in the denominator:**

$$s^2 + 4s + 13 = (s+2)^2 + 9 = (s+2)^2 + 3^2$$

**Step 2 — Rewrite the numerator in terms of $(s+2)$:**

$$2s + 5 = 2(s+2) + 1$$

So:

$$Y(s) = \frac{2(s+2) + 1}{(s+2)^2 + 3^2} = 2 \cdot \frac{s+2}{(s+2)^2 + 3^2} + \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{(s+2)^2 + 3^2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the inverse Laplace transform using standard pairs:**

Using $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\frac{s+a}{(s+a)^2 + \omega_0^2}\right\} = e^{-at} \cos(\omega_0 t) u(t)$ and $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\frac{\omega_0}{(s+a)^2 + \omega_0^2}\right\} = e^{-at} \sin(\omega_0 t) u(t)$ with $a = 2$ and $\omega_0 = 3$:

$$y(t) = 2 e^{-2t} \cos(3t) u(t) + \frac{1}{3} e^{-2t} \sin(3t) u(t)$$

$$\boxed{y(t) = e^{-2t} \left[ 2 \cos(3t) + \frac{1}{3} \sin(3t) \right] u(t)}$$

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Step 1 — Take the unilateral Laplace transform of both sides:**

Using the differentiation property:

$$\mathcal{L}\{y''(t)\} = s^2 Y(s) - s y(0^-) - y'(0^-) = s^2 Y(s) - s$$

$$\mathcal{L}\{y'(t)\} = s Y(s) - y(0^-) = s Y(s) - 1$$

$$\mathcal{L}\{y(t)\} = Y(s)$$

$$\mathcal{L}\{e^{-2t} u(t)\} = \frac{1}{s+2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the differential equation:**

$$[s^2 Y(s) - s] + 4[s Y(s) - 1] + 3 Y(s) = \frac{1}{s+2}$$

**Step 3 — Collect $Y(s)$ terms:**

$$(s^2 + 4s + 3) Y(s) - s - 4 = \frac{1}{s+2}$$

$$(s+1)(s+3) Y(s) = \frac{1}{s+2} + s + 4$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the right-hand side:**

$$\frac{1}{s+2} + s + 4 = \frac{1 + (s+4)(s+2)}{s+2} = \frac{1 + s^2 + 6s + 8}{s+2} = \frac{s^2 + 6s + 9}{s+2} = \frac{(s+3)^2}{s+2}$$

Therefore:

$$Y(s) = \frac{(s+3)^2}{(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{s+3}{(s+1)(s+2)}$$

**Step 5 — Partial Fraction Expansion:**

$$Y(s) = \frac{s+3}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{A}{s+1} + \frac{B}{s+2}$$

$$A = \left. \frac{s+3}{s+2} \right|_{s=-1} = \frac{2}{1} = 2$$

$$B = \left. \frac{s+3}{s+1} \right|_{s=-2} = \frac{1}{-1} = -1$$

**Step 6 — Inverse Laplace transform:**

$$y(t) = (2e^{-t} - e^{-2t}) u(t)$$

**Step 7 — Verification of Initial Conditions:**

$y(0^+) = 2 - 1 = 1 \quad \checkmark$ (matches $y(0^-) = 1$)

$y'(t) = (-2e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t}) u(t)$, so $y'(0^+) = -2 + 2 = 0 \quad \checkmark$ (matches $y'(0^-) = 0$)

$$\boxed{y(t) = (2e^{-t} - e^{-2t}) u(t)}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting $u(t)$:** Always include the unit step $u(t)$ in the final time-domain answer for unilateral Laplace problems. **(−1 mark)**
> 2. **Incorrect ROC for bilateral transforms:** Students frequently write ROC as the entire $s$-plane for left-sided signals. The ROC is the **left-half plane to the left of the leftmost pole**. **(−2 marks)**
> 3. **Invalid FVT application:** Applying the Final Value Theorem when $sX(s)$ has poles on the $j\omega$-axis (e.g., undamped sinusoids) or in the RHP. Always check pole locations **first**. **(−2 marks)**
> 4. **Sign errors in IVT/FVT limits:** Use $s \to \infty$ for IVT and $s \to 0$ for FVT — never reverse these. **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **Skipping initial conditions:** In the differentiation property, omitting $-x(0^-)$ or $-x'(0^-)$ is a **fatal error** in differential equation problems. **(−3 marks)**
> 6. **Partial fraction errors:** Wrong cover-up method application. Always substitute the pole value into the **remaining factors only**, not the pole's own factor. **(−2 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ **Bilateral Laplace Transform:** $X(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-st} dt$; **Unilateral:** $X(s) = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-st} dt$.
- ✅ **ROC is mandatory** for bilateral transforms and is a strip (or half-plane) **excluding all poles**.
- ✅ **Poles** are roots of the denominator (where $X(s) \to \infty$); **Zeros** are roots of the numerator.
- ✅ **Stability Criterion:** A causal LTI system is BIBO stable **iff** all poles lie in the **LHP** (ROC includes $j\omega$-axis).
- ✅ **Differentiation Property:** $\mathcal{L}\{x'(t)\} = sX(s) - x(0^-)$ — includes the initial condition.
- ✅ **Integration Property:** $\mathcal{L}\left\{\int_{0^-}^{t} x(\tau) d\tau\right\} = \dfrac{X(s)}{s}$.
- ✅ **Convolution Property:** $\mathcal{L}\{x_1(t) * x_2(t)\} = X_1(s) X_2(s)$ — basis for system transfer functions.
- ✅ **Time Shift:** $\mathcal{L}\{x(t - t_0) u(t-t_0)\} = e^{-st_0} X(s)$, valid for $t_0 \geq 0$.
- ✅ **Frequency Shift:** $\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at} x(t)\} = X(s+a)$ — shift poles left by $a$ for decay.
- ✅ **IVT:** $x(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} s X(s)$.
- ✅ **FVT:** $x(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} s X(s)$, valid **only** if poles of $sX(s)$ are in LHP.
- ✅ **Standard Pairs:** Impulse $= 1$, Step $= 1/s$, Exponential $= 1/(s+a)$, Cosine $= s/(s^2+\omega_0^2)$, Sine $= \omega_0/(s^2+\omega_0^2)$.
- ✅ **Inverse Laplace via PFE:** Always factor the denominator, find poles, and use the cover-up method.
- ✅ **Completing the Square:** Essential for inverse transforms involving $s^2 + 2as + (a^2 + \omega_0^2)$.
- ✅ **Initial Conditions in DEs:** Always carry $x(0^-)$ and $x'(0^-)$ through the Laplace transform — never drop them.
- ✅ **Causality Test:** A signal is causal iff its ROC is of the form $\text{Re}(s) > \sigma_{\max}$.
- ✅ **Final Solution Format:** Always express $y(t)$ with $u(t)$ for causal/unilateral problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Region of Convergence (ROC): Properties, Stability, and Causality Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT403 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Region of Convergence (ROC): Properties, Stability, and Causality Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Region of Convergence (ROC)

The **Region of Convergence (ROC)** of a Z-transform is the set of all complex values of the variable $z$ (in the complex $z$-plane) for which the Z-transform summation **converges to a finite value**. Mathematically, for a discrete-time signal $x[n]$, the Z-transform is defined as:

$$
X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, z^{-n}
$$

The ROC is formally the locus:

$$
\text{ROC} = \left\{ z \in \mathbb{C} \;:\; \left\vert \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, z^{-n} \right\vert < \infty \right\}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A signal **does not have a unique Z-transform representation** without its ROC. Two entirely different signals can share the same $X(z)$ algebraic expression but possess different ROCs. Therefore, in KTU board exam answers, **always specify the ROC** when expressing $X(z)$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Telescope" of Convergence

Imagine standing on the surface of a vast circular lake and tossing pebbles. The ripple (the Z-transform sum) is only well-behaved — finite, bounded, and non-explosive — in specific annular zones of the lake. These calm zones are the **ROC**. Inside the smallest ripple zone (near the origin) or beyond the largest zone (out toward infinity), the water becomes "unstable" (the series diverges to infinity). 

Just as a calm swimmer can only tread water in certain circular bands, the infinite series $\sum x[n] z^{-n}$ only "treads" safely within the ROC. **Poles are the dangerous whirlpools** — the series diverges at every pole, so poles always lie on the **boundary** of the ROC, never strictly inside it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key physical metric:** The ROC is always a **ring-shaped (annular) region** in the $z$-plane, bounded on the inside and/or outside by the locations of the poles of $X(z)$. It can degenerate into:
> - An **exterior of a circle** ($ \vert z \vert > r_{2}$ ) → right-sided/causal signals
> - An **interior of a circle** ($ \vert z \vert < r_{1}$ ) → left-sided/anti-causal signals
> - The **entire z-plane** (excluding $z=0$ or $z=\infty$) → finite-duration two-sided signals
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need the ROC? (The "Why" Behind It)

The Z-transform is an **infinite series**. Not every infinite series converges. For a series $\sum a_n$ to be finite, the terms $a_n$ must shrink to zero fast enough. In the discrete-time case, the term $x[n] z^{-n}$ shrinks only when $z$ balances the growth of $x[n]$. The ROC is precisely the set of $z$ values that perform this balancing act.

## 2.2 Fundamental Properties of the ROC (The "How" Behind It)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These six properties are **favourite KTU Part-A questions**. Memorize them as a numbered list.

1. **The ROC is a ring (annulus) in the z-plane**, defined by $r_2 < \vert z \vert < r_1$, where $0 \le r_2 < r_1 \le \infty$. (Any $r_1$ or $r_2$ can be 0 or $\infty$.)

2. **The ROC does not contain any poles** of $X(z)$. Poles mark the boundaries of the ROC.

3. **If $x[n]$ is a finite-duration right-sided sequence**, the ROC is the entire $z$-plane **except possibly $z = 0$**.

4. **If $x[n]$ is a finite-duration left-sided sequence**, the ROC is the entire $z$-plane **except possibly $z = \infty$**.

5. **If $x[n]$ is a finite-duration two-sided sequence**, the ROC is the **entire z-plane** (excluding $z=0$ and $z=\infty$ if both are problematic).

6. **The ROC must be a connected region.** It cannot be split into two disjoint pieces.

7. **If $X(z)$ is rational** (i.e., a ratio of polynomials $N(z)/D(z)$), the ROC is bounded by — but does not include — the poles. The inner radius equals the magnitude of the **innermost pole**, and the outer radius equals the magnitude of the **outermost pole** (or $\infty$ if extending outward).

## 2.3 Stability and Causality Conditions — The Heart of the Topic

A Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system is characterized completely by its **system function**:

$$
H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)}
$$

The location of the ROC of $H(z)$ dictates the **physical character** of the system.

### A. Causality Condition

A discrete-time LTI system is **causal** if and only if the ROC of $H(z)$ is the **exterior of a circle** that includes the point $z = \infty$:

$$
\text{ROC} = \left\{ z : \vert z \vert > r_{\max} \right\} \quad \text{(causal)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** A causal system's impulse response $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$. Hence the Z-transform is one-sided, and the series converges only when $\vert z \vert$ is large enough to damp the negative powers.

### B. Stability Condition (BIBO)

A discrete-time LTI system is **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stable** if and only if the ROC of $H(z)$ **includes the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$:

$$
1 \in \text{ROC} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty
$$

### C. Joint Condition — Stable AND Causal

The most-asked combined condition in KTU exams:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A system is **both stable and causal** if and only if **all poles of $H(z)$ lie strictly inside the unit circle**, and the ROC is $ \vert z \vert > r_{\max}$ where $r_{\max} < 1$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Property / Condition | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | General ROC form | $r_2 < \vert z \vert < r_1$ | Annular region between two radii |
| 2 | ROC of causal signal $x[n]=0,\; n<0$ | $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$ | Pole of largest magnitude |
| 3 | ROC of anti-causal signal $x[n]=0,\; n>0$ | $\vert z \vert < r_{\min}$ | Pole of smallest magnitude |
| 4 | ROC of two-sided signal | $r_2 < \vert z \vert < r_1$ | Between two pole magnitudes |
| 5 | Causal LTI system condition | ROC is $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$ | Includes $\infty$ |
| 6 | Stable LTI system condition | $\vert z \vert = 1$ inside ROC | BIBO stability |
| 7 | Stable + Causal LTI system | All poles satisfy $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ | Inside unit circle |
| 8 | Marginally stable | Simple poles on $\vert z \vert=1$, rest inside | Oscillatory, not decaying |
| 9 | Unstable | Any pole with $\vert p_k \vert > 1$, or repeated pole on unit circle | Output grows unbounded |
| 10 | Finite-energy signal ROC | Includes $\vert z \vert = 1$ | Absolutely summable |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital filter design:** The ROC and pole-location criteria directly determine whether a designed filter (FIR/IIR) will be **causally realizable** in a DSP chip. Stability ($\vert p_k \vert < 1$) is the **non-negotiable rule** in real-time audio/ECG processing.
- **Control systems:** Poles of the closed-loop transfer function must remain inside the unit circle (discrete) or left-half $s$-plane (continuous) for the system not to self-destruct.
- **Communications:** Equalizers and adaptive filters in 4G/5G receivers rely on ROC analysis to guarantee no overflow and bounded channel output.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why ROC Determines Causality

Consider a general right-sided sequence $x[n] = a^n \, u[n]$ where $u[n]$ is the unit step. Its Z-transform is:

$$
X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^n z^{-n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left( a z^{-1} \right)^n
$$

This is a **geometric series**. A geometric series $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^n$ converges if and only if $\vert r \vert < 1$. Substituting $r = a z^{-1}$:

$$
\left\vert a z^{-1} \right\vert < 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \frac{\vert a \vert}{\vert z \vert} < 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert
$$

So the ROC is the exterior of a circle of radius $\vert a \vert$. Since $a$ is also the pole location, this is precisely $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$. For a general causal $h[n]$, the same logic generalizes: the ROC is always the **exterior of the outermost pole**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step interpretation:** The factor $z^{-n}$ acts as a built-in "growth suppressor" for positive $n$ when $\vert z \vert > 1$. This is the mathematical reason causal systems have an *exterior* ROC.

## 3.2 Derivation: Why the Unit Circle Test Works for Stability

Take an arbitrary LTI system with impulse response $h[n]$. The output to a bounded input $\vert x[n] \vert \le M$ is, by convolution:

$$
y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h[k] \, x[n-k]
$$

Applying the triangle inequality:

$$
\vert y[n] \vert \le M \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[k] \vert
$$

For $y[n]$ to remain bounded for **every** bounded $x[n]$, the impulse response must be **absolutely summable**:

$$
\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[k] \vert < \infty
$$

Setting $z = e^{j\omega}$ (on the unit circle, $\vert z \vert = 1$) in the Z-transform:

$$
H(z) \bigg\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}} = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h[k] \, e^{-j\omega k}
$$

Taking the magnitude:

$$
\left\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \right\vert \le \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[k] \vert
$$

Therefore, **absolute summability of $h[n]$ is equivalent to $H(z)$ being finite on the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$. This is why the ROC must include $\vert z \vert = 1$ for stability.

## 3.3 Derivation: Pole-Location Criterion for Stable + Causal Systems

Assume $H(z)$ is rational with $M$ poles $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_M$ and let $r_{\max} = \max_k \vert p_k \vert$. For causality:

$$
\text{ROC}_{\text{causal}} = \{ z : \vert z \vert > r_{\max} \}
$$

For stability we additionally need the unit circle inside the ROC:

$$
1 \in \text{ROC}_{\text{causal}} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad 1 > r_{\max} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad r_{\max} < 1
$$

Since $r_{\max} = \max_k \vert p_k \vert$, this means **every** pole must satisfy $\vert p_k \vert < 1$. The proof is complete.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference Table of ROCs

| Signal Type | Time Support | ROC in $z$-plane | Causal? | Stable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $a^n u[n]$ | $n \ge 0$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ | ✅ Yes | ✅ only if $\vert a \vert < 1$ |
| $-a^n u[-n-1]$ | $n < 0$ | $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$ | ❌ Anti-causal | ✅ only if $\vert a \vert > 1$ |
| $\delta[n]$ | $n = 0$ | All $z$ | ✅ | ✅ |
| $u[n]$ | $n \ge 0$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ | ✅ | ❌ (marginal) |
| $a^{\vert n \vert}$ | all $n$ | $\vert a \vert < \vert z \vert < 1/\vert a \vert$ | ❌ Two-sided | ✅ only if $\vert a \vert < 1$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ROC Geometry for Different Signal Classes

The diagram below illustrates the three classical ROC shapes that appear in KTU exam problems. Each shaded annular region represents a valid convergence zone, and the crosses ($\times$) mark the pole locations that bound the ROC.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Discrete-Time Signal x n"] --> B{"Finite or Infinite Duration?"}
    B -- "Right-Sided" --> C["ROC: Exterior of Circle"]
    B -- "Left-Sided" --> D["ROC: Interior of Circle"]
    B -- "Two-Sided" --> E["ROC: Annular Ring"]

    C --> F["Pole p1, p2 ... outside ROC boundary"]
    D --> G["Pole p1, p2 ... outside ROC boundary"]
    E --> H["Poles bracket the ROC on both sides"]

    C --> I{"Includes unit circle?"}
    I -- "Yes" --> J["Stable Causal System"]
    I -- "No" --> K["Unstable Causal System"]

    D --> L{"Includes unit circle?"}
    L -- "Yes" --> M["Stable Anti-Causal System"]
    L -- "No" --> N["Unstable Anti-Causal System"]

    E --> O{"Includes unit circle?"}
    O -- "Yes" --> P["Stable Two-Sided System"]
    O -- "No" --> Q["Unstable Two-Sided System"]
```

## 4.2 Pole-Zero Plot with ROC for a Typical KTU Problem

Consider a causal system:

$$
H(z) = \frac{z}{(z-0.5)(z-0.8)}
$$

The poles are at $z = 0.5$ and $z = 0.8$. The outermost pole magnitude is $0.8$. The following block diagram shows the corresponding pole-zero geometry with the ROC shaded.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ZPLANE["Complex z-Plane"]
        ORIGIN["Origin z equals 0"]
        POLE1["Pole p1 at 0.5"]
        POLE2["Pole p2 at 0.8"]
        UC["Unit Circle radius 1"]
    end

    POLE1 --- POLE2
    POLE1 --> R1["Inner ROC boundary radius 0.5"]
    POLE2 --> R2["Outer ROC boundary radius 0.8"]
    UC --> STABLE["Unit circle is OUTSIDE ROC: Unstable"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the chart:** Because the ROC ($ \vert z \vert > 0.8 $) does **not** include the unit circle ($ \vert z \vert = 1$), this causal system is **unstable**. To make it stable, we would need both poles to have magnitudes less than 1, i.e., the ROC $ \vert z \vert > r_{\max} $ must extend beyond $\vert z \vert = 1$.

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart for KTU Exam Questions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Given H z, find system character"] --> Q2{"Is ROC exterior of circle?"}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> Q3["Causal"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> Q4["Non-Causal Anti-Causal or Two-Sided"]
    Q3 --> Q5{"Does ROC include unit circle?"}
    Q5 -- "Yes" --> Q6["Stable and Causal: all poles inside unit circle"]
    Q5 -- "No" --> Q7["Unstable Causal: at least one pole outside unit circle"]
    Q4 --> Q8{"Does ROC include unit circle?"}
    Q8 -- "Yes" --> Q9["Stable but Non-Causal"]
    Q8 -- "No" --> Q10["Unstable and Non-Causal"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2, Q1]**
> **Q1.** Define the Region of Convergence (ROC) of a Z-transform. Why is it necessary to specify the ROC along with $X(z)$?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The ROC of the Z-transform $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$ is the set of all values of $z$ in the complex plane for which the summation converges to a finite value **(1 mark)**. It is necessary to specify the ROC because two different sequences can have identical $X(z)$ expressions but different ROCs, making the ROC essential for unique signal representation **(1 mark)**. The ROC also determines whether the system is stable (unit circle inside ROC) and causal (exterior of a circle) **(1 mark)**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, Q2]**
> **Q2.** State any three properties of the Region of Convergence of the Z-transform of a discrete-time signal.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The ROC is a ring-shaped region in the $z$-plane of the form $r_2 < \vert z \vert < r_1$ **(1 mark)**.
2. The ROC does not contain any poles of $X(z)$ **(1 mark)**.
3. If $x[n]$ is a right-sided causal sequence, the ROC is the exterior of a circle $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$ **(1 mark)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice Provided)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2, Q3]**
> 
> ### Question A (14 Marks)
> 
> **(a)** For the Z-transform $X(z) = \dfrac{z}{(z-0.5)(z-2)}$, determine all possible ROCs. For each ROC, identify whether the corresponding signal is causal, anti-causal, or two-sided, and whether it is stable. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A causal LTI system is described by $H(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 - 1.5 z^{-1} + 0.5 z^{-2}}$. Determine whether the system is stable. Justify your answer using pole locations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the poles.** The denominator is $(z-0.5)(z-2)$, so the poles are:

$$
p_1 = 0.5, \qquad p_2 = 2
$$

with magnitudes $\vert p_1 \vert = 0.5$ and $\vert p_2 \vert = 2$.

**Step 2 — List the three possible ROCs.** For a rational $X(z)$ with two real poles, the ROC can be:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROC}_1 &: \vert z \vert < 0.5 \\
\text{ROC}_2 &: 0.5 < \vert z \vert < 2 \\
\text{ROC}_3 &: \vert z \vert > 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Classify each ROC.**

| ROC | Signal Type | Includes $\vert z \vert = 1$? | Stable? |
|-----|-------------|-------------------------------|---------|
| $\vert z \vert < 0.5$ | Anti-causal (left-sided) | No | No |
| $0.5 < \vert z \vert < 2$ | Two-sided | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| $\vert z \vert > 2$ | Causal (right-sided) | No | No |

**Step 4 — Conclusion.** Only ROC$_2$ corresponds to a stable system, and the corresponding sequence is two-sided (non-causal). The causal ROC does not yield a stable system. **(1 mark for tabular conclusion, 1 mark for stating the reason)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert to positive powers of $z$.** Multiply numerator and denominator by $z^2$:

$$
H(z) = \frac{z^2}{z^2 - 1.5 z + 0.5}
$$

**Step 2 — Factor the denominator.** Solving $z^2 - 1.5z + 0.5 = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z &= \frac{1.5 \pm \sqrt{2.25 - 2}}{2} = \frac{1.5 \pm 0.5}{2} \\
z &= 1.0 \quad \text{or} \quad z = 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

So the poles are $p_1 = 1.0$ and $p_2 = 0.5$ **(2 marks)**.

**Step 3 — Determine the ROC for a causal system.** Since the system is causal, the ROC is the exterior of the outermost pole:

$$
\text{ROC} = \{ z : \vert z \vert > 1.0 \}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the stability test.** The unit circle is $\vert z \vert = 1$. The ROC begins at $\vert z \vert = 1.0$ but **does not strictly include** it. Even if we are lenient and consider $\vert z \vert = 1$ as included, the pole at $z = 1$ is **on** the unit circle, which corresponds to **marginal stability**, not asymptotic stability. **(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Conclusion.** The system is **NOT strictly stable** because the pole at $z=1$ lies exactly on the unit circle. For strict BIBO stability of a causal LTI system, **all poles must lie strictly inside the unit circle** $\vert p_k \vert < 1$. **(1 mark)**

---

> ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
> 
> **(a)** Explain the conditions under which an LTI discrete-time system is (i) causal and (ii) stable, in terms of the ROC of its system function $H(z)$. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A discrete-time LTI system has impulse response $h[n] = (0.8)^n u[n] - (1.2)^n u[-n-1]$. Determine whether the system is causal, anti-causal, two-sided, stable, and unstable. Justify each conclusion. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Causality (3 marks):** A discrete-time LTI system is causal if and only if its impulse response satisfies $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$. In the $z$-domain, this translates to a **right-sided (one-sided) Z-transform**, whose ROC is the **exterior of a circle**:

$$
\text{ROC}_{\text{causal}} = \{ z : \vert z \vert > r_{\max} \}, \quad \text{where } r_{\max} = \max_k \vert p_k \vert
$$

Equivalently, the ROC must include the point $z = \infty$ **(1 mark for condition, 1 mark for ROC form, 1 mark for the role of poles)**.

**Stability (4 marks):** An LTI system is BIBO stable if and only if its impulse response is absolutely summable:

$$
\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty
$$

This is equivalent to the **Z-transform $H(z)$ converging on the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$. Hence, the condition is:

$$
\text{ROC must include the unit circle } \{ z : \vert z \vert = 1 \}
$$

Combining both:

| System Property | ROC Condition | Pole Condition (for causal $H(z)$) |
|-----------------|---------------|-------------------------------------|
| Causal | $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$ | Any pole positions |
| Stable | $\vert z \vert = 1 \in \text{ROC}$ | At least one pole not at $\vert p_k \vert > 1$ |
| Stable AND Causal | $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$ AND $\vert z \vert = 1 \in$ ROC | All $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ |

**(1 mark for the joint condition table and concluding remark)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Decompose the impulse response.** The signal has two components:

$$
h_1[n] = (0.8)^n u[n] \quad \text{(right-sided, starting at } n = 0\text{)}
$$

$$
h_2[n] = -(1.2)^n u[-n-1] \quad \text{(left-sided, extending to } n = -\infty\text{)}
$$

**Step 2 — Find individual Z-transforms and ROCs.**

For $h_1[n] = (0.8)^n u[n]$:

$$
H_1(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC}_1: \vert z \vert > 0.8
$$

For $h_2[n] = -(1.2)^n u[-n-1]$:

$$
H_2(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 1.2 z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC}_2: \vert z \vert < 1.2
$$

**Step 3 — Combined system function and ROC.** The total ROC is the **intersection**:

$$
\text{ROC} = \text{ROC}_1 \cap \text{ROC}_2 = \{ z : 0.8 < \vert z \vert < 1.2 \}
$$

**Step 4 — Classify the system.** Since the ROC is an annulus (bounded on both sides), $h[n]$ is **two-sided (non-causal, non-anti-causal)**. **(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Test stability.** The unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ satisfies $0.8 < 1 < 1.2$, so the unit circle lies inside the ROC. Therefore the system is **stable**. **(1 mark)**

**Step 6 — Pole positions for completeness.** Poles are at $z = 0.8$ (inside unit circle) and $z = 1.2$ (outside unit circle). Stability does not require all poles to be inside the unit circle for non-causal systems; it only requires the ROC (a ring) to enclose the unit circle. **(1 mark)**

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks on ROC questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the ROC when writing $X(z)$:** Many students write the algebraic expression of $X(z)$ but omit the ROC. The ROC is a **mandatory part** of a complete Z-transform specification, and a missing ROC usually costs **2 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 
> 2. **Confusing "exterior" and "interior" ROC:** Students often write $\vert z \vert < 1$ when they mean $\vert z \vert > 1$. Remember: **causal $\Rightarrow$ exterior**, **anti-causal $\Rightarrow$ interior**.
> 
> 3. **Stability + Causality confusion:** A causal system is NOT automatically stable. Causal + Stable requires **all poles strictly inside the unit circle**. Many students write "causal so stable" — this is incorrect and costs 1–2 marks.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting to take the magnitude of complex poles:** If poles are complex, compute $\vert p \vert = \sqrt{\text{Re}^2 + \text{Im}^2}$ before comparing with 1. Writing $p = 0.5 + 0.5j$ and concluding "$\vert p \vert < 1$" without computing $\sqrt{0.5} \approx 0.707$ is treated as incomplete.
> 
> 5. **Misidentifying the outermost pole:** When $X(z)$ has poles at $0.5$ and $0.8$, the outermost is $0.8$ (the larger magnitude), and the ROC is $\vert z \vert > 0.8$, **not** $\vert z \vert > 0.5$.

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ROC is a ring** $r_2 < \vert z \vert < r_1$ bounded by pole magnitudes; **poles never lie inside** the ROC.
- **Causal signal/system** $\Longleftrightarrow$ ROC is **exterior of a circle** ($\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$).
- **Anti-causal signal/system** $\Longleftrightarrow$ ROC is **interior of a circle** ($\vert z \vert < r_{\min}$).
- **Two-sided signal/system** $\Longleftrightarrow$ ROC is an **annulus** ($r_{\min} < \vert z \vert < r_{\max}$).
- **BIBO Stability** $\Longleftrightarrow$ ROC **includes the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$.
- **Stable + Causal** $\Longleftrightarrow$ ROC is $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$ **and** $r_{\max} < 1$ (i.e., **all poles strictly inside the unit circle**).
- **ROC must be connected** — disjoint regions are impossible.
- For a finite-duration sequence, the ROC is the entire $z$-plane except possibly $z = 0$ or $z = \infty$.
- The ROC of a **multiplication** in time is the **intersection** of individual ROCs.
- The ROC of a **convolution** in time is the **intersection** of individual ROCs (with a possible widening to a connected region).
- **Always specify the ROC** when writing $X(z)$ in KTU exams — it is not optional.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relation between Fourier and Laplace Transforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT403 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Relation between Fourier and Laplace Transforms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Fourier Transform** and the **Laplace Transform** are two of the most fundamental transform tools in signals and systems analysis, and they are deeply interconnected. The **Fourier Transform (FT)** decomposes a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ into its frequency components, while the **Bilateral Laplace Transform (LT)** generalizes this idea by introducing a complex frequency variable $s = \sigma + j\omega$.

The formal relationship can be stated as:

> The **Fourier Transform** of a signal $x(t)$ is a special case of the **Bilateral Laplace Transform** evaluated on the imaginary axis of the complex $s$-plane, i.e., at $s = j\omega$ (i.e., when the real part $\sigma = 0$), provided the Region of Convergence (ROC) of the Laplace Transform includes the $j\omega$-axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a high-yield Module 2 question. Expect direct 3-mark questions asking the relationship, and 14-mark derivation-based questions that interlink the two transform definitions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **musical orchestra recording**:
- The **Laplace Transform** is like analyzing the orchestra's sound under a *damping environment* (concert hall with soft walls absorbing some frequencies). The parameter $\sigma$ controls how much the signal is "damped" before analysis.
- The **Fourier Transform** is like analyzing the orchestra's sound in a *perfect, undamped open field* ($\sigma = 0$). You get the pure frequency content without any exponential weighting.

So, **setting $\sigma = 0$ in the Laplace Transform gives you the Fourier Transform** — like turning off the damping knob to get raw frequencies. This is the heart of the relationship.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Complex frequency variable:** $s = \sigma + j\omega$, with units of **radians/second (rad/s)**.
- The Fourier frequency variable $\omega$ is measured in **rad/s**, while the Laplace damping factor $\sigma$ has the same unit (rad/s).
- The **Region of Convergence (ROC)** is a region in the complex $s$-plane.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definitions Box:**
> - **Fourier Transform:** $X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$
> - **Bilateral Laplace Transform:** $X(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-st} dt$, where $s = \sigma + j\omega$
> - **Relation:** $X(\omega) = X(s)\big\vert_{s=j\omega}$ *(only if $j\omega$ lies inside the ROC)*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Relationship

The relationship between the Fourier and Laplace Transforms can be understood through the following structured logic:

- **Step 1 — Start with the Bilateral Laplace Transform:**
  The bilateral Laplace Transform is defined as a frequency-domain representation with a complex exponential kernel $e^{-st}$.

  $$X(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-st} dt$$

- **Step 2 — Substitute the Complex Variable:**
  The complex frequency $s$ has a real part $\sigma$ (damping/attenuation) and an imaginary part $j\omega$ (oscillation).

  $$s = \sigma + j\omega$$

  Therefore:

  $$X(\sigma + j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-(\sigma + j\omega)t} dt$$

- **Step 3 — Split the Exponential:**
  Using the exponential property $e^{(a+b)} = e^a \cdot e^b$:

  $$X(\sigma + j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[x(t) e^{-\sigma t}\right] e^{-j\omega t} dt$$

- **Step 4 — Recognize the Inner Structure:**
  The expression $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} [x(t) e^{-\sigma t}] e^{-j\omega t} dt$ is precisely the **Fourier Transform of the damped signal** $x(t) e^{-\sigma t}$.

- **Step 5 — Set $\sigma = 0$:**
  When $\sigma = 0$, the damping factor $e^{-\sigma t} = e^{0} = 1$, leaving only the Fourier kernel $e^{-j\omega t}$:

  $$X(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt = F\{x(t)\}$$

- **Step 6 — State the Relationship:**
  Hence, the Fourier Transform is the Laplace Transform evaluated on the imaginary axis:

  $$X_{FT}(\omega) = X_{LT}(s) \big\vert_{s=j\omega}$$

### 2.2 Why This Relationship Matters (The "Why")

- **Why does this work?** The Laplace Transform multiplies the signal by $e^{-\sigma t}$ *before* applying the Fourier kernel. This multiplication acts as a convergence accelerator. For growing signals like $e^{at} u(t)$ where $a > 0$, the Fourier Transform may not exist, but multiplying by $e^{-\sigma t}$ (with $\sigma > a$) ensures convergence.
- **How is it used?** Whenever a problem gives you a Laplace-domain expression $X(s)$, you can directly obtain the Fourier-domain expression by substituting $s = j\omega$, provided the $j\omega$-axis lies within the ROC.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these are the most-tested formulas for Module 2.**

| **Transform / Property** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Region / Condition** |
|--------------------------|------------------------------|-------------------------|
| Fourier Transform | $X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$ | $x(t)$ must be absolutely integrable |
| Bilateral Laplace Transform | $X(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-st} dt$ | ROC: a strip in the $s$-plane |
| Complex Frequency | $s = \sigma + j\omega$ | $\sigma \in \mathbb{R}$, $\omega \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| Damped FT View of LT | $X(s) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t) e^{-\sigma t}\}$ | Always valid when LT exists |
| **Key Relation** | $X(\omega) = X(s) \big\vert_{s=j\omega}$ | Valid **only if** $j\omega$-axis $\in$ ROC |
| Condition for FT Existence | $j\omega$-axis must be within ROC of LT | ROC must include $\text{Re}(s) = 0$ |
| Inverse Relation | $X(s)\big\vert_{s=\sigma+j\omega} = \mathcal{F}\{x(t) e^{-\sigma t}\}$ | General case |
| Inverse FT | $x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(\omega) e^{j\omega t} d\omega$ | Standard inverse formula |
| Inverse LT (Bilateral) | $x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \int_{\sigma - j\infty}^{\sigma + j\infty} X(s) e^{st} ds$ | Bromwich contour integral |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Control Systems:** Laplace Transforms dominate SISO/MIMO control design (poles/zeros in $s$-plane). When a system is stable, the Fourier Transform (frequency response) is obtained by $s \to j\omega$ substitution.
- **Filter Design:** Bode plots and frequency response plots use the FT, which is essentially the LT evaluated on the $j\omega$-axis.
- **Signal Processing:** LT is used for transient analysis; FT is used for steady-state frequency content. The $s = j\omega$ substitution bridges them.
- **Communication Systems:** The $j\omega$-axis represents the actual physical frequencies transmitted over a channel.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Laplace to Fourier

We now derive the relationship between the Fourier and Laplace Transforms from first principles.

**Starting Point — Bilateral Laplace Transform:**

$$X(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-st} dt$$

**Step 1: Express $s$ in Cartesian form:**

Let the complex frequency variable be:

$$s = \sigma + j\omega$$

**Step 2: Substitute this into the LT definition:**

$$X(\sigma + j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-(\sigma + j\omega) t} dt$$

**Step 3: Apply the exponential separation rule $e^{a \cdot b} = e^a \cdot e^b$:**

The exponent can be split as:

$$-(\sigma + j\omega)t = -\sigma t - j\omega t$$

Therefore:

$$e^{-(\sigma + j\omega) t} = e^{-\sigma t} \cdot e^{-j\omega t}$$

**Step 4: Substitute back into the integral:**

$$X(\sigma + j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \cdot e^{-\sigma t} \cdot e^{-j\omega t} dt$$

**Step 5: Re-arrange to identify the Fourier Transform structure:**

Since $e^{-\sigma t}$ is a deterministic function of $t$ (for a fixed $\sigma$), we can bring it inside $x(t)$:

$$X(\sigma + j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[x(t) e^{-\sigma t}\right] e^{-j\omega t} dt$$

**Step 6: Recognize this as the FT of a damped signal:**

By the definition of the Fourier Transform:

$$\mathcal{F}\{g(t)\} = G(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$$

Let $g(t) = x(t) e^{-\sigma t}$. Then:

$$X(\sigma + j\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t) e^{-\sigma t}\}$$

**Step 7: Specialize to the imaginary axis ($\sigma = 0$):**

When $\sigma = 0$, the damping term $e^{-\sigma t} = e^{0} = 1$:

$$X(0 + j\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t) \cdot 1\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$$

**Step 8: Identify the result as the Fourier Transform:**

$$X(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt = X_{FT}(\omega)$$

**Step 9: Final Compact Relation:**

$$\boxed{X_{FT}(\omega) = X_{LT}(s) \big\vert_{s = j\omega}}$$

This result is valid **only if** the integral converges, i.e., the $j\omega$-axis belongs to the ROC of $X(s)$.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Demonstrating the Relation

**Problem:** A causal signal is given as $x(t) = e^{-at} u(t)$, where $a > 0$. Find the LT, then derive the FT using the relation, and verify the result independently.

**Step 1: Compute the Bilateral LT of $x(t)$:**

$$X(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-at} u(t) \cdot e^{-st} dt$$

Since $u(t) = 1$ for $t \geq 0$ and $0$ otherwise:

$$X(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-at} \cdot e^{-st} dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s+a)t} dt$$

**Step 2: Evaluate the integral:**

$$X(s) = \left[ \frac{e^{-(s+a)t}}{-(s+a)} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{s+a}, \quad \text{for } \text{Re}(s) > -a$$

The ROC is $\text{Re}(s) > -a$.

**Step 3: Apply the relation $s = j\omega$:**

$$X_{FT}(\omega) = X_{LT}(s) \big\vert_{s = j\omega} = \frac{1}{j\omega + a}$$

**Step 4: Verify by computing the FT directly:**

$$X_{FT}(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-at} u(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(a+j\omega)t} dt$$

$$X_{FT}(\omega) = \left[ \frac{e^{-(a+j\omega)t}}{-(a+j\omega)} \right]_{0}^{\infty}$$

**Step 5: Convergence check:**

For $a > 0$, as $t \to \infty$, $e^{-(a+j\omega)t} \to 0$. Therefore:

$$X_{FT}(\omega) = \frac{0 - 1}{-(a+j\omega)} = \frac{1}{a + j\omega}$$

**Step 6: Compare results:**

The result $\dfrac{1}{a + j\omega}$ matches the substitution result from Step 3. Hence verified. ✓

---

### 3.3 Counter-Example — When the Relation Fails

**Problem:** Consider $x(t) = e^{2t} u(t)$. Compute the LT, then attempt to find the FT.

**Step 1: Compute the LT:**

$$X(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{2t} e^{-st} dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s-2)t} dt = \frac{1}{s-2}, \quad \text{Re}(s) > 2$$

**Step 2: The ROC is $\text{Re}(s) > 2$.**

**Step 3: Attempt to use the relation:**

The $j\omega$-axis corresponds to $\text{Re}(s) = 0$, which is **not in the ROC** (since ROC is $\text{Re}(s) > 2$).

**Step 4: Confirm by computing the FT directly:**

$$X_{FT}(\omega) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{2t} e^{-j\omega t} dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{(2-j\omega)t} dt$$

Since the exponent's real part is $2 > 0$, the integrand grows without bound. **The FT does not exist.**

**Conclusion:** The relation $X_{FT}(\omega) = X_{LT}(s)\big\vert_{s=j\omega}$ fails when the $j\omega$-axis is outside the ROC.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students blindly substitute $s = j\omega$ without checking the ROC. Always verify that the $j\omega$-axis lies within the ROC of $X(s)$ before applying the relation.

---

### 3.4 Summary of the Analytical Matrix

| **Scenario** | **ROC of $X(s)$** | **$j\omega$-axis in ROC?** | **FT Exists?** | **Relation Valid?** |
|--------------|--------------------|------------------------------|------------------|-----------------------|
| $e^{-at}u(t),\ a>0$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ | Yes | Yes | ✓ Valid |
| $e^{2t}u(t)$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 2$ | No | No | ✗ Invalid |
| $u(t)$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ | No (boundary) | Distributional FT | Conditionally |
| $e^{-a\vert t\vert}$ | $-a < \text{Re}(s) < a$ | Yes (if $a > 0$) | Yes | ✓ Valid |
| $\delta(t)$ | Entire $s$-plane | Yes | Yes | ✓ Valid |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Laplace to Fourier Bridge

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the conceptual bridge from the time domain to the Laplace and Fourier domains, emphasizing the $s = j\omega$ substitution.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Time Domain Signal x of t"] --> B["Apply Laplace Kernel e to the power of minus s times t"]
    A --> C["Apply Fourier Kernel e to the power of minus j omega times t"]
    B --> D["Bilateral Laplace Transform X of s"]
    D --> E["s plane Representation"]
    E --> F["ROC is a vertical strip in s plane"]
    D --> G{"Is j omega axis inside ROC?"}
    G -->|"Yes"| H["Substitute s equals j omega"]
    G -->|"No"| I["Fourier Transform Does NOT Exist"]
    H --> J["Fourier Transform X of omega"]
    C --> J
    J --> K["Frequency Domain Representation"]
    F --> L["Region of Convergence strip with sigma equals 0 line"]
    L --> G
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Engineering View (Control System)

The following diagram shows how the relation is used in practical control systems and signal processing applications.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Input Signal x of t"] --> P2["System described by Transfer Function H of s"]
    P2 --> P3["Laplace Domain Output Y of s equals H of s times X of s"]
    P3 --> P4{"Stability Check: ROC includes j omega axis?"}
    P4 -->|"Yes, all poles in LHP"| P5["Substitute s equals j omega in H of s"]
    P4 -->|"No, poles in RHP"| P6["System Unstable: No Steady State FT"]
    P5 --> P7["Frequency Response H of j omega"]
    P7 --> P8["Bode Plot and Magnitude Phase Plots"]
    P7 --> P9["Steady State Sinusoidal Analysis"]
```

### 4.3 Comparison Flowchart — When to Use LT vs. FT

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Given Signal x of t"] --> Q2{"Is the signal absolutely integrable?"}
    Q2 -->|"Yes"| Q3["Both FT and LT exist"]
    Q2 -->|"No, growing signal"| Q4{"Does the signal grow exponentially?"}
    Q4 -->|"Yes, like e to the a t with a greater than 0"| Q5["FT may NOT exist"]
    Q4 -->|"No, bounded non integrable like u of t"| Q6["FT exists in distribution sense, LT exists classically"]
    Q3 --> Q7["Use relation s equals j omega freely"]
    Q5 --> Q8["Use LT only, or check ROC carefully"]
    Q6 --> Q9["Use LT for transient, FT for steady state sinusoidal response"]
```

### 4.4 s-Plane Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SP["Complex s Plane"]
        direction LR
        AX["Real Axis: sigma"] --- IM["Imaginary Axis: j omega"]
    end
    R1["ROC Right Half Plane: Re of s greater than a"]
    R2["ROC Strip: minus b less than Re of s less than a"]
    R3["ROC Left Half Plane: Re of s less than minus b"]
    SP --> NOTE["The j omega axis is the vertical line sigma equals 0"]
    NOTE --> CK["FT exists only if the vertical line sigma equals 0 lies inside the ROC"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the relationship between the Fourier Transform and the Laplace Transform. When is this relationship valid?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Fourier Transform of a signal $x(t)$ is obtained from its Bilateral Laplace Transform by substituting $s = j\omega$:

$$X(\omega) = X(s) \big\vert_{s = j\omega}$$

This is because, starting from the bilateral Laplace Transform:

$$X(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-st} dt, \quad s = \sigma + j\omega$$

Setting $\sigma = 0$ reduces the kernel to $e^{-j\omega t}$, which is exactly the Fourier Transform kernel:

$$X(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt = X_{FT}(\omega)$$

**Validity Condition:** The relation is valid **only when the $j\omega$-axis lies within the Region of Convergence (ROC) of $X(s)$**. If the $j\omega$-axis is outside the ROC, the Fourier Transform does not exist in the conventional sense.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Explain why the Fourier Transform of $e^{2t}u(t)$ does not exist, but its Laplace Transform does. Use the relation $X(\omega) = X(s)\vert_{s=j\omega}$ in your explanation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The bilateral Laplace Transform of $x(t) = e^{2t} u(t)$ is:

$$X(s) = \frac{1}{s-2}, \quad \text{ROC: } \text{Re}(s) > 2$$

The ROC is a **right half-plane** bounded away from the $j\omega$-axis. Specifically, the $j\omega$-axis corresponds to $\text{Re}(s) = 0$, which is **not inside** the ROC.

When we attempt to apply the relation:

$$X(\omega) = X(s) \big\vert_{s=j\omega} = \frac{1}{j\omega - 2}$$

This substitution is **mathematically allowed** but the integral definition diverges:

$$X_{FT}(\omega) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{2t} e^{-j\omega t} dt \to \infty$$

since $e^{2t}$ grows without bound. Hence, the Fourier Transform does **not exist**, while the Laplace Transform converges because the multiplicative factor $e^{-\sigma t}$ (with $\sigma > 2$) damps the exponential growth.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the Fourier Transform and the Bilateral Laplace Transform, starting from the definition of the Laplace Transform. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the signal $x(t) = e^{-3t} u(t)$:
  - (i) Find its Laplace Transform $X(s)$ and specify the ROC. **(3 marks)**
  - (ii) Using the relation $X(\omega) = X(s)\vert_{s=j\omega}$, find the Fourier Transform. **(2 marks)**
  - (iii) Verify the result by directly computing the Fourier Transform from its definition. **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from the bilateral Laplace Transform definition:

$$X(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-st} dt$$

Express $s$ in Cartesian form $s = \sigma + j\omega$:

$$X(\sigma + j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-(\sigma + j\omega)t} dt$$

Apply the exponential product rule:

$$X(\sigma + j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-\sigma t} e^{-j\omega t} dt$$

Re-arrange the integrand:

$$X(\sigma + j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[x(t) e^{-\sigma t}\right] e^{-j\omega t} dt$$

Recognize this as the Fourier Transform of the damped signal $x(t) e^{-\sigma t}$:

$$X(\sigma + j\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t) e^{-\sigma t}\}$$

Now, set $\sigma = 0$. The damping term becomes $e^{0} = 1$:

$$X(0 + j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$$

The right-hand side is the definition of the Fourier Transform. Therefore:

$$\boxed{X_{FT}(\omega) = X_{LT}(s) \big\vert_{s = j\omega}}$$

**Validity:** This holds if and only if the $j\omega$-axis ($\text{Re}(s) = 0$) lies within the ROC of $X(s)$.

---

**(b) Solution:**

**(i) Laplace Transform and ROC (3 marks):**

$$X(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-3t} u(t) e^{-st} dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s+3)t} dt$$

$$X(s) = \left[\frac{e^{-(s+3)t}}{-(s+3)}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{s+3}, \quad \text{ROC: } \text{Re}(s) > -3$$

**(ii) Apply the relation (2 marks):**

$$X_{FT}(\omega) = X_{LT}(s) \big\vert_{s=j\omega} = \frac{1}{j\omega + 3}$$

**Validity check:** The $j\omega$-axis ($\sigma = 0$) satisfies $\sigma > -3$, so it lies within the ROC. ✓

**(iii) Direct verification (2 marks):**

$$X_{FT}(\omega) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-3t} e^{-j\omega t} dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(3+j\omega)t} dt$$

$$X_{FT}(\omega) = \left[\frac{e^{-(3+j\omega)t}}{-(3+j\omega)}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{0 - 1}{-(3+j\omega)} = \frac{1}{3 + j\omega}$$

Both methods yield the same result. ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question]`

**(a)** Discuss the Region of Convergence (ROC) and explain, with a suitable example, how the relationship $X(\omega) = X(s)\vert_{s=j\omega}$ depends on the ROC. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the Laplace Transform of $x(t) = e^{-2t} u(t) + e^{3t} u(-t)$ and determine whether its Fourier Transform exists. Justify your answer using the relation between FT and LT. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) ROC and Its Role (7 marks):**

The **Region of Convergence (ROC)** of a Laplace Transform $X(s)$ is the set of all complex values $s = \sigma + j\omega$ for which the integral $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-st} dt$ converges. The ROC is bounded by vertical lines (poles) in the complex $s$-plane.

**Connection to FT:** Since the Fourier Transform requires the integral $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$ to converge, the FT exists if and only if the imaginary axis $\text{Re}(s) = 0$ (the $j\omega$-axis) lies within the ROC.

**Example — $x(t) = e^{2t} u(t)$:**

$$X(s) = \frac{1}{s-2}, \quad \text{ROC: } \text{Re}(s) > 2$$

The $j\omega$-axis ($\text{Re}(s) = 0$) is **not** in the ROC. Therefore, the FT does not exist.

**Example — $x(t) = e^{-2t} u(t)$:**

$$X(s) = \frac{1}{s+2}, \quad \text{ROC: } \text{Re}(s) > -2$$

The $j\omega$-axis is in the ROC. The FT exists:

$$X(\omega) = \frac{1}{j\omega + 2}$$

This demonstrates that **the ROC is the determining factor** for the validity of the relation $X(\omega) = X(s)\vert_{s=j\omega}$.

---

**(b) Two-sided Signal Analysis (7 marks):**

Given: $x(t) = e^{-2t} u(t) + e^{3t} u(-t)$.

**Step 1: Compute the Laplace Transform of each term:**

For $e^{-2t} u(t)$:

$$X_1(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2t} e^{-st} dt = \frac{1}{s+2}, \quad \text{ROC}_1: \text{Re}(s) > -2$$

For $e^{3t} u(-t)$:

$$X_2(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{0} e^{3t} e^{-st} dt = \int_{-\infty}^{0} e^{(3-s)t} dt$$

$$X_2(s) = \left[\frac{e^{(3-s)t}}{3-s}\right]_{-\infty}^{0} = \frac{1}{-(s-3)} = \frac{-1}{s-3}, \quad \text{ROC}_2: \text{Re}(s) < 3$$

**Step 2: Total Laplace Transform:**

$$X(s) = X_1(s) + X_2(s) = \frac{1}{s+2} - \frac{1}{s-3}$$

**Step 3: Combined ROC:**

$$\text{ROC}: \text{Re}(s) > -2 \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{Re}(s) < 3$$

$$-2 < \text{Re}(s) < 3$$

This is a vertical **strip** in the $s$-plane.

**Step 4: Check if $j\omega$-axis is in the ROC:**

The $j\omega$-axis corresponds to $\text{Re}(s) = 0$. Since $-2 < 0 < 3$, the $j\omega$-axis **lies within the ROC**. ✓

**Step 5: Apply the relation to find the FT:**

$$X_{FT}(\omega) = X_{LT}(s) \big\vert_{s=j\omega} = \frac{1}{j\omega + 2} - \frac{1}{j\omega - 3}$$

$$X_{FT}(\omega) = \frac{1}{j\omega + 2} + \frac{1}{3 - j\omega}$$

The Fourier Transform **exists** because the $j\omega$-axis is in the ROC.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Skipping the ROC check** (–2 marks): Many students blindly substitute $s = j\omega$ without verifying ROC inclusion. Always state and verify the ROC.
> 2. **Confusing unilateral with bilateral LT** (–2 marks): For causal signals, the unilateral and bilateral LTs often coincide, but for two-sided signals they differ. Be clear which one you are using.
> 3. **Forgetting to state validity conditions** (–1 mark): Always mention the condition "$j\omega$-axis must lie in ROC" when stating the relation.
> 4. **Sign errors in $e^{-st}$ exponent** (–1 to 2 marks): Carefully handle $e^{-(\sigma + j\omega)t} = e^{-\sigma t} e^{-j\omega t}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Relation:** $X_{FT}(\omega) = X_{LT}(s) \big\vert_{s=j\omega}$
- **Complex Frequency:** $s = \sigma + j\omega$ — $\sigma$ is damping, $j\omega$ is oscillation
- **LT of damped signal:** $X(\sigma + j\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t) e^{-\sigma t}\}$
- **Validity Condition:** The $j\omega$-axis must be inside the ROC of $X(s)$
- **Fourier Transform is a slice of the Laplace Transform** along the imaginary axis
- **LT is more general** — it can handle growing signals (e.g., $e^{at} u(t)$ with $a > 0$) where FT fails
- **ROC shapes:** Right half-plane (right-sided), left half-plane (left-sided), strip (two-sided), entire plane (finite duration)
- **Damping intuition:** $e^{-\sigma t}$ multiplies the signal before FT analysis, ensuring convergence for a wider class of signals
- **When $j\omega$-axis is outside ROC:** FT does not exist in conventional sense (may exist as distribution or not at all)
- **Application areas:** Control systems (substitute $s = j\omega$ in transfer function to get frequency response), filter design, communication systems
- **Always check ROC first**, then apply the substitution — never reverse the order
- **FT formula:** $X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$
- **Bilateral LT formula:** $X(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-st} dt$
- **Key insight:** Setting $\sigma = 0$ in LT is the mathematical operation that recovers the FT

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete-Time Fourier Series (DTFS) and its Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT403 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Discrete-Time Fourier Series (DTFS) and its Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Discrete-Time Fourier Series (DTFS)** is a frequency-domain representation of a **periodic discrete-time signal**. It expresses a periodic sequence $x[n]$ as a weighted sum of complex exponentials whose frequencies are integer multiples of the **fundamental digital frequency** $\Omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{N}$, where $N$ is the fundamental period of the signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Period Condition:** A discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is periodic with period $N$ if and only if $x[n+N] = x[n]$ for all integers $n$, where $N$ is the **smallest positive integer** satisfying this condition. The DTFS representation requires that the signal is *absolutely summable over one period* (which is a much weaker condition than the Dirichlet conditions of the continuous-time case).

**Synthesis Equation (Time Domain → Frequency Domain Reconstruction):**

$$
x[n] = \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k\, e^{j k \Omega_0 n}, \quad \Omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{N}
$$

**Analysis Equation (Coefficient Extraction):**

$$
a_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x[n]\, e^{-j k \Omega_0 n}
$$

The notation $\sum_{k=\langle N \rangle}$ denotes summation over any **contiguous block of $N$ integer values of $k$** (typically $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$). The DTFS coefficients $a_k$ are themselves **periodic with period $N$** in the index $k$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **musical chord played on a piano**. What you actually *hear* is a single complex sound wave reaching your ear, but that sound is in reality a **superposition (sum) of several pure musical notes** — say, the fundamental tone C, the perfect fifth E, and the octave C′. Your ear performs the role of a *Fourier analyzer*, breaking the chord into its constituent notes (frequencies).

The **DTFS** does the *exact same job* for digital signals:

| Real-World Analogy | DTFS Concept |
|---|---|
| Musical chord reaching your ear | Periodic discrete-time signal $x[n]$ |
| Individual pure notes inside the chord | Complex exponentials $e^{jk\Omega_0 n}$ |
| Loudness (amplitude) of each note | DTFS coefficient $a_k$ |
| Piano tuner finding the notes | Analysis equation (extracting $a_k$) |
| Orchestra playing all notes together | Synthesis equation (reconstructing $x[n]$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Unlike the Continuous-Time Fourier Series (CTFS) which may require *infinitely many* harmonics, the DTFS representation of a signal with period $N$ contains **at most $N$ distinct complex exponential components** — because $e^{jk\Omega_0 n}$ is *already periodic* in $k$ with period $N$. This is a **unique and powerful property** of discrete-time analysis, leading to compact finite-dimensional representations.

---

## 1.3 Why DTFS Matters in Engineering

DTFS is the backbone of numerous modern engineering applications:

- **Digital Audio Processing:** Decomposing periodic musical notes into harmonics for equalizers and synthesizers.
- **Spectral Analysis in Communications:** Analyzing OFDM (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing) subcarriers in 4G/5G, Wi-Fi, and LTE systems.
- **Image Processing:** Decomposing periodic textures (e.g., wallpaper patterns) for compression.
- **Vibration Analysis:** Identifying harmonic content of rotating machinery (turbines, engines) from sampled accelerometer data.
- **Power System Analysis:** Discrete harmonic analysis of sampled AC waveforms to detect distortion.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to "represent $x[n]$ using DTFS", examiners expect you to: (1) verify periodicity, (2) compute coefficients $a_k$ over one period using the analysis equation, (3) explicitly state the synthesis equation, and (4) comment on the periodicity of $a_k$ in $k$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Existence Conditions (Dirichlet Conditions for DTFS)

For a periodic discrete-time signal $x[n]$ with period $N$ to have a valid DTFS representation, the following **sufficient conditions** must be satisfied over any single period:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DTFS Existence Conditions:**
> 1. **Absolute Summability:** $x[n]$ must be absolutely summable over one period, i.e., $\sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$
> 2. **Finite Energy per Period:** $\sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} \vert x[n] \vert^2 < \infty$
>
> Unlike the continuous-time Fourier Series, these conditions are *automatically satisfied* for any **practical bounded periodic sequence**, making DTFS nearly *always* well-defined.

---

## 2.2 Logical Steps of DTFS Analysis and Synthesis

### Step 1 — Verify Periodicity
Confirm that $x[n+N] = x[n]$ for the smallest positive integer $N$. This is the **fundamental period**.

### Step 2 — Identify the Fundamental Frequency
Compute the **fundamental digital frequency** in radians per sample:
$$
\Omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{N}
$$
The **$k$-th harmonic** corresponds to frequency $k\Omega_0$.

### Step 3 — Compute DTFS Coefficients (Analysis)
Apply the analysis equation over **one period** of $n$:
$$
a_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x[n]\, e^{-j k \Omega_0 n}
$$
The coefficients $\{a_k\}$ are periodic in $k$ with period $N$: $a_{k+N} = a_k$.

### Step 4 — Reconstruct the Signal (Synthesis)
Combine the weighted complex exponentials:
$$
x[n] = \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k\, e^{j k \Omega_0 n}
$$

### Step 5 — Exploit Symmetry (Optional but KTU-Favored)
If $x[n]$ is **real**, then $a_{-k} = a_k^{*}$, simplifying computation.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **direct KTU 2024 ESE exam essentials**. Memorize the table layout, not just the equations — KTU examiners test whether students remember *which formula applies to which scenario*.

| # | Concept | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Periodicity | $x[n+N] = x[n]$ | $N$ is smallest positive integer |
| 2 | Fundamental Frequency | $\Omega_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{N}$ | In **radians/sample** |
| 3 | Synthesis Equation | $x[n] = \displaystyle\sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k e^{j k \Omega_0 n}$ | Sum over any $N$ consecutive $k$ values |
| 4 | Analysis Equation | $a_k = \dfrac{1}{N} \displaystyle\sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x[n] e^{-j k \Omega_0 n}$ | Extract one coefficient per period |
| 5 | Periodicity of Coefficients | $a_{k+N} = a_k$ | Always true; only $N$ distinct values |
| 6 | DC Component | $a_0 = \dfrac{1}{N} \displaystyle\sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x[n]$ | Average of $x[n]$ over one period |
| 7 | Real Signal Symmetry | $a_{-k} = a_k^{*}$ | If $x[n] \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 8 | Even Signal Symmetry | $a_k \in \mathbb{R}$ | If $x[n]$ is real and even |
| 9 | Odd Signal Symmetry | $a_k \in \text{purely imaginary}$ | If $x[n]$ is real and odd |
