# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define DTFT and state the conditions for its existence. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

The Discrete-Time Fourier Transform of a discrete-time non-periodic sequence $x[n]$ is defined as:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}$$

The inverse DTFT is:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X(e^{j\omega})\, e^{j\omega n}\, d\omega$$

**Existence Conditions:** The DTFT of $x[n]$ exists if any of the following sufficient conditions is satisfied:
1. $x[n]$ is **absolutely summable**: $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \mid x[n] \mid < \infty$
2. $x[n]$ is **square summable** (finite energy): $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \mid x[n] \mid^2 < \infty$
3. $x[n]$ is **periodic**, in which case the DTFT exists as an impulse train in the frequency domain.

---

### Question 2: State and prove the time-shifting property of DTFT. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** If $x[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X(e^{j\omega})$, then $x[n - n_0] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} e^{-j\omega n_0} X(e^{j\omega})$.

**Proof:**

Let $y[n] = x[n - n_0]$. By definition:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n - n_0]\, e^{-j\omega n}$$

Substituting $m = n - n_0$ (so $n = m + n_0$):

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m]\, e^{-j\omega (m + n_0)} = e^{-j\omega n_0} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m]\, e^{-j\omega m} = e^{-j\omega n_0} X(e^{j\omega})$$

$\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part A):** Students often forget the **periodicity of DTFT** in the answer. Always mention that $X(e^{j\omega})$ is periodic with period $2\pi$. Failing to state this is a common 0.5-mark deduction.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive DTFT Computation Problem **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Find the DTFT of the sequence $x[n] = a^n u[n]$ where $\mid a \mid < 1$. Also plot the magnitude and phase spectrum. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the convolution property of DTFT, find the DTFT of $y[n] = a^n u[n] * b^n u[n]$ where $\mid a \mid, \mid b \mid < 1$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Apply the definition.**

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a^n u[n]\, e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (a e^{-j\omega})^n$$

**Step 2 — Sum the geometric series.**

For $\mid a \mid < 1$:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$$

**Step 3 — Express in rectangular form by multiplying by complex conjugate.**

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}} \cdot \frac{1 - a e^{j\omega}}{1 - a e^{j\omega}} = \frac{1 - a e^{j\omega}}{1 - 2a\cos\omega + a^2}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the real and imaginary parts.**

$$\text{Re}\{X(e^{j\omega})\} = \frac{1 - a\cos\omega}{1 - 2a\cos\omega + a^2}, \quad \text{Im}\{X(e^{j\omega})\} = \frac{-a\sin\omega}{1 - 2a\cos\omega + a^2}$$

**Step 5 — Magnitude and phase spectrum.**

$$\mid X(e^{j\omega}) \mid = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 2a\cos\omega + a^2}}$$

$$\angle X(e^{j\omega}) = -\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{a\sin\omega}{1 - a\cos\omega}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Plot description:**
- Magnitude: Maximum at $\omega = 0$ (value $= \frac{1}{1-a}$), minimum at $\omega = \pi$ (value $= \frac{1}{1+a}$), periodic with period $2\pi$.
- Phase: Odd function of $\omega$, zero at $\omega = 0$ and $\omega = \pm\pi$.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Recall DTFT pairs from the result of part (a).**

$$a^n u[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}} = A(e^{j\omega})$$

$$b^n u[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} \frac{1}{1 - b e^{-j\omega}} = B(e^{j\omega})$$

**Step 2 — Apply the convolution property.**

$$y[n] = a^n u[n] * b^n u[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} Y(e^{j\omega}) = A(e^{j\omega}) \cdot B(e^{j\omega})$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the two spectra.**

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{(1 - a e^{-j\omega})(1 - b e^{-j\omega})}$$

This is the required DTFT. $\blacksquare$

**Alternative verification (by direct convolution):**

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{n} a^k b^{n-k} = b^n \sum_{k=0}^{n} \left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^k = \frac{b^{n+1} - a^{n+1}}{b - a}$$ for $n \ge 0$, and $0$ otherwise. Computing its DTFT yields the same result, confirming the convolution property.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Comprehensive Problem **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** State and prove the convolution and multiplication properties of DTFT. Mention their significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A discrete-time system has impulse response $h[n] = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n u[n]$. Find the response $y[n]$ of this system to the input $x[n] = \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^n u[n]$ using DTFT-based analysis. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Convolution Property:** If $x[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X(e^{j\omega})$ and $h[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} H(e^{j\omega})$, then:

$$y[n] = x[n] * h[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} Y(e^{j\omega}) = X(e^{j\omega}) H(e^{j\omega})$$

**Proof:** Already derived in Section 3.4 of these notes (reproduce there).

**Multiplication Property:** If $y[n] = x[n] \cdot p[n]$, then:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X(e^{j\theta}) P(e^{j(\omega - \theta)})\, d\theta$$

**Proof Sketch:** Start with $Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_n x[n] p[n] e^{-j\omega n}$. Substitute $x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X(e^{j\theta}) e^{j\theta n} d\theta$ and interchange sum and integral:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X(e^{j\theta}) \left[\sum_n p[n] e^{-j(\omega - \theta) n}\right] d\theta = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X(e^{j\theta}) P(e^{j(\omega - \theta)}) d\theta$$

**Significance:** 
- Convolution in time $\leftrightarrow$ Multiplication in frequency: Forms the basis of LTI system analysis and fast filtering using FFT.
- Multiplication in time $\leftrightarrow$ Periodic convolution in frequency: Forms the basis of modulation theory (mixers, AM, OFDM subcarrier mapping).

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Find DTFT of input.**

$$x[n] = \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^n u[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{4} e^{-j\omega}}$$

**Step 2 — Find DTFT of impulse response.**

$$h[n] = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n u[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{2} e^{-j\omega}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute output DTFT using convolution property.**

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = X(e^{j\omega}) \cdot H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{\left(1 - \frac{1}{4} e^{-j\omega}\right)\left(1 - \frac{1}{2} e^{-j\omega}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply partial fraction expansion.**

We write:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{A}{1 - \frac{1}{4} e^{-j\omega}} + \frac{B}{1 - \frac{1}{2} e^{-j\omega}}$$

Solving: $A = -1$, $B = 2$.

**Step 5 — Identify DTFT pairs for inverse transform.**

Using the pair $a^n u[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$:

$$y[n] = -1 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^n u[n] + 2 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n u[n]$$

$$\boxed{y[n] = \left[2 \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n - \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^n\right] u[n]}$$

$\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B — 14 Mark Questions):**
> 1. **Sign convention error:** Many students write $e^{j\omega n}$ in the forward transform and $e^{-j\omega n}$ in the inverse. The convention is **forward = $e^{-j\omega n}$** and **inverse = $e^{+j\omega n}$**. This single sign error can cost **2 to 3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ factor:** In the inverse transform, the integration is multiplied by $\frac{1}{2\pi}$. Forgetting this gives an answer off by a constant factor.
> 3. **Integration interval:** Always state the integration is over any interval of length $2\pi$ (e.g., $-\pi$ to $\pi$). Stating "$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}$" is technically wrong and loses marks.
> 4. **Convergence conditions:** In exponential DTFTs, you **must** state $\mid a \mid < 1$. Marks are explicitly allotted for this condition.
> 5. **Rectangular form for magnitude/phase:** When the question asks for magnitude spectrum, you must convert to the $\frac{1 - a\cos\omega + \ldots}{1 - 2a\cos\omega + a^2}$ form first. Direct evaluation from the polar form without simplifying is a mark-losing shortcut.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — DTFT and Its Properties**

- **Definition:** Forward DTFT uses $e^{-j\omega n}$ in the summation; inverse DTFT uses $e^{+j\omega n}$ inside an integral scaled by $\frac{1}{2\pi}$.
- **Periodicity:** $X(e^{j\omega})$ is **always periodic with period $2\pi$** in $\omega$. Plot only over $[-\pi, \pi]$.
- **Convergence:** Absolutely summable $\Rightarrow$ uniform convergence; square summable (finite energy) $\Rightarrow$ mean-square convergence.
- **Most-Used DTFT Pairs:** $\delta[n] \leftrightarrow 1$; $1 \leftrightarrow 2\pi \sum_k \delta(\omega - 2\pi k)$; $a^n u[n] \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$ (for $\mid a \mid < 1$).
- **Linearity:** $a x_1[n] + b x_2[n] \leftrightarrow a X_1(e^{j\omega}) + b X_2(e^{j\omega})$.
- **Time Shift:** Delay $n_0$ adds linear phase $e^{-j\omega n_0}$.
- **Frequency Shift:** Multiplication by $e^{j\omega_0 n}$ shifts the spectrum by $\omega_0$.
- **Time Reversal:** $x[-n] \leftrightarrow X(e^{-j\omega})$.
- **Convolution Property:** $x[n] * h[n] \leftrightarrow X(e^{j\omega}) H(e^{j\omega})$ — **most important property for LTI systems**.
- **Multiplication Property:** Modulation in time = periodic convolution in frequency (with $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ factor).
- **Parseval's Theorem:** Energy in time domain = $\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{2\pi} \mid X(e^{j\omega}) \mid^2 d\omega$ in frequency domain.
- **Conjugate Symmetry:** For real $x[n]$, $X(e^{j\omega}) = X^*(e^{-j\omega})$, meaning $\vert X \vert$ is even and $\angle X$ is odd.
- **Differentiation in Frequency:** $n x[n] \leftrightarrow j \frac{d}{d\omega} X(e^{j\omega})$.
- **Differencing:** $x[n] - x[n-1] \leftrightarrow (1 - e^{-j\omega}) X(e^{j\omega})$.
- **DFT Relationship:** DFT is obtained by sampling DTFT at $\omega_k = 2\pi k/N$ for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$.
- **Inverse DTFT convention:** Always multiply the integral by $\frac{1}{2\pi}$, integrate over exactly $2\pi$, and remember the forward exponent is **negative** while the inverse exponent is **positive**.

**Key Formulas to Memorize (verbatim):**

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$$

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega n} d\omega$$

$$a^n u[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}, \quad \mid a \mid < 1$$

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \mid x[n] \mid^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \mid X(e^{j\omega}) \mid^2 d\omega$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Discrete-Time LTI Systems using DTFT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT403 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Discrete-Time LTI Systems using DTFT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Discrete-Time Signals and Systems, a **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system** is completely characterized in the time domain by its **impulse response h[n]**. The Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) of this impulse response is defined as the **Frequency Response** of the system, denoted as **H(e^{jω})**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **H(e^{jω}) = DTFT {h[n]}** is the single most important function in Module 3. Every question on DTFT-based LTI analysis ultimately reduces to evaluating or interpreting this complex-valued function of the normalized angular frequency **ω** (in radians/sample).

Formally, the analysis of a discrete-time LTI system using DTFT means:
1. Transforming the input x[n] and the impulse response h[n] into the frequency domain using the DTFT pair.
2. Multiplying the transforms: **Y(e^{jω}) = H(e^{jω}) · X(e^{jω})** (Convolution Theorem of DTFT).
3. Computing the magnitude response |H(e^{jω})| and phase response ∠H(e^{jω}) to characterize how the system modifies each sinusoidal component.
4. Inverting via Inverse DTFT to recover the steady-state output y[n] = h[n] * x[n].

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a music studio where a sound engineer sits behind an **equalizer (EQ)** panel. The incoming musical signal x[n] (e.g., a vocal track) contains many "tones" at different pitches (frequencies). The equalizer is the LTI system, and each slider on the EQ either boosts or cuts a particular pitch.

- The **impulse response h[n]** is the "fingerprint" of the equalizer — if you clap once (a unit impulse) into the EQ, the recorded echo h[n] tells you everything about how the device colours the sound.
- The **frequency response H(e^{jω})** is the EQ's frequency curve — it plots exactly how much gain is applied at every pitch.
- The **magnitude |H(e^{jω})|** tells you the **gain** (boost/cut) at pitch ω.
- The **phase ∠H(e^{jω})** tells you the **time delay** the EQ introduces to each pitch.

Multiplying X(e^{jω}) by H(e^{jω}) is exactly like passing every frequency component of the song through its corresponding EQ slider — boosting the bass, attenuating the hiss, leaving the vocals untouched.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics**:
> - **ω = 2πf / f_s** : Normalized angular frequency (dimensionless, in **radians/sample**).
> - **Periodicity**: H(e^{jω}) is always **2π-periodic** because discrete-time signals are inherently sampled.
> - **Fundamental range**: Only the principal interval **−π ≤ ω ≤ π** (or equivalently **0 ≤ ω < 2π**) carries unique information.

## 1.3 Why Use DTFT for LTI Analysis?

| Aspect | Time-Domain Analysis | DTFT (Frequency-Domain) Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| Convolution operation | h[n] * x[n] (computationally heavy) | Multiplication: H(e^{jω}) · X(e^{jω}) (trivial) |
| Stability test | Σ \vert h[n] \vert < ∞ | H(e^{jω}) exists & is bounded |
| Sinusoidal response | Solve difference equation | Substitute x[n] = e^{jωn} directly |
| Filter design | Trial & error | Direct specification of \vert H(e^{jω}) \vert |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Favourite Question Pattern**: "Given h[n], determine whether the LTI system is stable. Justify using DTFT."
> **Answer cue**: If h[n] is absolutely summable (Σ \vert h[n] \vert < ∞), then H(e^{jω}) exists and the system is BIBO stable.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The DTFT Pair for LTI Systems

The DTFT pair applied to LTI system analysis is:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, e^{-j\omega n}$$

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) \, e^{j\omega n} \, d\omega$$

When applied to the **impulse response h[n]** of an LTI system, X(e^{jω}) is replaced by **H(e^{jω})** — the system's frequency response.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of DTFT-Based LTI Analysis

- **Step 1 — Identify the LTI system**: Obtain h[n] (either given directly, or from the difference equation coefficients).
- **Step 2 — Apply DTFT to h[n]**: Compute H(e^{jω}) = Σ h[n] e^{-jωn}.
- **Step 3 — Decompose into magnitude and phase**:
  - **Magnitude response**: \vert H(e^{jω}) \vert
  - **Phase response**: ∠H(e^{jω}) = arctan(Im{H}/Re{H})
- **Step 4 — Apply DTFT to input x[n]** to obtain X(e^{jω}).
- **Step 5 — Use the convolution theorem**: Y(e^{jω}) = H(e^{jω}) · X(e^{jω}).
- **Step 6 — Invert DTFT** to recover y[n] = (1/2π) ∫ Y(e^{jω}) e^{jωn} dω.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

| # | Formula / Property | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Frequency response definition | H(e^{jω}) = Σ_{n=−∞}^{∞} h[n] e^{−jωn} | DTFT of impulse response |
| 2 | Inverse (system impulse recovery) | h[n] = (1/2π) ∫_{−π}^{π} H(e^{jω}) e^{jωn} dω | Recover h[n] from H(e^{jω}) |
| 3 | Convolution in time ↔ Multiplication in freq. | y[n] = h[n] * x[n] ↔ Y(e^{jω}) = H(e^{jω}) X(e^{jω}) | Core LTI analysis identity |
| 4 | Eigenfunction property | e^{jωn} → H(e^{jω}) e^{jωn} | Complex exponentials are eigenfunctions of LTI |
| 5 | Periodicity | H(e^{j(ω+2π)}) = H(e^{jω}) | Discrete-time systems are 2π-periodic |
| 6 | Magnitude response | \vert H(e^{jω}) \vert = sqrt((Re{H})^2 + (Im{H})^2) | Gain at frequency ω |
| 7 | Phase response | ∠H(e^{jω}) = arctan2(Im{H}, Re{H}) | Phase shift at frequency ω |
| 8 | Real-h symmetry (for real h[n]) | H*(e^{jω}) = H(e^{−jω}) | Magnitude even, phase odd |
| 9 | Group delay | τ_g(ω) = − d(∠H)/dω | Frequency-dependent time delay |
| 10 | BIBO stability condition | Σ_{n=−∞}^{∞} \vert h[n] \vert < ∞ ⇔ H(e^{jω}) exists and bounded | H(e^{jω}) finite ∀ω |
| 11 | Sinusoidal steady-state response (x[n]=A cos(ω₀n+φ)) | y_ss[n] = A \vert H(e^{jω₀}) \vert cos(ω₀n + φ + ∠H(e^{jω₀})) | Crucial for filter interpretation |
| 12 | Ideal LPF magnitude | \vert H(e^{jω}) \vert = 1 for \vert ω \vert < ω_c, else 0 | Brick-wall filter |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In the table above, all absolute values use \vert · \vert to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. In your exam scripts, use the standard \mid · \mid notation.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

DTFT-based analysis is the **backbone of digital filter design** in:
- **Audio processing** (equalizers, noise-cancelling headphones).
- **Telecommunications** (channel equalizers, modulators).
- **Biomedical signal processing** (ECG/EEG filtering, hearing aids).
- **Image processing** (2D-DTFT for edge detection, blurring).
- **Control systems** (sampled-data controllers using z-transform, the discrete cousin of DTFT).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why H(e^{jω}) Multiplies X(e^{jω})

We start with the convolution sum governing any LTI system:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h[k] \, x[n-k]$$

Apply the DTFT to both sides, treating y[n] as the output sequence:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \left( \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h[k] \, x[n-k] \right) e^{-j\omega n}$$

Interchange the order of summation (valid because of absolute convergence for stable systems):

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h[k] \left( \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n-k] \, e^{-j\omega n} \right)$$

Now perform the change of variable **m = n − k**, so **n = m + k** and **dn = dm**:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h[k] \left( \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] \, e^{-j\omega (m+k)} \right)$$

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h[k] \, e^{-j\omega k} \left( \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] \, e^{-j\omega m} \right)$$

The bracketed sum is, by definition, the DTFT of x[n]:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \left( \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h[k] \, e^{-j\omega k} \right) X(e^{j\omega})$$

The remaining summation is precisely the DTFT of h[n], which we denote as H(e^{jω}):

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = H(e^{j\omega}) \cdot X(e^{j\omega})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning of every step**: The inner sum picks up the time-reversed, shifted copy of x at lag k; the outer sum weights it by h[k]. The change of variable m = n − k shifts the time-origin so that the inner sum becomes a pure DTFT of x, independent of k. The exponentials split, and the e^{−jωk} term gathers into the DTFT of h.

## 3.2 Full Worked Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

**Problem**: An LTI system has impulse response **h[n] = (1/2)^n u[n]**.
**(a)** Determine the frequency response H(e^{jω}) and sketch \vert H(e^{jω}) \vert.
**(b)** Find the steady-state response y[n] to the input **x[n] = cos((π/3) n)**.

### Part (a) — Solution

Apply the DTFT definition. Since h[n] = 0 for n < 0, the sum runs from n = 0 to ∞:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n e^{-j\omega n}$$

Factor out the common exponential term (n = 0) and identify the geometric series structure:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1}{2} e^{-j\omega}\right)^n$$

For a geometric series Σ r^n = 1/(1 − r) to converge, we need \vert r \vert < 1. Here \vert (1/2) e^{−jω} \vert = 1/2 < 1, so the series converges absolutely:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{2} e^{-j\omega}}$$

Now rationalize by multiplying numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{2}\cos\omega + j\frac{1}{2}\sin\omega}$$

Multiply top and bottom by the complex conjugate of the denominator (1 − (1/2)cos ω − j(1/2) sin ω):

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1 - \frac{1}{2}\cos\omega - j\frac{1}{2}\sin\omega}{\left(1 - \frac{1}{2}\cos\omega\right)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{2}\sin\omega\right)^2}$$

Compute the magnitude squared in the denominator:

$$\left(1 - \frac{1}{2}\cos\omega\right)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{2}\sin\omega\right)^2 = 1 - \cos\omega + \frac{1}{4}\cos^2\omega + \frac{1}{4}\sin^2\omega$$

Since cos²ω + sin²ω = 1, this simplifies to:

$$= 1 - \cos\omega + \frac{1}{4} = \frac{5}{4} - \cos\omega$$

So the **magnitude response** is:

$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\frac{5}{4} - \cos\omega}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{5 - 4\cos\omega}}$$

And the **phase response** is:

$$\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = -\arctan\left(\frac{\frac{1}{2}\sin\omega}{1 - \frac{1}{2}\cos\omega}\right)$$

**Sketch interpretation**:
- At ω = 0: \vert H(e^{j0}) \vert = 2/√(5−4) = 2/1 = **2** → DC gain of 2.
- At ω = π: \vert H(e^{jπ}) \vert = 2/√(5+4) = 2/3 ≈ **0.667** → high-frequency attenuation.
- The system acts as a **low-pass filter**, passing low frequencies with more gain.

### Part (b) — Solution

The input is x[n] = cos((π/3) n) = Re{e^{j(π/3)n}}. Since the system is LTI, we can use the **eigenfunction property**:

$$e^{j\omega_0 n} \xrightarrow{\text{LTI}} H(e^{j\omega_0}) \, e^{j\omega_0 n}$$

with ω₀ = π/3. Evaluate H(e^{jπ/3}):

$$H(e^{j\pi/3}) = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{2} e^{-j\pi/3}} = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{2}\left(\cos\frac{\pi}{3} - j\sin\frac{\pi}{3}\right)} = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2} - j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)}$$

$$= \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{4} + j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}} = \frac{1}{\frac{3}{4} + j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}} = \frac{4}{3 + j\sqrt{3}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate (3 − j√3):

$$H(e^{j\pi/3}) = \frac{4(3 - j\sqrt{3})}{9 + 3} = \frac{4(3 - j\sqrt{3})}{12} = \frac{3 - j\sqrt{3}}{3} = 1 - j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$$

Magnitude and phase:

$$\vert H(e^{j\pi/3}) \vert = \sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{3}} = \sqrt{\frac{4}{3}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.155$$

$$\angle H(e^{j\pi/3}) = \arctan\left(\frac{-\sqrt{3}/3}{1}\right) = -\frac{\pi}{6}$$

Therefore, the steady-state response is:

$$y_{ss}[n] = \vert H(e^{j\pi/3}) \vert \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{3}n + \angle H(e^{j\pi/3})\right) = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{3}n - \frac{\pi}{6}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\,y_{ss}[n] = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{3}n - \frac{\pi}{6}\right)\,}$$

## 3.3 Important Multiplication Theorem (Convolution in Frequency ↔ Multiplication in Time)

Although less common in elementary LTI analysis, the dual property is:

$$\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h[k] \, x[n-k] \;\longleftrightarrow\; H(e^{j\omega}) X(e^{j\omega})$$

(modulation property: **multiplication in time** ↔ **periodic convolution in frequency**):

$$h[n] \cdot x[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} H(e^{j\theta}) X(e^{j(\omega-\theta)}) d\theta$$

This is a 2π-periodic convolution, not a simple product.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — DTFT-Based LTI System Analysis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal x of n] --> B[DTFT Block]
    B --> C[Spectrum X exp j omega]
    D[Impulse Response h of n] --> E[DTFT Block]
    E --> F[Frequency Response H exp j omega]
    C --> G[Multiplier Y equals H times X]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Spectrum Y exp j omega]
    H --> I[Inverse DTFT Block]
    I --> J[Output Signal y of n]
    F --> K[Magnitude Calculator]
    F --> L[Phase Calculator]
    K --> M[Plot magnitude vs omega]
    L --> N[Plot phase vs omega]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Eigenfunction Property Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Complex Exponential e raised to j omega0 n] --> B[LTI System h of n]
    B --> C[Output y of n equals H at e raised to j omega0 times e raised to j omega0 n]
    D[Coefficient H at e raised to j omega0] --> C
    subgraph SG1 [Magnitude Phase Split]
        E[Magnitude H at e raised to j omega0] --> F[Scales Amplitude]
        G[Phase H at e raised to j omega0] --> H[Adds Time Shift]
    end
    D --> SG1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Filter Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[LTI System H of e raised to j omega] --> B{Type}
    B -->|Magnitude Behaviour| C[Filter Class]
    C --> D[Low Pass Passes omega near 0]
    C --> E[High Pass Passes omega near pi]
    C --> F[Band Pass Passes omega1 to omega2]
    C --> G[Band Stop Rejects omega1 to omega2]
    B -->|Phase Behaviour| H[Phase Property]
    H --> I[Linear Phase Constant Group Delay]
    H --> J[Nonlinear Phase Distortion]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the frequency response of a discrete-time LTI system. Explain how it is obtained from the impulse response.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The frequency response H(e^{jω}) of a discrete-time LTI system is defined as the **DTFT of its impulse response h[n]**.

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h[n] \, e^{-j\omega n}$$

It is a complex-valued, **2π-periodic** function of the normalized angular frequency ω. It is obtained by applying the DTFT to h[n], which transforms the system characterization from the time domain (impulse response) to the frequency domain (gain and phase at every frequency). H(e^{jω}) completely characterizes the LTI system because, for any input x[n], the output spectrum is simply Y(e^{jω}) = H(e^{jω})·X(e^{jω}).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the convolution property of DTFT and its significance in analyzing LTI systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The convolution property of DTFT states that **convolution in the time domain corresponds to multiplication in the frequency domain**:

$$y[n] = h[n] * x[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; Y(e^{j\omega}) = H(e^{j\omega}) \, X(e^{j\omega})$$

**Significance in LTI analysis:**
1. It replaces the computationally expensive convolution sum with a simple pointwise multiplication.
2. It provides a direct way to compute the output spectrum without solving the difference equation.
3. It reveals the eigenfunction property — complex exponentials pass through the LTI system scaled by H(e^{jω}).
4. It forms the theoretical foundation for digital filter design — by specifying H(e^{jω}), we can engineer any desired magnitude and phase response.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme] — 14 Marks

**An LTI system is described by the difference equation y[n] − (1/2) y[n−1] = x[n].**

**(a)** Determine the impulse response h[n] and the frequency response H(e^{jω}) of the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the magnitude and phase response of the system. Plot |H(e^{jω})| for 0 ≤ ω ≤ π. Comment on the type of filter. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

For impulse response, set x[n] = δ[n] and assume zero initial conditions (causal system):

$$h[n] - \frac{1}{2} h[n-1] = \delta[n]$$

For n ≥ 0, with h[−1] = 0 (causality):

- **n = 0:** h[0] − (1/2)·0 = 1 → h[0] = 1
- **n = 1:** h[1] − (1/2)h[0] = 0 → h[1] = 1/2
- **n = 2:** h[2] = (1/2)h[1] = (1/2)²
- In general, h[n] = (1/2)^n for n ≥ 0, and 0 for n < 0.

$$\boxed{\,h[n] = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n u[n]\,}$$

Take DTFT of both sides of the difference equation (using linearity and time-shift property):

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) - \frac{1}{2} e^{-j\omega} Y(e^{j\omega}) = X(e^{j\omega})$$

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) \left[1 - \frac{1}{2} e^{-j\omega}\right] = X(e^{j\omega})$$

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{Y(e^{j\omega})}{X(e^{j\omega})} = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{2} e^{-j\omega}}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Magnitude response**:

$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{1}{\left|1 - \frac{1}{2} e^{-j\omega}\right|} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1 - \frac{1}{2}\cos\omega\right)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{2}\sin\omega\right)^2}}$$

Simplify the denominator (as done in Section 3.2):

$$\left(1 - \frac{1}{2}\cos\omega\right)^2 + \frac{1}{4}\sin^2\omega = \frac{5}{4} - \cos\omega$$

$$\boxed{\,\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\frac{5}{4} - \cos\omega}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{5 - 4\cos\omega}}\,}$$

**Phase response**:

$$\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = -\arctan\left(\frac{\frac{1}{2}\sin\omega}{1 - \frac{1}{2}\cos\omega}\right)$$

**Numerical values for plotting** (sample 5 points):

| ω | 0 | π/4 | π/2 | 3π/4 | π |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| cos ω | 1 | 0.707 | 0 | −0.707 | −1 |
| 5 − 4 cos ω | 1 | 2.172 | 5 | 7.828 | 9 |
| \vert H \vert | 2.000 | 1.364 | 0.894 | 0.715 | 0.667 |
| ∠H (deg) | 0 | −19.1 | −30.0 | −35.8 | −38.9 |

**Plot description (0 ≤ ω ≤ π)**:
- \vert H \vert starts at 2 at ω = 0 (DC).
- Monotonically decreases to 2/3 ≈ 0.667 at ω = π (Nyquist).
- The phase is monotonically more negative.

**Comment**: Since the magnitude is **maximum at ω = 0 and minimum at ω = π**, the system **passes low frequencies with high gain and attenuates high frequencies**. Hence it is a **low-pass filter**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-A)**:
> - **1 mark lost** if students forget the **2π-periodicity** statement of H(e^{jω}).
> - **2 marks lost** if the rationalization step (multiplying by conjugate) is skipped, leaving the answer in un-simplified form.
> - **1 mark lost** for not explicitly identifying the filter type (LPF/HPF/BPF/BSF) and giving a numerical example.
> - Always verify the **DC gain** (H(e^{j0}) = sum of h[n]) as a consistency check.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Alternative Module Choice] — 14 Marks

**A causal LTI system has impulse response h[n] = δ[n] + 2δ[n−1] + δ[n−2].**

**(a)** Find the frequency response H(e^{jω}) and the magnitude response |H(e^{jω})|. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the response y[n] of this system to the input x[n] = 1 + 2cos(πn/2) using the eigenfunction property. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Apply DTFT to h[n]. Using the linearity and time-shift properties of DTFT:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = 1 + 2e^{-j\omega} + e^{-j2\omega}$$

Recognize this as a **perfect square** of (1 + e^{−jω}):

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = (1 + e^{-j\omega})^2$$

Factor out e^{−jω} from the inner bracket to expose the linear-phase structure:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \left(e^{-j\omega/2}\right)^2 \left(e^{j\omega/2} + e^{-j\omega/2}\right)^2 = e^{-j\omega} \left(2\cos\frac{\omega}{2}\right)^2$$

$$\boxed{\,H(e^{j\omega}) = 4\cos^2\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) e^{-j\omega}\,}$$

Therefore:
- **Magnitude response**: \vert H(e^{jω}) \vert = 4 cos²(ω/2)
- **Phase response**: ∠H(e^{jω}) = −ω (linear phase, with cos² term being non-negative so contributes 0 phase)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The input x[n] = 1 + 2cos(πn/2) has two components:
1. A **DC component** of amplitude 1 at frequency ω = 0.
2. A **sinusoid** of amplitude 2 at frequency ω = π/2.

By linearity, the total output is the sum of the responses to each component.

**Component 1: x₁[n] = 1 = e^{j0·n}**
- Evaluate H(e^{j0}): \vert H(e^{j0}) \vert = 4 cos²(0) = 4, ∠H(e^{j0}) = 0.
- Output: y₁[n] = 1 · 4 · cos(0·n + 0) = **4**.

**Component 2: x₂[n] = 2cos(πn/2)**
- Evaluate H(e^{jπ/2}): \vert H(e^{jπ/2}) \vert = 4 cos²(π/4) = 4 · (1/√2)² = 4 · (1/2) = **2**.
- Phase: ∠H(e^{jπ/2}) = −π/2.
- Output: y₂[n] = 2 · 2 · cos((π/2)n − π/2) = 4 cos((π/2)n − π/2).

Using the identity cos(θ − π/2) = sin(θ):
$$y_2[n] = 4\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{2}n\right)$$

**Total response** (by linearity):

$$\boxed{\,y[n] = 4 + 4\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2}n\right)\,}$$

**Verification of filter type**: Since |H(e^{j0})| = 4 (max) and |H(e^{jπ})| = 4 cos²(π/2) = 0, this is a **low-pass filter** that completely nullifies the highest frequency (Nyquist).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-B)**:
> - **2 marks lost** if students forget the **linearity property** when the input has multiple frequency components (treat each one separately, then sum).
> - **1 mark lost** if the **linear-phase form** is not extracted correctly. Always factor out e^{−jω·(delay)}.
> - **1 mark lost** for missing the perfect-square structure (1 + e^{−jω})² — this is what makes the magnitude response simplify so elegantly.
> - **Common mistake**: Computing |1 + 2e^{−jω} + e^{−j2ω}| as 1 + 2 + 1 = 4 directly. This is WRONG; magnitude doesn't distribute over addition.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for DTFT-based LTI Analysis**

- ✅ **Frequency response H(e^{jω}) is the DTFT of h[n]** — memorize this fact; it's the foundation of Module 3.
- ✅ **Always 2π-periodic** in ω — because DTFT inherently lives on a discrete-time torus.
- ✅ **Convolution theorem** — y[n] = h[n] * x[n] ↔ Y(e^{jω}) = H(e^{jω}) X(e^{jω}) — never confuse the two directions.
- ✅ **Eigenfunction property** — complex exponentials are eigenfunctions of every LTI system with eigenvalue H(e^{jω}).
- ✅ **BIBO stability ⇔ H(e^{jω}) exists and bounded** for all ω — equivalent to Σ \vert h[n] \vert < ∞.
- ✅ **For real h[n]**: magnitude response is **even**, phase response is **odd** — quick sanity check.
- ✅ **Sinusoidal steady-state response** — y_ss[n] = A \vert H(e^{jω₀}) \vert cos(ω₀n + φ + ∠H(e^{jω₀})).
- ✅ **Filter type** is determined by where \vert H(e^{jω}) \vert is large:
  - Large near ω = 0 → **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)**
  - Large near ω = π → **High-Pass Filter (HPF)**
  - Large in middle band → **Band-Pass Filter (BPF)**
  - Small in middle band → **Band-Stop Filter (BSF)**
- ✅ **Linear phase** corresponds to a **constant group delay** — no phase distortion (important for audio/image processing).
- ✅ **Difference-equation shortcut** — for systems of the form Σ aₖ y[n−k] = Σ bₖ x[n−k], H(e^{jω}) = Σ bₖ e^{−jωk} / Σ aₖ e^{−jωk} — saves time on the exam.
- ✅ **Never confuse** DTFT (continuous in ω, periodic 2π) with DFT (discrete, periodic N). DTFT analysis is the theoretical base; DFT is the computational tool.
- ✅ **Geometric series** trick: Σ (a)^n for n = 0 to ∞ = 1/(1−a) if \vert a \vert < 1 — appears in nearly every h[n] problem involving exponentials.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Z-Transform: Mapping of s-plane to Z-plane, Inverse Transform, and Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT403 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Z-Transform: Mapping of s-plane to Z-plane, Inverse Transform, and Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — Z-TRANSFORM: s-PLANE TO z-PLANE MAPPING, INVERSE TRANSFORM & PROPERTIES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Z-Transform (Bilateral / Two-Sided):**
> For a discrete-time signal $x[n]$, the **Z-Transform** is formally defined as the bilateral summation:
> $$X(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{x[n]\} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[n]\; z^{-n}$$
> where $z$ is a complex variable of the form $z = re^{j\Omega}$, and the transform exists only for those values of $z$ for which the series converges — this region is called the **Region of Convergence (ROC)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Unilateral Z-Transform (commonly used in KTU problems):**
> $$X(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{x[n]\} = \sum_{n=0}^{+\infty} x[n]\; z^{-n}$$
> This variant assumes the signal is **causal** ($x[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$), which is the standard assumption in B.Tech examination problems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the **s-plane** as a vast, continuous ocean representing all possible **analog signal behaviors** — every point in that ocean describes how a continuous waveform grows, decays, or oscillates. The **z-plane**, on the other hand, is a **discrete, sampled snapshot** of that ocean — a coordinate system designed specifically for digital (sampled) signals.

> 🌊 **Analogy:** Imagine dipping a ladle into the ocean at regular intervals. Each scoop captures the water level at that instant. The s-plane describes the entire continuous water body, while the z-plane is the ledger of all your scoops. The mathematical relationship $z = e^{sT}$ is essentially the **rule that translates "ocean coordinates" into "ladle coordinates."**

The critical insight for KTU exams: **the entire s-plane is "wrapped" around the z-plane's unit circle** through exponential mapping. Stability, frequency response, and pole locations all get transformed — making the z-plane the natural playground for digital signal processing.

### 1.3 Standard Notation & Physical Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Parameters (must be memorized):**
> - **Sampling Period:** $T$ (in seconds) — time gap between two consecutive samples.
> - **Sampling Frequency:** $f_s = 1/T$ (in Hz).
> - **Sampling Angular Frequency:** $\Omega_s = 2\pi f_s = 2\pi / T$ (in rad/sample).
> - **Digital Angular Frequency:** $\omega$ (in rad/sample) — the discrete equivalent of analog $\Omega$.
> - **Unit Circle:** The locus $|z| = 1$ in the z-plane, corresponding to $s = j\omega$ (the imaginary axis) in the s-plane.

### 1.4 Mapping Function — The Heart of the Topic

The fundamental bridge between the continuous-time s-plane and the discrete-time z-plane is:

$$z = e^{sT}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** The Laplace transform of a sampled signal $x_s(t) = \sum_n x[n]\,\delta(t - nT)$ is $\sum_n x[n] e^{-snT}$. Substituting $z = e^{sT}$ gives $\sum_n x[n] z^{-n}$ — the Z-Transform. Thus, the Z-Transform is the **Laplace transform of a sampled signal evaluated on the substitution** $z = e^{sT}$.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Mapping Logic

Let $s = \sigma + j\omega$ (general point in the s-plane). Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z &= e^{sT} = e^{(\sigma + j\omega)T} \\
  &= e^{\sigma T} \cdot e^{j\omega T}
\end{aligned}
$$

Writing $z$ in polar form $z = |z| e^{j\angle z}$:

**Magnitude (Radius) Condition:**
$$|z| = e^{\sigma T}$$

**Phase (Angle) Condition:**
$$\angle z = \omega T$$

### 2.2 Critical Mapping Rules (HIGH-YIELD for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 1 — Real Axis ($\sigma$-axis) Mapping:**
> The entire real axis of the s-plane ($\omega = 0$, varying $\sigma$) maps to the **positive real axis** of the z-plane.
> - $\sigma = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; |z| = 1$ (the point $z = 1$).
> - $\sigma > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; |z| > 1$ (outside the unit circle).
> - $\sigma < 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 0 < |z| < 1$ (inside the unit circle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 2 — Imaginary Axis ($j\omega$-axis) Mapping:**
> The imaginary axis ($\sigma = 0$, varying $\omega$) maps to the **unit circle** $|z| = 1$ in the z-plane.
> - As $\omega$ increases from $0$ to $\pi/T$, the point traces from $z = +1$ to $z = -1$ (upper half of unit circle).
> - As $\omega$ goes from $\pi/T$ to $2\pi/T$, the trace returns from $-1$ to $+1$ (lower half of unit circle).
> - The mapping is **periodic** with period $2\pi/T$ (this captures aliasing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 3 — Left Half Plane (LHP) to Interior:**
> The entire **Left Half of the s-plane** ($\sigma < 0$) maps to the **interior of the unit circle** ($|z| < 1$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 4 — Right Half Plane (RHP) to Exterior:**
> The entire **Right Half of the s-plane** ($\sigma > 0$) maps to the **exterior of the unit circle** ($|z| > 1$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Implication (Vital for KTU):** A **causal** discrete-time LTI system is **BIBO stable** if and only if **all poles lie strictly inside the unit circle** ($|z| < 1$). This is the z-plane equivalent of the s-plane "all poles in LHP" rule.

### 2.3 Strip Mapping (Critical Concept for KTU)

The vertical strip in the s-plane of width $2\pi/T$ (i.e., $-\pi/T \leq \omega \leq \pi/T$) maps **one-to-one and onto** the entire z-plane. This is known as the **principal strip**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Strip: } \quad &-\frac{\pi}{T} \leq \omega \leq +\frac{\pi}{T} \\
\text{Maps to: } \quad &\text{Entire z-plane}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Property / Concept** | **Mathematical Statement** | **KTU Significance** |
|:-----:|:----------------------|:---------------------------|:---------------------|
| 1 | Mapping Equation | $z = e^{sT}$ | Core bridge between planes |
| 2 | Magnitude Mapping | $\vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T}$ | Determines stability regions |
| 3 | Phase Mapping | $\angle z = \omega T$ | Determines frequency folding |
| 4 | LHP $\to$ Interior | $\sigma < 0 \;\Leftrightarrow\; \vert z \vert < 1$ | Stability criterion |
| 5 | $j\omega$-axis $\to$ Unit Circle | $\sigma = 0 \;\Leftrightarrow\; \vert z \vert = 1$ | Frequency response locus |
| 6 | Bilateral Z-Transform | $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,z^{-n}$ | Full two-sided definition |
| 7 | Unilateral Z-Transform | $X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n]\,z^{-n}$ | For causal signals |
| 8 | Standard Pairs: $\delta[n]$ | $X(z) = 1$ (ROC: all $z$) | Impulse |
| 9 | Standard Pairs: $u[n]$ | $X(z) = \dfrac{z}{z-1}$ (ROC: $\vert z \vert > 1$) | Unit step |
| 10 | Standard Pairs: $a^n u[n]$ | $X(z) = \dfrac{z}{z-a}$ (ROC: $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$) | Causal exponential |
| 11 | Standard Pairs: $-a^n u[-n-1]$ | $X(z) = \dfrac{z}{z-a}$ (ROC: $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$) | Anti-causal exponential |
| 12 | Linearity | $\mathcal{Z}\{a\,x[n] + b\,y[n]\} = aX(z) + bY(z)$ | ROC $\supseteq$ ROC$_x \cap$ ROC$_y$ |
| 13 | Time Shifting | $\mathcal{Z}\{x[n - n_0]\} = z^{-n_0} X(z)$ | Delay/advance |
| 14 | Time Reversal | $\mathcal{Z}\{x[-n]\} = X(1/z)$ | ROC is inverted |
| 15 | Multiplication by $a^n$ | $\mathcal{Z}\{a^n x[n]\} = X(z/a)$ | Frequency scaling |
| 16 | Differentiation in z | $\mathcal{Z}\{n\,x[n]\} = -z\,\dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$ | Used for $n\,a^n$ |
| 17 | Convolution | $\mathcal{Z}\{x[n] * y[n]\} = X(z) Y(z)$ | LTI system response |
| 18 | Multiplication (Complex) | $\mathcal{Z}\{x[n]\,y[n]\} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi j}\oint X(v)\,Y(z/v)\,\dfrac{dv}{v}$ | Windowing property |
| 19 | Initial Value Theorem | $x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ | For causal signals |
| 20 | Final Value Theorem | $\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1)\,X(z)$ | Requires poles inside UC except possibly at $z=1$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The s-plane $\to$ z-plane mapping is **the** foundational tool behind:
- **Digital Filter Design:** Converting analog Butterworth, Chebyshev, and Bessel filter prototypes into digital IIR filters.
- **Bilinear Transformation:** The famous $s = \frac{2}{T}\cdot\frac{z-1}{z+1}$ is itself derived from the $z = e^{sT}$ relationship.
- **Sampled Data Control Systems:** Stability of digital controllers is analyzed purely in the z-plane.
- **DSP Processors & Communication Systems:** Every digital equalizer, noise canceller, and codec relies on z-plane analysis.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Mapping Function $z = e^{sT}$

Starting from the **sampled signal** representation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_s(t) &= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\;\delta(t - nT) \\[4pt]
\text{Laplace Transform: }\quad X_s(s) &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_s(t)\,e^{-st}\,dt \\[4pt]
&= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\;\delta(t - nT)\right] e^{-st}\,dt \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT)\,e^{-st}\,dt \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\;e^{-snT} \quad \text{(using the sifting property of }\delta\text{)} \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\;\left(e^{sT}\right)^{-n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Comparing with the Z-Transform definition $X(z) = \sum x[n]\,z^{-n}$, we make the substitution:

$$\boxed{\,z = e^{sT}\,}$$

This is the **defining equation** that maps the s-plane into the z-plane.

### 3.2 Derivation: Magnitude and Phase Conditions

Let $s = \sigma + j\omega$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z &= e^{(\sigma + j\omega)T} \\[4pt]
  &= e^{\sigma T}\,e^{j\omega T}
\end{aligned}
$$

In polar form $z = r\,e^{j\theta}$:

$$
\boxed{\,r = \vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T}\quad \text{and} \quad \theta = \angle z = \omega T\,}
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- If $\sigma > 0$ (RHP), then $r > 1$ (outside unit circle).
- If $\sigma = 0$ ($j\omega$-axis), then $r = 1$ (on unit circle).
- If $\sigma < 0$ (LHP), then $0 < r < 1$ (inside unit circle).

### 3.3 Derivation: Primary Strip and Aliasing

The frequency $\omega$ in the analog domain is **infinite** (extends from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$). However, in the z-plane, the angle $\theta = \omega T$ is computed modulo $2\pi$. So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \omega T \\[4pt]
\Delta\theta &= \Delta\omega \cdot T
\end{aligned}
$$

For $\Delta\omega = 2\pi/T$, we get $\Delta\theta = 2\pi$ — a **full circle**. This means the segment

$$-\frac{\pi}{T} \leq \omega \leq \frac{\pi}{T}$$

maps to the **entire unit circle** exactly once. Each subsequent strip of width $2\pi/T$ overlaps the previous one — this is the **aliasing** phenomenon in the frequency domain.

### 3.4 Inverse Z-Transform Methods (Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU requires mastery of three inverse Z-transform methods.** Below is the analytical procedure for each.

#### **Method 1: Long Division (Power Series Method)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Used when the Z-transform is a **rational polynomial** and you need the first few samples directly.

**Procedure:**
1. Arrange $X(z) = \dfrac{N(z)}{D(z)}$ such that both numerator and denominator are in **descending powers of $z^{-1}$** (or ascending powers of $z$).
2. Perform **polynomial long division** of numerator by denominator.
3. The resulting coefficients of $z^{-n}$ are the samples $x[n]$.

**Worked Example:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(z) &= \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} \quad \text{for } \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert \\[6pt]
&= 1 + a z^{-1} + a^2 z^{-2} + a^3 z^{-3} + \cdots \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow\;\; x[n] &= a^n u[n]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Division verification (manual long division):**
> Divide $1$ by $(1 - a z^{-1})$:
> - $1 \div (1 - a z^{-1})$: First term $= 1$, remainder $= a z^{-1}$.
> - $a z^{-1} \div (1 - a z^{-1})$: Next term $= a z^{-1}$, remainder $= a^2 z^{-2}$.
> - Continuing gives $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} a^k z^{-k}$.

#### **Method 2: Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most commonly tested** method in KTU. It works when $X(z)$ is a **proper rational function**.

**Procedure:**
1. Write $X(z)$ in the form $\dfrac{N(z)}{D(z)}$.
2. Express as $\dfrac{X(z)}{z} = \sum \dfrac{A_k}{z - p_k}$ where $p_k$ are the poles.
3. Find residues: $A_k = \left.\dfrac{N(z)}{D'(z)}\right|_{z = p_k}$ or via cover-up.
4. Multiply back by $z$: $X(z) = \sum A_k \cdot \dfrac{z}{z - p_k}$.
5. Invert term-by-term using standard pairs.

**Worked Example:**

$$
X(z) = \frac{z}{(z-1)(z-2)}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 2
$$

**Step 1:** Compute $\dfrac{X(z)}{z}$:

$$
\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{1}{(z-1)(z-2)}
$$

**Step 2:** Partial fractions:

$$
\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{A_1}{z-1} + \frac{A_2}{z-2}
$$

**Step 3:** Find residues:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= \left.\frac{1}{z-2}\right|_{z=1} = \frac{1}{1-2} = -1 \\[4pt]
A_2 &= \left.\frac{1}{z-1}\right|_{z=2} = \frac{1}{2-1} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by $z$:

$$
X(z) = \frac{-z}{z-1} + \frac{z}{z-2}
$$

**Step 5:** Invert term-by-term (ROC $|z| > 2$ implies causal, right-sided sequences):

$$
\boxed{\,x[n] = \left(-1 + 2^n\right) u[n]\,}
$$

#### **Method 3: Residue Method (Inverse via Contour Integration)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula:**
> $$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_C X(z)\,z^{n-1}\,dz = \sum_{\text{poles } p_k \text{ inside } C} \text{Res}\left[X(z)\,z^{n-1},\, p_k\right]$$

**Worked Example:** $X(z) = \dfrac{z^2 + z}{(z-1)(z-2)}$, find $x[n]$ for $n \geq 0$ (causal).

$$
X(z)\,z^{n-1} = \frac{(z^2 + z)\,z^{n-1}}{(z-1)(z-2)} = \frac{z^{n+1} + z^n}{(z-1)(z-2)}
$$

Poles inside the contour (assuming $|z| > 2$ for causality): $z = 1$ and $z = 2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Res at } z=1: \quad R_1 &= \left.\frac{z^{n+1} + z^n}{z-2}\right|_{z=1} = \frac{1 + 1}{1 - 2} = \frac{2}{-1} = -2 \\[4pt]
\text{Res at } z=2: \quad R_2 &= \left.\frac{z^{n+1} + z^n}{z-1}\right|_{z=2} = \frac{2^{n+1} + 2^n}{2 - 1} = 2^{n+1} + 2^n
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,x[n] = -2 + 2^{n+1} + 2^n = -2 + 3 \cdot 2^n \quad \text{for } n \geq 0\,}
$$

### 3.5 Proof of Initial Value Theorem

For a causal signal, $X(z) = x[0] + x[1]z^{-1} + x[2]z^{-2} + \cdots$

Taking the limit as $z \to \infty$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{z \to \infty} X(z) &= \lim_{z \to \infty}\left[x[0] + x[1]z^{-1} + x[2]z^{-2} + \cdots\right] \\[4pt]
&= x[0] + 0 + 0 + \cdots \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{\,x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.6 Proof of Final Value Theorem

Assuming $\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n]$ exists:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(z-1)X(z) &= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n]\,z^{1-n} - \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n]\,z^{-n} \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n]\,z^{-(n-1)} - \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n]\,z^{-n} \\[4pt]
&= x[0]\,z + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} x[n]\,z^{1-n} - \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n]\,z^{-n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking $\lim_{z \to 1}$ after multiplying by $(1 - z^{-1})$ and simplifying (using the fact that $z^{1-n} - z^{-n} = z^{-n}(z-1)$):

$$
\boxed{\,\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1)\,X(z)\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Applicability condition for FVT:** All poles of $(z-1)X(z)$ must lie **strictly inside the unit circle** (except possibly a simple pole at $z = 1$). Otherwise, the limit does not exist or is infinite.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 s-Plane to z-Plane Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["s-Plane\nsigma + j omega"]
    nodeB["Substitution\nz = e to power sT"]
    nodeC["z-Plane\nre to power j theta"]
    nodeD["LHP\nsigma less than 0"]

```
