KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** > **Q:** State the **Whittaker-Shannon interpolation formula** for reconstructing a bandlimited continuous-time signal $x(t)$ from its samples $x[n] = x(nT_s)$. Under what conditions is the reconstruction exact? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The Whittaker-Shannon interpolation formula is: $$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\frac{\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi(t - nT_s)}{T_s}\right)}{\frac{\pi(t - nT_s)}{T_s}}$$ **Conditions for exact reconstruction:** 1. The original signal $x(t)$ must be **strictly bandlimited** to a maximum frequency $\omega_m$ (i.e., $X(j\omega) = 0$ for $\vert\omega\vert > \omega_m$). 2. The sampling frequency must satisfy the **Nyquist criterion**: $F_s \geq 2F_m$ (or $\omega_s \geq 2\omega_m$). 3. The reconstruction filter must be an **ideal lowpass filter** with gain $T_s$ and cutoff $\omega_c = \omega_m = \pi/T_s$. **Scoring (3 Marks):** Formula = 1 Mark; Condition 1 (bandlimited) = 1 Mark; Conditions 2 & 3 (Nyquist + ideal LPF) = 1 Mark. --- ### Question A2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Series 1]** > **Q:** What is a **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)**? Write its impulse response and frequency response. Mention one limitation. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** is a practical reconstruction device that holds the value of each sample $x[n]$ constant over the entire sampling interval $T_s$, producing a **staircase waveform**. **Impulse response:** $$h_{ZOH}(t) = u(t) - u(t - T_s) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \leq t < T_s \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ **Frequency response:** $$H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = T_s \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega T_s/2)}{\omega T_s/2} \cdot e^{-j\omega T_s/2} = T_s \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega T_s}{2\pi}\right) \cdot e^{-j\omega T_s/2}$$ **Limitation:** The $\text{sinc}$ envelope causes **spectral droop** (high-frequency attenuation of about **−3.92 dB** at $\omega = \omega_s/2$) and the output is delayed by $T_s/2$ (linear phase but not zero phase). **Scoring (3 Marks):** Definition = 1 Mark; Impulse response = 1 Mark; Frequency response + limitation = 1 Mark. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **[KTU ESE Pattern: Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Solve ANY ONE of the two choices provided below.]** --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Paper] > **(a)** Starting from the impulse-train representation of discrete samples, derive the **Whittaker-Shannon interpolation formula** for reconstructing a bandlimited continuous-time signal $x(t)$ from its samples $x(nT_s)$. Clearly state the assumptions and the role of the ideal lowpass filter. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency component $F_m = 5$ kHz is sampled at $F_s = 12$ kHz. >     (i) Verify whether the Nyquist criterion is satisfied. >     (ii) Determine the cutoff frequency of the ideal reconstruction filter. >     (iii) Write the expression for the reconstructed signal in terms of sinc functions. >     (iv) Find the reconstructed value at $t = T_s/4$ if $x(0) = 2$, $x(T_s) = -1$, and all other samples are zero. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** ### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks) **Assumptions:** - $x(t)$ is bandlimited: $X(j\omega) = 0$ for $\vert\omega\vert > \omega_m$. - Sampling frequency $F_s \geq 2F_m$ (Nyquist criterion satisfied). **Step 1:** Express the discrete samples as an impulse train: $$\tilde{x}_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,\delta(t - nT_s)$$ **Step 2:** Take the Fourier transform: $$\tilde{X}_s(j\omega) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,e^{-j\omega nT_s} = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X\!\left(j(\omega - k\omega_s)\right)$$ **Step 3:** Since $F_s \geq 2F_m$, the spectral replicas do not overlap. The baseband copy (k=0) contains $X(j\omega)/T_s$. To extract it, we apply a **lowpass filter**: $$H_r(j\omega) = \begin{cases} T_s, & \vert\omega\vert \leq \omega_m \\ 0, & \vert\omega\vert > \omega_m \end{cases}$$ **Step 4:** Multiply in frequency domain (equivalent to convolution in time domain): $$X(j\omega) = \tilde{X}_s(j\omega) \cdot H_r(j\omega)$$ **Step 5:** The impulse response of the ideal lowpass filter is the sinc function: $$h_r(t) = \frac{\sin(\pi t/T_s)}{\pi t/T_s} = \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t}{T_s}\right)$$ **Step 6:** Convolution in time domain: $$x(t) = \tilde{x}_s(t) * h_r(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\tilde{x}_s(\tau)\,h_r(t-\tau)\,d\tau$$ **Step 7:** Substitute impulse train and use sifting property: $$x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,\delta(\tau - nT_s)\,h_r(t-\tau)\,d\tau = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,h_r(t - nT_s)$$ **Step 8:** Final formula: $$\boxed{\;x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)\;}$$ **Mark Distribution (7 Marks):** - Impulse train + Fourier transform = 1.5 Marks - Spectral replication statement = 1 Mark - Ideal LPF specification = 1.5 Marks - Sinc impulse response derivation = 1 Mark - Convolution and sifting step = 1 Mark - Final Whittaker-Shannon formula = 1 Mark --- ### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks) **(i) Nyquist Verification (1.5 Marks):** Nyquist rate $= 2F_m = 2 \times 5 = 10$ kHz. Given $F_s = 12$ kHz. Since $F_s = 12 \text{ kHz} > 10 \text{ kHz} = 2F_m$, the **Nyquist criterion is satisfied**. No aliasing will occur. **(ii) Cutoff Frequency (1.5 Marks):** $$\omega_c = \omega_m = 2\pi F_m = 2\pi \times 5000 = 10000\pi \text{ rad/sec}$$ or equivalently, $f_c = F_m = 5$ kHz. **(iii) Reconstructed Signal Expression (2 Marks):** With $T_s = 1/F_s = 1/12000$ sec: $$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,\text{sinc}\!\left(12000\,(t - n/12000)\right) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,\frac{\sin(12000\pi(t - nT_s))}{12000\pi(t - nT_s)}$$ **(iv) Reconstruction at $t = T_s/4$ (2 Marks):** With only $x[0] = 2$ and $x[1] = -1$ nonzero: $$x(T_s/4) = 2 \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{T_s/4}{T_s}\right) + (-1) \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{T_s/4 - T_s}{T_s}\right)$$ $$= 2 \cdot \text{sinc}(1/4) + (-1) \cdot \text{sinc}(-3/4)$$ Since $\text{sinc}$ is an even function, $\text{sinc}(-3/4) = \text{sinc}(3/4)$: $$= 2 \cdot \text{sinc}(1/4) - \text{sinc}(3/4)$$ Computing: $$\text{sinc}(1/4) = \frac{\sin(\pi/4)}{\pi/4} = \frac{\sqrt{2}/2}{\pi/4} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \approx 0.9003$$ $$\text{sinc}(3/4) = \frac{\sin(3\pi/4)}{3\pi/4} = \frac{\sqrt{2}/2}{3\pi/4} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{3\pi} \approx 0.3001$$ $$x(T_s/4) \approx 2(0.9003) - 0.3001 = 1.8006 - 0.3001 = 1.5005 \approx 1.50$$ **Mark Distribution:** Nyquist check (1.5) + Cutoff (1.5) + Expression (2) + Numerical eval (2) = 7 Marks. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Supplementary] > **(a)** Explain the **practical reconstruction** of a continuous-time signal from its samples using a **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)**. Derive its frequency response from its impulse response and explain why an additional **smoothing filter** is required after the ZOH. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the complete process of **discrete-time signal processing** including sampling, processing, and reconstruction. Compare **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** and **First-Order Hold (FOH)** reconstruction methods. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** ### Part (a) — ZOH Derivation (7 Marks) **Concept (1 Mark):** A ZOH holds each sample value constant for the duration of one sampling period, producing a staircase approximation of the original signal. **Impulse Response (1 Mark):** $$h_{ZOH}(t) = u(t) - u(t - T_s) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \leq t < T_s \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ **Fourier Transform Derivation (3 Marks):** $$H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = \int_0^{T_s} e^{-j\omega t}\,dt = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega T_s}}{j\omega}$$ Factor out $e^{-j\omega T_s/2}$: $$H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = \frac{e^{-j\omega T_s/2}(e^{j\omega T_s/2} - e^{-j\omega T_s/2})}{j\omega} = \frac{2j\sin(\omega T_s/2)\,e^{-j\omega T_s/2}}{j\omega}$$ $$\boxed{\;H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = T_s \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega T_s/2)}{\omega T_s/2} \cdot e^{-j\omega T_s/2}\;}$$ **Why a Smoothing Filter is Needed (2 Marks):** The ZOH spectrum contains: - The desired baseband signal, scaled by $\text{sinc}(\omega T_s/2\pi)$. - Infinite spectral replicas at multiples of $\omega_s$. The $\text{sinc}$ droop attenuates high frequencies within the baseband (≈ −3.92 dB at $\omega_m$). To remove the spectral replicas and partially equalize the droop, an **analog reconstruction lowpass filter** (smoothing filter) is cascaded. Ideally: $$H_{LPF}(j\omega) = \begin{cases} 1/T_s \cdot \text{sinc}^{-1}(\omega T_s/2\pi), & \vert\omega\vert \leq \omega_m \\ 0, & \vert\omega\vert > \omega_m \end{cases}$$ The $\text{sinc}^{-1}$ term is an **equalizer** that compensates for the ZOH droop. --- ### Part (b) — Complete System & Comparison (7 Marks) **Block Diagram (3 Marks):** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.) ``` x(t) → Anti-Alias LPF → ADC (Sampler + Quantizer) → DSP/Storage → DAC (ZOH/FOH) → Reconstruction LPF → x̂(t) ``` **Role of Each Block:** - **Anti-aliasing LPF** limits $x(t)$ to $F_m < F_s/2$. - **ADC** samples and quantizes. - **DSP** performs digital operations (filtering, modulation, etc.). - **DAC** converts discrete to continuous using ZOH/FOH. - **Reconstruction LPF** smooths the staircase output. **ZOH vs FOH Comparison Table (4 Marks):** | Feature | ZOH | FOH | |---------|-----|-----| | Output | Staircase | Piecewise linear | | Impulse response | $u(t) - u(t-T_s)$ | Triangular pulse of width $2T_s$ | | Frequency response | $T_s \cdot \text{sinc}(\omega T_s/2\pi) \cdot e^{-j\omega T_s/2}$ | $T_s \cdot \text{sinc}^2(\omega T_s/2\pi) \cdot e^{-j\omega T_s}$ | | Smoothing | More aggressive $\text{sinc}$ droop | Less in-band droop, but sharper nulls | | Spectral replicas | First null at $\omega = 2\pi/T_s$ | First null at $\omega = \pi/T_s$ (worse stopband) | | Hardware cost | Cheapest (1 flip-flop per channel) | More expensive (2 ZOH + subtractor) | | Phase delay | $T_s/2$ | $T_s$ | | KTU-typical use | Most DACs, control outputs | Function generators, displays | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the $T_s$ gain in the reconstruction filter** — Many students write $H_r(j\omega) = 1$ for $\vert\omega\vert \leq \omega_m$. The correct gain is $T_s$ to compensate for the $1/T_s$ scaling in the impulse train spectrum. **Penalty: −1 Mark.** > > 2. **Confusing $h_r(t)$ and $H_r(j\omega)$** — Students mix up the time-domain sinc and the frequency-domain rectangular shape. The sinc is in **time**, the rectangle is in **frequency**. **Penalty: −1 Mark.** > > 3. **Missing the assumption of strict bandlimitation** — The Whittaker-Shannon formula gives *exact* reconstruction only if $X(j\omega) = 0$ for $\vert\omega\vert > \omega_m$. Real-world signals are never strictly bandlimited. **Penalty: −0.5 to −1 Mark.** > > 4. **Ignoring the linear phase $e^{-j\omega T_s/2}$ in ZOH** — Examiners specifically test this. Write the full complex frequency response, not just the magnitude. **Penalty: −1 Mark.** > > 5. **Confusing reconstruction with anti-aliasing filtering** — Anti-aliasing is done **before** sampling (input side); reconstruction filtering is done **after** sampling (output side). **Penalty: −1 Mark.** > > 6. **Misapplying the Nyquist rate in the wrong units** — Use Hz for $F_s$ and rad/sec for $\omega_s$. Mixing them = **−0.5 Mark.** --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 (Reconstruction):** > > ✅ The **sampling theorem** guarantees perfect reconstruction **only** for bandlimited signals sampled at $F_s \geq 2F_m$. > > ✅ The **reconstruction filter** is an **ideal lowpass filter** with gain $\mathbf{T_s}$ and cutoff $\boldsymbol{\omega_c = \pi/T_s}$. > > ✅ The **Whittaker-Shannon interpolation formula** is the time-domain statement of perfect reconstruction: $x(t) = \sum x[n]\,\text{sinc}((t - nT_s)/T_s)$. > > ✅ The **sinc function** has zeros at all $t = nT_s$ for $n \neq 0$, ensuring each sample is reproduced exactly. > > ✅ The **impulse train representation** $\tilde{x}_s(t) = \sum x[n]\,\delta(t - nT_s)$ is the bridge between discrete samples and continuous-time convolution. > > ✅ **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** is the practical reconstruction method in real DACs; it produces a staircase output with $\text{sinc}$ droop in the frequency domain. > > ✅ **ZOH frequency response:** $H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = T_s \cdot \text{sinc}(\omega T_s/2\pi) \cdot e^{-j\omega T_s/2}$ — note the linear phase delay of $T_s/2$. > > ✅ **First-Order Hold (FOH)** linearly interpolates between consecutive samples; its frequency response has a $\text{sinc}^2$ envelope and phase delay of $T_s$. > > ✅ **Aliasing** occurs when $F_s < 2F_m$ and is **irreversible** — once spectral copies overlap, the original information is lost. > > ✅ A **smoothing (reconstruction) lowpass filter** is required after the ZOH/FOH to remove spectral replicas and improve in-band flatness. > > ✅ **Oversampling** ($F_s \gg 2F_m$) relaxes reconstruction filter design and is the standard approach in modern audio DACs (e.g., 192 kHz for 20 kHz audio). > > ✅ For $x(t)$ at sample instant $mT_s$: $x(mT_s) = x[m] \cdot \text{sinc}(0) = x[m]$ — reconstruction passes through every sample. > > ✅ **Reconstruction SNR penalty** at $\omega = \omega_m$ with ZOH (no equalizer) is **−3.92 dB** = $20\log_{10}(2/\pi)$. > > ✅ **Anti-aliasing filter (input) ≠ Reconstruction filter (output)** — both are lowpass, but serve different purposes and sit on different sides of the ADC/DAC. ---

topics.title: Interpolation Methods (Zero-Order Hold, First-Order Hold, Sinc Interpolation)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT403 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interpolation Methods (Zero-Order Hold, First-Order Hold, Sinc Interpolation)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Interpolation in Signals and Systems?

In the context of digital signal processing, **interpolation** is the process of reconstructing a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ from its discrete-time samples $x[n] = x(nT)$, where $T$ is the sampling period. Whenever we convert a discrete signal back into a continuous one (e.g., at the output of a Digital-to-Analog Converter or DAC), we must mathematically "fill in" the values between the discrete sample points. The method we use to perform this filling defines the **interpolation method**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Interpolation is the operation of reconstructing a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ from its samples $x(nT)$ using a linear time-invariant (LTI) system characterized by an impulse response $h(t)$, such that $x_r(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT) h(t - nT)$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Staircase, The Ramp, and The Smooth Curve

Imagine you are walking along a road and a friend only records your position at every 1-second interval (i.e., your positions at $t = 0, 1, 2, 3, ...$ seconds). When your friend comes back, they have to draw a path on a map between these recorded dots. There are three plausible ways to do this:

1.  **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) — The Staircase Path:** Your friend assumes you stood completely still at each recorded position until the next second. The path becomes a series of flat steps — a staircase.

2.  **First-Order Hold (FOH) — The Ramp Path:** Your friend assumes you walked in a perfectly straight line at a constant velocity between two recorded dots. The path becomes a series of ramps — piecewise linear.

3.  **Sinc Interpolation — The Smooth Curve Path:** Your friend (a mathematician) uses a special smooth, bell-shaped mathematical "curve-fitter" to perfectly reconstruct your exact path, assuming the path contained no frequencies higher than some maximum limit (bandlimited signal). This is the **mathematically ideal** reconstruction.

### 1.3 Why Do We Need Different Interpolation Methods?

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** No single interpolation method is universally "best." ZOH is physically realized in DAC hardware; FOH gives smoother visuals; Sinc interpolation is theoretically perfect for bandlimited signals but physically unrealizable. KTU examiners expect you to compare and contrast their **frequency responses**, **implementation complexity**, and **reconstruction fidelity**.

The three methods differ by the shape of their **reconstruction filter impulse response $h(t)$**, which convolves with the sampled signal.

| Interpolation Method | Impulse Response Shape | Practical Realizability | Reconstruction Quality |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Zero-Order Hold** | Rectangular pulse | Easy (DAC hardware) | Approximate, staircase-like |
| **First-Order Hold** | Triangular pulse | Moderate | Smoother, linear segments |
| **Sinc (Bandlimited)** | $\text{sinc}(t/T)$ function | Ideal/Not realizable | Perfect (for bandlimited signals) |

### 1.4 The Mathematics of Reconstruction — A Common Framework

All three methods can be expressed using the same elegant framework:

$$x_r(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT) \cdot h(t - nT)$$

where $h(t)$ is the **reconstruction filter's impulse response** unique to each method. The discrete samples $x(nT)$ act as scaled, time-shifted impulses, and $h(t)$ is the "shape" placed at each impulse.

In the **frequency domain**, this convolution becomes multiplication:

$$X_r(j\omega) = X_s(j\omega) \cdot H_r(j\omega)$$

where $X_s(j\omega)$ is the spectrum of the sampled signal (periodic replicas of the original spectrum) and $H_r(j\omega)$ is the frequency response of the reconstruction filter that ideally extracts only the baseband replica.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) — Detailed Analysis

#### 2.1.1 Concept
The **Zero-Order Hold** holds the value of each sample constant for an entire sample period $T$ until the next sample arrives. The output is a staircase-like approximation of the original signal. It is the **most common practical reconstruction method** because it is exactly how a Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) with a register-based output works.

#### 2.1.2 Impulse Response and Frequency Response

The ZOH has a rectangular impulse response of width $T$ and height 1:

$$h_{ZOH}(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \leq t < T \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Taking the Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT):

$$H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = \int_{0}^{T} (1) \cdot e^{-j\omega t} \, dt = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega T}}{j\omega}$$

Using the algebraic identity $1 - e^{-j\omega T} = e^{-j\omega T/2}(e^{j\omega T/2} - e^{-j\omega T/2}) = 2j e^{-j\omega T/2} \sin(\omega T / 2)$, we get the canonical form:

$$H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = T \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega T / 2)}{\omega T / 2} \cdot e^{-j\omega T / 2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Result:** The magnitude response of ZOH is a **sinc function**, $\vert H_{ZOH}(j\omega) \vert = T \cdot \vert \text{sinc}(\omega T / 2) \vert$, with zeros at $\omega = \pm 2\pi k / T$ for $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$. This sinc-shaped magnitude causes **amplitude distortion** (the high frequencies near $\pi/T$ are attenuated by the sinc envelope) — this distortion is called the **$\sin(x)/x$ roll-off** or **aperture effect**.

#### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of ZOH
- The sampled signal is treated as a train of weighted impulses: $x_s(t) = \sum_{n} x(nT) \delta(t - nT)$.
- Each impulse is passed through the ZOH filter, which responds with a rectangular pulse of width $T$ and height equal to the sample value.
- The overlapping rectangles sum together to form the staircase output.
- The filter introduces a half-sample delay ($T/2$ in time) — visible as the $e^{-j\omega T/2}$ phase term.

### 2.2 First-Order Hold (FOH) — Detailed Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Concept
The **First-Order Hold** (also called **Linear Interpolation**) connects two consecutive samples with a straight line. The output is piecewise linear, providing a smoother approximation than ZOH. It uses the current sample and predicts the next via a linear slope.

#### 2.2.2 Impulse Response and Frequency Response

The FOH has a **triangular impulse response**:

$$h_{FOH}(t) = \begin{cases} 1 - \frac{\vert t \vert}{T}, & \vert t \vert \leq T \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This triangle can be viewed as the **convolution of two rectangular pulses of width $T$**. Therefore, in the frequency domain:

$$H_{FOH}(j\omega) = \left[ H_{ZOH}(j\omega) \right]^2 = T \cdot \left[ \frac{\sin(\omega T / 2)}{\omega T / 2} \right]^2 \cdot e^{-j\omega T}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Result:** The magnitude response of FOH is a **sinc-squared** function, $\vert H_{FOH}(j\omega) \vert = T \cdot \text{sinc}^2(\omega T / 2)$. The roll-off is **steeper** than ZOH (squared sinc rolls off faster), but the **half-sample delay doubles** to $T$ (visible in the $e^{-j\omega T}$ phase term).

#### 2.2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of FOH
- The FOH filter is the cascade (convolution) of two ZOH filters — geometrically, convolving a rectangle with itself yields a triangle.
- It uses both the **previous** and the **next** sample to determine the slope of the linear segment, which is why it must wait for $n+1$ (introducing the full $T$ delay).
- The smoother frequency roll-off (sinc²) makes it a **better low-pass filter** at the cost of more phase delay.

### 2.3 Sinc Interpolation (Bandlimited / Ideal Reconstruction) — Detailed Analysis

#### 2.3.1 Concept
**Sinc interpolation**, also called the **Whittaker–Shannon Interpolation Formula**, is the **mathematically ideal** reconstruction method for a strictly bandlimited signal sampled at or above the Nyquist rate. It uses shifted $\text{sinc}(t/T)$ functions as the reconstruction basis.

#### 2.3.2 Impulse Response and Frequency Response

The ideal low-pass reconstruction filter has:

$$h_{SINC}(t) = \frac{\sin(\pi t / T)}{\pi t / T} = \text{sinc}\left(\frac{t}{T}\right)$$

with the ideal brick-wall frequency response:

$$H_{SINC}(j\omega) = \begin{cases} T, & \vert \omega \vert \leq \pi / T \\ 0, & \vert \omega \vert > \pi / T \end{cases}$$

The reconstructed signal is:

$$x_r(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT) \cdot \text{sinc}\left(\frac{t - nT}{T}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Result (Whittaker–Shannon Theorem):** If a signal $x(t)$ is **strictly bandlimited** to $\omega_m$ and sampled at $T < \pi / \omega_m$ (Nyquist criterion), the above sum **exactly equals** $x(t)$ for all $t$. This is the *only* interpolation method that provides **perfect** reconstruction.

#### 2.3.3 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Sinc Interpolation
- The sampled signal's spectrum is periodic: $X_s(j\omega) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k} X(j(\omega - k\omega_s))$.
- A perfect low-pass filter of gain $T$ and cutoff $\pi/T$ extracts exactly the $k=0$ replica.
- Multiplication in frequency ↔ convolution in time with $\text{sinc}(t/T)$.
- It is **not realizable** in practice because the sinc function is non-causal (extends from $t = -\infty$ to $+\infty$) and non-decaying.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) | First-Order Hold (FOH) | Sinc (Ideal) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Impulse Response $h(t)$** | Rectangle of width $T$, height 1 | Triangle of base $2T$, peak 1 | $\text{sinc}(t/T)$ (non-causal) |
| **Frequency Response $H(j\omega)$** | $T \cdot \text{sinc}(\omega T/2) \cdot e^{-j\omega T/2}$ | $T \cdot \text{sinc}^2(\omega T/2) \cdot e^{-j\omega T}$ | $T$ for $\vert \omega \vert \leq \pi/T$, $0$ otherwise |
| **Magnitude Roll-off** | $\sin(x)/x$ | $[\sin(x)/x]^2$ | Brick-wall (ideal) |
| **Time Delay** | $T/2$ | $T$ | $0$ |
| **Causality** | Causal (causal if shifted) | Causal (with delay) | **Non-causal** (unrealizable) |
| **Stability** | Stable (FIR) | Stable (FIR) | Marginally stable (non-decaying) |
| **Reconstruction Quality** | Approximate (staircase) | Smoother (linear) | **Perfect** (bandlimited only) |
| **Hardware Realizability** | Yes (standard DAC) | Possible (specialized) | No (infinite extent required) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ZOH** is the de-facto output stage in nearly all **commercial DACs** in audio cards, CD players, and microcontrollers. The "staircase" is then smoothed by an **analog reconstruction filter** (low-pass) to remove the higher-order spectral images.
- **FOH** is used in **real-time plotting software** (e.g., MATLAB default plot rendering) and **CAD tools** for visual smoothness, since linear segments look natural to the human eye.
- **Sinc interpolation** is the gold standard in **image scaling algorithms** (e.g., Photoshop bicubic is a windowed/truncated sinc), **medical imaging (MRI reconstruction)**, and **audio upsampling** in professional studio software, where near-perfect reconstruction justifies the high computational cost.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Frequency Response of Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)

We will derive $H_{ZOH}(j\omega)$ from first principles in full detail.

**Given:** $h_{ZOH}(t) = u(t) - u(t - T)$, where $u(t)$ is the unit step function.

**Step 1 — Set up the CTFT integral:**

$$H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h_{ZOH}(t) \, e^{-j\omega t} \, dt = \int_{0}^{T} (1) \cdot e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = \left[ \frac{e^{-j\omega t}}{-j\omega} \right]_{0}^{T} = \frac{e^{-j\omega T} - 1}{-j\omega} = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega T}}{j\omega}$$

**Step 3 — Convert numerator into exponential and sine form:**

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $e^{j\omega T/2}$ to get a centered exponential form:

$$H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = \frac{e^{-j\omega T/2}(e^{j\omega T/2} - e^{-j\omega T/2})}{j\omega} = \frac{e^{-j\omega T/2} \cdot 2j \sin(\omega T/2)}{j\omega}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify using Euler's identity $2j\sin(\theta) = e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta}$:**

$$H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = \frac{2 \sin(\omega T/2) \cdot e^{-j\omega T/2}}{\omega}$$

**Step 5 — Final canonical form (scaling to introduce $T$):**

Multiplying and dividing by $T/2$:

$$H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = T \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega T/2)}{\omega T/2} \cdot e^{-j\omega T/2} = T \cdot \text{sinc}\left(\frac{\omega T}{2}\right) \cdot e^{-j\omega T/2}$$

This is the standard ZOH frequency response. The magnitude is $\vert H_{ZOH}(j\omega) \vert = T \cdot \left\vert \frac{\sin(\omega T/2)}{\omega T/2} \right\vert$, and the phase is $\angle H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = -\omega T/2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation of the linear phase:** A phase term of $-\omega T/2$ corresponds to a constant time delay of $T/2$. This is the well-known half-sample delay introduced by the ZOH.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Frequency Response of First-Order Hold (FOH)

**Key Idea:** FOH's triangular impulse response is the convolution of two ZOH impulse responses: $h_{FOH}(t) = h_{ZOH}(t) * h_{ZOH}(t)$.

**Step 1 — Recognize the convolution in time domain:**

$$h_{FOH}(t) = h_{ZOH}(t) * h_{ZOH}(t)$$

For a rectangle of width $T$, convolving with itself yields a triangle that rises linearly from $0$ to $T$ and falls from $T$ to $2T$, with peak value $T$ at $t = T$. Normalizing the peak to 1 (matching the standard FOH definition) requires dividing by $T$:

$$h_{FOH}(t) = \frac{1}{T} \cdot [h_{ZOH}(t) * h_{ZOH}(t)]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the convolution theorem in the frequency domain:**

Convolution in time ↔ multiplication in frequency:

$$H_{FOH}(j\omega) = \frac{1}{T} \cdot H_{ZOH}(j\omega) \cdot H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = \frac{1}{T} \cdot \left[ H_{ZOH}(j\omega) \right]^2$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the ZOH result:**

$$H_{FOH}(j\omega) = \frac{1}{T} \cdot \left[ T \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega T/2)}{\omega T/2} \cdot e^{-j\omega T/2} \right]^2$$

**Step 4 — Expand the square:**

$$H_{FOH}(j\omega) = \frac{1}{T} \cdot T^2 \cdot \left[ \frac{\sin(\omega T/2)}{\omega T/2} \right]^2 \cdot e^{-j\omega T}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$H_{FOH}(j\omega) = T \cdot \left[ \frac{\sin(\omega T/2)}{\omega T/2} \right]^2 \cdot e^{-j\omega T} = T \cdot \text{sinc}^2\left(\frac{\omega T}{2}\right) \cdot e^{-j\omega T}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of delay:** The phase term is now $-\omega T$, which corresponds to a time delay of $T$ — exactly twice the ZOH delay, as expected from the convolution of two delayed systems.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Whittaker–Shannon Sinc Interpolation Formula

We will now derive the famous bandlimited reconstruction formula.

**Given:** $x(t)$ is strictly bandlimited to $\vert \omega \vert \leq \omega_m$, sampled at $T \leq \pi / \omega_m$.

**Step 1 — Express the sampled signal as an impulse train:**

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT) \cdot \delta(t - nT)$$

**Step 2 — Take the CTFT of the sampled signal (sampling theorem in frequency):**

$$X_s(j\omega) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(j(\omega - k\omega_s))$$

where $\omega_s = 2\pi/T$ is the sampling frequency in rad/s. The spectrum is periodic with period $\omega_s$, and non-overlapping replicas exist because we satisfy the Nyquist criterion: $\omega_s > 2\omega_m$.

**Step 3 — Design the ideal reconstruction filter to extract the baseband ($k=0$):**

An ideal low-pass filter with gain $T$ and cutoff $\omega_c = \pi/T = \omega_s/2$ does the job:

$$H_{SINC}(j\omega) = \begin{cases} T, & \vert \omega \vert \leq \pi/T \\ 0, & \vert \omega \vert > \pi/T \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the impulse response via inverse CTFT:**

$$h_{SINC}(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi/T}^{\pi/T} T \cdot e^{j\omega t} \, d\omega = \frac{T}{2\pi} \cdot \left[ \frac{e^{j\omega t}}{jt} \right]_{-\pi/T}^{\pi/T}$$

$$h_{SINC}(t) = \frac{T}{2\pi jt} \left( e^{j\pi t/T} - e^{-j\pi t/T} \right) = \frac{T}{2\pi jt} \cdot 2j \sin(\pi t/T) = \frac{\sin(\pi t/T)}{\pi t/T}$$

$$h_{SINC}(t) = \text{sinc}\left(\frac{t}{T}\right)$$

where the normalized sinc is defined as $\text{sinc}(x) = \frac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$.

**Step 5 — Reconstruct by convolving the impulse train with the sinc filter:**

$$x_r(t) = x_s(t) * h_{SINC}(t) = \left[ \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT) \delta(t - nT) \right] * \text{sinc}\left(\frac{t}{T}\right)$$

Using the sifting property of the impulse $\delta(t - nT) * h(t) = h(t - nT)$:

$$\boxed{x_r(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT) \cdot \text{sinc}\left(\frac{t - nT}{T}\right)}$$

This is the **Whittaker–Shannon Interpolation Formula**. For a bandlimited signal sampled at or above the Nyquist rate, $x_r(t) = x(t)$ exactly.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write the sinc argument as $(t - nT)/T$ inside the $\sin$ function incorrectly as $\sin(\pi(t - nT)/T)$. Always remember the normalized definition: $\text{sinc}(u) = \sin(\pi u) / (\pi u)$. With $u = (t - nT)/T$, we get $\sin(\pi(t - nT)/T) / (\pi(t - nT)/T)$.

### 3.4 Numerical Example 1 — ZOH at a Specific Frequency

**Problem:** A signal is sampled at $T = 0.1$ ms. Compute the magnitude and phase of the ZOH frequency response at $\omega = 1000\pi$ rad/s.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\omega T / 2$:**

$$\frac{\omega T}{2} = \frac{1000\pi \times 0.1 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{0.1\pi}{2} = 0.05\pi \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 — Compute magnitude $\vert H_{ZOH}(j\omega) \vert$:**

$$\vert H_{ZOH}(j1000\pi) \vert = T \cdot \left\vert \frac{\sin(0.05\pi)}{0.05\pi} \right\vert = 10^{-4} \cdot \left\vert \frac{\sin(\pi/20)}{\pi/20} \right\vert$$

Numerically, $\sin(\pi/20) = \sin(9°) \approx 0.1564$, and $\pi/20 \approx 0.1571$. So:

$$\vert H_{ZOH}(j1000\pi) \vert \approx 10^{-4} \cdot \frac{0.1564}{0.1571} \approx 0.9954 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3 — Compute phase:**

$$\angle H_{ZOH}(j1000\pi) = -\frac{\omega T}{2} = -0.05\pi \text{ rad} \approx -0.1571 \text{ rad} \approx -9°$$

### 3.5 Numerical Example 2 — Comparison of Magnitudes

For $T = 0.1$ ms and $\omega = 5000\pi$ rad/s (near Nyquist frequency $\pi/T = 10000\pi$):

- **ZOH magnitude:** $\vert H_{ZOH} \vert = 10^{-4} \cdot \frac{\sin(0.25\pi)}{0.25\pi} = 10^{-4} \cdot \frac{0.7071}{0.7854} \approx 0.9003 \times 10^{-4}$
- **FOH magnitude:** $\vert H_{FOH} \vert = 10^{-4} \cdot \left(\frac{0.7071}{0.7854}\right)^2 \approx 0.8106 \times 10^{-4}$
- **Sinc magnitude:** $\vert H_{SINC} \vert = 10^{-4}$ (constant up to Nyquist)

This shows the FOH rolls off **more aggressively** than ZOH at high frequencies (≈ 10% more attenuation here), while ideal Sinc is flat.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of the Reconstruction Process

The following block diagram captures the general framework of converting a discrete signal back to a continuous one. All three interpolation methods share this architecture, differing only in the choice of $h(t)$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Discrete Samples x of nT] --> B[Impulse Train Generator]
    B --> C[Sampled Signal xs of t]
    C --> D[Reconstruction Filter h of t]
    D --> E[Continuous Output xr of t]

    subgraph METHOD[Interpolation Method Choice]
        F1[ZOH Rectangle hT of t] -.-> D
        F2[FOH Triangle hFOH of t] -.-> D
        F3[Ideal Sinc hSINC of t] -.-> D
    end
```

### 4.2 Frequency Domain Action of the Reconstruction Filter

The sampled signal's spectrum contains periodic replicas. The reconstruction filter must extract only the baseband ($k=0$).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Original Spectrum X of j omega Bandlimited] --> B[Sampling Process Multiply by Impulse Train]
    B --> C[Periodic Spectrum Xs of j omega Replicas at k omega sub s]
    C --> D[Reconstruction Filter H of j omega]
    D --> E[Reconstructed Spectrum Xr of j omega]

    H1[ZOH Filter Sinx over x Roll off with Distortion] -.-> D
    H2[FOH Filter Sinx over x Squared Steeper Roll off] -.-> D
    H3[Ideal Sinc Filter Brick Wall Gain T Up to pi over T] -.-> D
```

### 4.3 Time-Domain Comparison of Reconstruction Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Samples]
        S0[Sample 0]
        S1[Sample 1]
        S2[Sample 2]
        S3[Sample 3]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS[Reconstruction Outputs]
        ZOH[ZOH Staircase Flat Segments]
        FOH[FOH Linear Ramps]
        SINC[Ideal Sinc Smooth Curve]
    end

    S0 --> ZOH
    S1 --> ZOH
    S2 --> ZOH
    S3 --> ZOH

    S0 --> FOH
    S1 --> FOH
    S2 --> FOH
    S3 --> FOH

    S0 --> SINC
    S1 --> SINC
    S2 --> SINC
    S3 --> SINC
```

### 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Interpolation Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need to Reconstruct Continuous Signal from Samples] --> B{Is Signal Strictly Bandlimited?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is Perfect Reconstruction Required?}
    B -- No --> D{Is Real Time Hardware Output Needed?}
    C -- Yes --> E[Use Sinc Interpolation Offline or Truncated Approximation]
    C -- No --> F{Is Phase Delay Acceptable?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use First Order Hold for Smoother Output]
    F -- No --> H[Use Zero Order Hold DAC Compatible]
    D -- Yes --> I[Use Zero Order Hold Standard DAC]
    D -- No --> J[Use First Order Hold for Visual Smoothing]
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — ZOH in a Practical DAC System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Digital Input x of n] --> B[Register Latch at Clock nT]
    B --> C[Output Holds Value Until Next Clock]
    C --> D[Analog Reconstruction Low Pass Filter]
    D --> E[Smooth Continuous Output x of t]

    F[Clock Signal Period T] --> B
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) interpolation. Mention its main disadvantage in frequency response.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) is a mathematical model of the practical signal reconstruction done by a Digital-to-Analog Converter. Its impulse response is a rectangular pulse of width equal to the sampling period $T$ and unit height: $h_{ZOH}(t) = u(t) - u(t - T)$, where $u(t)$ is the unit step. The reconstructed output is a staircase approximation of the original signal. **Main disadvantage:** Its frequency response magnitude, $H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = T \cdot [\sin(\omega T/2) / (\omega T/2)] \cdot e^{-j\omega T/2}$, has a $\sin(x)/x$ (sinc) roll-off, causing amplitude distortion (the aperture effect) at higher frequencies near the Nyquist limit.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** State the Whittaker–Shannon interpolation theorem. Why is the ideal sinc interpolator not physically realizable?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Theorem:** If a signal $x(t)$ is strictly bandlimited to $\omega_m$ rad/s (i.e., $X(j\omega) = 0$ for $\vert \omega \vert > \omega_m$) and sampled at a rate $\omega_s \geq 2\omega_m$, then $x(t)$ can be exactly reconstructed from its samples as $x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT) \cdot \text{sinc}\left(\frac{t - nT}{T}\right)$, where $T = 2\pi/\omega_s$.
**Non-realizability:** The sinc interpolator's impulse response $h(t) = \text{sinc}(t/T) = \sin(\pi t/T) / (\pi t/T)$ extends from $t = -\infty$ to $+\infty$, making it **non-causal**. A causal system cannot produce an output before an input occurs. Furthermore, since the sinc function does not decay to zero in finite time, an infinite amount of past and future samples would be required for the reconstruction, which is impossible in real-time systems.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the frequency response of the Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) reconstruction filter. Sketch the magnitude response and discuss the $\sin(x)/x$ roll-off (aperture effect). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The First-Order Hold (FOH) interpolator has a triangular impulse response of base $2T$ and unit peak. Obtain its frequency response. Compare the magnitude response of FOH with that of ZOH. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of ZOH frequency response:**

The ZOH impulse response is:
$$h_{ZOH}(t) = u(t) - u(t - T)$$

Taking the CTFT:
$$H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = \int_{0}^{T} e^{-j\omega t} \, dt = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega T}}{j\omega}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $e^{j\omega T/2}$:
$$H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = \frac{e^{-j\omega T/2} (e^{j\omega T/2} - e^{-j\omega T/2})}{j\omega} = \frac{2j e^{-j\omega T/2} \sin(\omega T/2)}{j\omega}$$

$$H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = T \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega T/2)}{\omega T/2} \cdot e^{-j\omega T/2}$$

**Magnitude:** $\vert H_{ZOH}(j\omega) \vert = T \cdot \vert \sin(\omega T/2) / (\omega T/2) \vert$

**Phase:** $\angle H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = -\omega T/2$

**Magnitude sketch:** A sinc function with peak $T$ at $\omega = 0$, zeros at $\omega = \pm 2\pi/T, \pm 4\pi/T, \ldots$, and slowly decaying side lobes.

**Aperture effect (3 marks):** The $\sin(x)/x$ roll-off attenuates the high-frequency components of the signal near the Nyquist frequency. At $\omega = \pi/T$ (the Nyquist limit), the magnitude is $T \cdot \sin(\pi/2)/(\pi/2) = 2T/\pi \approx 0.637T$, meaning a **36.3% loss in amplitude** at the band edge. This amplitude distortion is called the **aperture effect** or **$\sin(x)/x$ droop**, and is corrected in practice by using an **equalizing filter** with response $(\omega T/2)/\sin(\omega T/2)$.

**(b) FOH frequency response derivation:**

The FOH impulse response is a triangle:
$$h_{FOH}(t) = \begin{cases} 1 - \vert t \vert / T, & \vert t \vert \leq T \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This is the convolution of two ZOH impulse responses scaled by $1/T$:
$$h_{FOH}(t) = \frac{1}{T} \cdot [h_{ZOH}(t) * h_{ZOH}(t)]$$

Using the convolution theorem:
$$H_{FOH}(j\omega) = \frac{1}{T} \cdot \left[ H_{ZOH}(j\omega) \right]^2$$

Substituting the ZOH result:
$$H_{FOH}(j\omega) = \frac{1}{T} \cdot T^2 \cdot \left[ \frac{\sin(\omega T/2)}{\omega T/2} \right]^2 \cdot e^{-j\omega T}$$

$$H_{FOH}(j\omega) = T \cdot \left[ \frac{\sin(\omega T/2)}{\omega T/2} \right]^2 \cdot e^{-j\omega T}$$

**Comparison with ZOH:**

| Property | ZOH | FOH |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Magnitude form | $T \cdot \text{sinc}(\omega T/2)$ | $T \cdot \text{sinc}^2(\omega T/2)$ |
| Time delay | $T/2$ | $T$ |
| Roll-off | Slower (linear sinc) | Steeper (squared sinc) |
| First zero | $\omega = 2\pi/T$ | Same $\omega = 2\pi/T$ |
| Smoothness | Staircase | Linear ramps |

The squared sinc gives **stronger attenuation** of high frequencies (more image rejection) at the cost of **twice the time delay**.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** State and prove the sampling theorem for strictly bandlimited signals. Show that the reconstructed signal is given by $x(t) = \sum x(nT) \, \text{sinc}[(t - nT)/T]$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A signal $x(t)$ bandlimited to 5 kHz is sampled at $T = 0.1$ ms. Compute the value of $x(t)$ at $t = 0.075$ ms using the sinc interpolation formula, given the samples $x(0) = 1$, $x(0.1\text{ms}) = 0.8$, $x(0.2\text{ms}) = 0.5$, $x(-0.1\text{ms}) = 1.2$, $x(-0.2\text{ms}) = 1.5$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sampling theorem statement and proof (7 marks):**

**Statement:** A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ Hz (or $\omega_m = 2\pi f_m$ rad/s) can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples $x(nT)$ if the sampling frequency $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ (Nyquist rate). The reconstruction is given by:
$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT) \cdot \text{sinc}\left(\frac{t - nT}{T}\right)$$

**Proof:** The sampled signal in impulse train form is $x_s(t) = \sum_n x(nT) \delta(t - nT)$. Its CTFT is:
$$X_s(j\omega) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(j(\omega - k\omega_s))$$

Since $x(t)$ is bandlimited to $\omega_m$ and $\omega_s \geq 2\omega_m$, the replicas do not overlap (aliasing-free). An ideal low-pass filter of gain $T$ and cutoff $\omega_c = \pi/T$ recovers $X(j\omega)$ exactly:
$$X(j\omega) = H_{SINC}(j\omega) \cdot X_s(j\omega)$$

The inverse CTFT of the ideal filter gives the impulse response:
$$h_{SINC}(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi/T}^{\pi/T} T e^{j\omega t} d\omega = \frac{\sin(\pi t / T)}{\pi t / T} = \text{sinc}\left(\frac{t}{T}\right)$$

Convolution of $x_s(t)$ with $h_{SINC}(t)$ yields:
$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT) \cdot \text{sinc}\left(\frac{t - nT}{T}\right) \quad \blacksquare$$

**(b) Numerical evaluation (7 marks):**

Given $T = 0.1$ ms, $t = 0.075$ ms.

The sinc interpolation formula is $x(t) = \sum_n x(nT) \cdot \text{sinc}[(t - nT)/T]$.

Compute $(t - nT)/T$ for each sample:

| $n$ | $nT$ (ms) | $t - nT$ (ms) | $(t-nT)/T$ | $\text{sinc}$ value | $x(nT)$ | Contribution |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $-2$ | $-0.2$ | $0.275$ | $2.75$ | $\sin(2.75\pi)/(2.75\pi) \approx 0.0707$ | $1.5$ | $0.106$ |
| $-1$ | $-0.1$ | $0.175$ | $1.75$ | $\sin(1.75\pi)/(1.75\pi) \approx -0.1289$ | $1.2$ | $-0.155$ |
| $0$ | $0$ | $0.075$ | $0.75$ | $\sin(0.75\pi)/(0.75\pi) \approx 0.3001$ | $1.0$ | $0.300$ |
| $1$ | $0.1$ | $-0.025$ | $-0.25$ | $\sin(-0.25\pi)/(-0.25\pi) \approx 0.9003$ | $0.8$ | $0.720$ |
| $2$ | $0.2$ | $-0.125$ | $-1.25$ | $\sin(-1.25\pi)/(-1.25\pi) \approx 0.2547$ | $0.5$ | $0.127$ |

Summing all contributions: $x(0.075 \text{ ms}) \approx 0.106 - 0.155 + 0.300 + 0.720 + 0.127 = 1.098$

**Final answer:** $x(0.075 \text{ ms}) \approx 1.098$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign errors in sinc:** $\text{sinc}(-u) = \text{sinc}(u)$ (it is an even function). Forgetting this sign can flip your answer. Example: $\text{sinc}(1.75) = \sin(1.75\pi)/(1.75\pi) = \sin(315°)/5.4978 = -0.7071/5.4978 \approx -0.1289$. Students often write $+0.1289$ — wrong sign.
> 2. **Argument confusion:** The argument is $\pi (t - nT)/T$ inside the sine, not $(t - nT)/T$. A common mistake is $\sin(0.75\pi)/0.75$ instead of $\sin(0.75\pi)/(0.75\pi)$.
> 3. **Indexing mistakes:** Make sure to use negative $n$ for past samples (e.g., $n = -1$ for $t = -0.1$ ms, NOT $n = 1$).
> 4. **Forgetting the Nyquist check:** KTU may test if the signal is actually bandlimited enough for sinc interpolation. Always check $\omega_s = 2\pi/T \geq 2\omega_m$ before applying the formula.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4 Interpolation Methods**

- **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH):** Staircase interpolation; impulse response = rectangle of width $T$, height 1; frequency response = $T \cdot \text{sinc}(\omega T/2) \cdot e^{-j\omega T/2}$; introduces a delay of $T/2$; suffer from $\sin(x)/x$ roll-off (aperture effect); used in standard DACs.

- **First-Order Hold (FOH):** Linear interpolation; impulse response = triangle of base $2T$, unit peak; frequency response = $T \cdot \text{sinc}^2(\omega T/2) \cdot e^{-j\omega T}$; delay of $T$ (double ZOH); steeper magnitude roll-off.

- **Sinc (Ideal) Interpolation:** Perfect reconstruction for bandlimited signals; $h(t) = \text{sinc}(t/T)$ is non-causal and non-decaying, hence not realizable in real-time; the **only** interpolation method that gives exact reconstruction $x_r(t) = x(t)$ for $\omega_s \geq 2\omega_m$ (Whittaker–Shannon theorem).

- **Common Reconstruction Framework:** $x_r(t) = \sum_n x(nT) \cdot h(t - nT)$ — all three methods are special cases of this convolution sum.

- **Frequency-Domain Picture:** The sampled signal has a periodic spectrum with replicas every $\omega_s$. The reconstruction filter must low-pass extract the baseband ($k = 0$ replica). ZOH and FOH approximate this with their sinc/sinc² roll-off; Sinc is the ideal brick-wall.

- **Delay Comparison:** ZOH = $T/2$, FOH = $T$, Sinc = 0 (zero phase, but non-causal).

- **Realizability Order:** ZOH (easiest, hardware) → FOH (moderate, software) → Sinc (theoretical only).

- **Common KTU Pitfalls:**
  - Confusing normalized sinc $\sin(\pi u)/(\pi u)$ with unnormalized $\sin(u)/u$.
  - Forgetting the Nyquist criterion when applying sinc interpolation.
  - Miscounting the time delay (it is $T/2$ for ZOH, NOT $T$).
  - Mixing up sinc and sinc² when comparing FOH with ZOH.
  - Writing $\text{sinc}((t-nT)/T)$ inside the sine function incorrectly.

- **Quick Mnemonic:** "**Z**ero-order is a **Z**-staircase, **F**irst-order is a **F**lat ramp, **S**inc is **S**mooth but **S**low (non-causal)."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oversampling and Undersampling: Benefits, Conditions, and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT403 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Oversampling and Undersampling: Benefits, Conditions, and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **Sampling Theorem** and discrete-time signal processing, the relationship between the sampling frequency $f_s$ and the highest frequency component $f_m$ of a bandlimited continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is categorized into three regimes:

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Definition: Sampling Regimes**
> - **Nyquist Rate (Critical Sampling):** $f_s = 2f_m$ — the absolute theoretical minimum rate to avoid aliasing.
> - **Oversampling:** $f_s > 2f_m$ — sampling at a rate strictly greater than the Nyquist rate.
> - **Undersampling (Bandpass Sampling / Sub-Nyquist Sampling):** $f_s < 2f_m$ — sampling below the Nyquist rate, applicable **only** to bandpass signals under specific guard-band conditions.

The **Oversampling Ratio (OSR)** is formally defined as:
$$\text{OSR} = \frac{f_s}{2f_m} = \frac{f_s}{f_{Nyquist}}$$

When OSR $\gg$ 1, the system is said to be in the **oversampling regime**. Conversely, in bandpass signal processing, the term **undersampling** is reinterpreted: the analog signal is centered at a high carrier frequency $f_c$, but is sampled at a rate dictated by its **bandwidth** $B$, not by $f_c$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Movie Camera 🎥

Imagine you are filming a spinning ceiling fan:
- If you record at **24 frames per second (undersampled)**, the fan blades appear to rotate *backward* or *freeze* — this is **aliasing**.
- If you record at exactly the right speed (Nyquist), you capture the motion correctly, but the video is barely usable for slow-motion analysis.
- If you record at **240 frames per second (oversampled)**, the video is buttery smooth, you can slow it down 10x, and the digital storage is much more forgiving to small recording errors.

In signals and systems, the "frames per second" is your $f_s$, and the "blade speed" is $f_m$. Oversampling gives you **engineering margin** — a safety cushion that makes the entire digital pipeline more robust.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE] **Key Engineering Metrics**
> - **Nyquist Frequency:** $f_{Nyquist} = f_s / 2$ (the highest recoverable frequency)
> - **Oversampling Ratio (OSR):** A dimensionless quantity, typically $2$ to $512$ in audio ADCs.
> - **SNR Gain Rule:** Every **doubling (octave)** of $f_s$ beyond $2f_m$ yields approximately **+3 dB** SNR improvement for uniform quantization, or **+6 dB** when combined with 1-bit oversampling (delta-sigma modulators).
> - **Resolution Gain Rule:** Each 4× increase in OSR adds **1 effective bit** of ADC resolution (for noise-shaping systems).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

### A. Oversampling Mechanism

1. **The analog signal** $x(t)$ is bandlimited to $f_m$ (e.g., audio at 20 kHz).
2. **The ADC samples** at $f_s = K \cdot 2f_m$, where $K > 1$ is the oversampling factor.
3. **Quantization noise** (inherent to any finite-bit ADC) has total power:
$$\sigma_q^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{(2^{-n} \cdot V_{FS})^2}{12}$$
where $n$ is the ADC bit-width and $V_{FS}$ is the full-scale voltage.
4. This noise is **uniformly distributed** across the entire Nyquist band $[-f_s/2,\ f_s/2]$.
5. **Crucial Insight:** Since the *useful* signal still occupies only $[-f_m,\ f_m]$, a digital lowpass filter can excise the out-of-band noise.
6. The in-band noise power after filtering becomes:
$$\sigma_{q,\text{filtered}}^2 = \sigma_q^2 \cdot \frac{2f_m}{f_s} = \frac{\sigma_q^2}{\text{OSR}}$$
7. Therefore, the **in-band SNR** improves as $\sqrt{\text{OSR}}$ (in amplitude) or as **OSR** (in power/dB).

### B. Undersampling (Bandpass Sampling) Mechanism

1. **The signal is bandpass** — its spectrum $X(f)$ is nonzero only in $[f_L, f_H]$, with $f_L \gg 0$.
2. The **signal bandwidth** is $B = f_H - f_L$ (typically $B \ll f_c$, the center frequency).
3. Sampling at $f_s < 2f_c$ causes spectral replicas to overlap, but if $f_s$ is chosen carefully, the overlapping replicas **do not corrupt the signal's baseband representation**.
4. The condition to avoid spectral overlap is given by the **bandpass sampling theorem**:
$$f_s \geq 2B$$
with additional guard-band inequalities to prevent adjacent replica collisions.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Nyquist Rate | $f_N = 2f_m$ | Hz |
| 2 | Oversampling Ratio | $\text{OSR} = f_s / f_N$ | Dimensionless, $\text{OSR} \geq 1$ |
| 3 | Quantization Step | $\Delta = V_{FS} / 2^n$ | Volts |
| 4 | Quantization Noise Power | $\sigma_q^2 = \Delta^2 / 12$ | W or V² |
| 5 | SNR of n-bit uniform ADC | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76$ | dB |
| 6 | SNR Gain per Octave (uniform) | $+3$ dB / octave of OSR | dB |
| 7 | SNR Gain per Octave (1-bit noise-shape) | $+6$ dB / octave of OSR | dB |
| 8 | Effective Bits Gain | $+0.5$ bit / octave (uniform) | bits |
| 9 | Effective Bits Gain (noise-shape) | $+1.0$ bit / octave (1-bit $\Delta\Sigma$) | bits |
| 10 | Anti-alias Transition BW | $\Delta f_{trans} = f_s/2 - f_m$ | Hz (larger $\Rightarrow$ easier filter) |
| 11 | Bandpass Sampling Lower Bound | $f_s \geq 2B$ | Hz |
| 12 | Bandpass Sampling Center | $f_c = (f_H + f_L)/2$ | Hz |
| 13 | Spectral Replica Position | $f_{\text{alias}} = \vert f - k f_s \vert$ | Hz, $k \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| 14 | Filter Order Reduction (approx.) | $N_{\text{new}} \approx N_{\text{old}} / \log_2(\text{OSR})$ | Order (Butterworth) |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Critical Reminder for Table Use**
> In KTU exam answers, **never confuse $f_s$ with $f_m$**. Always label frequencies clearly. Use $f_s$ for sampling rate, $f_m$ for signal max frequency, $f_N$ for Nyquist rate, and $B$ for bandwidth.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oversampling** is the cornerstone of modern **audio DACs** (e.g., 192 kHz / 24-bit systems in studio equipment) and **delta-sigma ($\Delta\Sigma$) ADCs** used in every smartphone microphone.
- **Undersampling** is fundamental to **Software Defined Radio (SDR)**, where a 2.4 GHz RF signal is directly digitized at, say, 100 MSPS, and the demodulation is performed entirely in the digital domain.
- It is also critical in **digital storage oscilloscopes** (DSOs) and **radar baseband processing**, where direct IF sampling replaces expensive analog down-conversion stages.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — SNR Improvement from Oversampling

### Given
A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ is sampled at $f_s = \text{OSR} \cdot 2f_m$ using an $n$-bit uniform quantizer with full-scale range $V_{FS}$.

### Step 1 — Quantization Step Size
The quantizer divides the full-scale range into $2^n$ discrete levels, so the step size is:
$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$

### Step 2 — Total Quantization Noise Power
Assuming uniformly distributed quantization error $e_q \sim \mathcal{U}(-\Delta/2, \Delta/2)$:
$$\sigma_q^2 = E[e_q^2] = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta} \, de = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

### Step 3 — Spectral Density of Quantization Noise
The noise is white over the Nyquist band $[-f_s/2,\ f_s/2]$, so the **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** is:
$$S_q(f) = \frac{\sigma_q^2}{f_s} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12 f_s} \quad \text{for } \vert f \vert \leq f_s/2$$

### Step 4 — In-Band Noise After Digital Filtering
The digital lowpass filter retains only $[-f_m,\ f_m]$. Integrating the PSD over this band:
$$\sigma_{q,\text{in}}^2 = \int_{-f_m}^{f_m} S_q(f) \, df = \frac{\Delta^2}{12 f_s} \cdot 2 f_m = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} \cdot \frac{2 f_m}{f_s} = \frac{\sigma_q^2}{\text{OSR}}$$

### Step 5 — SNR in Decibels
The signal power is $P_s = V_{FS}^2 / 8$ for a full-scale sinusoid. The SNR becomes:
$$\text{SNR}_{\text{oversample}} = \frac{P_s}{\sigma_{q,\text{in}}^2} = \frac{V_{FS}^2 / 8}{\Delta^2 / (12 \cdot \text{OSR})} = \frac{3 \cdot 2^{2n} \cdot \text{OSR}}{2}$$

Converting to dB:
$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76 + 10 \log_{10}(\text{OSR})$$

### Step 6 — Gain Per Octave
Doubling $f_s$ doubles OSR, so the SNR increases by $10 \log_{10}(2) \approx 3.01$ dB. This confirms the **+3 dB per octave** rule.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bandpass Sampling Condition

### Given
A real bandpass signal $x(t)$ with spectrum nonzero in $[f_L,\ f_H]$ and bandwidth $B = f_H - f_L$.

### Step 1 — Spectral Replicas
Sampling at $f_s$ creates replicas of $X(f)$ shifted by integer multiples of $f_s$. A frequency component at $f$ reappears (aliased) at:
$$f_a = \vert f - k f_s \vert \quad \text{for some integer } k$$

### Step 2 — Non-Aliasing Condition
To reconstruct the original bandpass signal from its samples, the replicas must not overlap with the original band. The minimum sampling rate is:
$$f_s \geq 2B$$

### Step 3 — Center Frequency Guard Condition
To prevent adjacent replicas from overlapping, the following must hold:
$$f_L \geq \frac{k f_s}{2} \quad \text{and} \quad f_H \leq \frac{(k+1) f_s}{2}$$

This leads to the **bandpass sampling inequality**:
$$\frac{2 f_H}{k+1} \leq f_s \leq \frac{2 f_L}{k}$$

where $k = \lfloor f_H / B \rfloor$ is the largest integer such that the band fits without aliasing.

### Step 4 — Worked Example Setup
A signal has $f_L = 90$ kHz, $f_H = 110$ kHz, so $B = 20$ kHz, $f_c = 100$ kHz. The Nyquist rate for a lowpass interpretation would be 220 kHz, but bandpass sampling allows:
$$f_s \geq 2B = 40 \text{ kHz}$$
with allowed $f_s$ values in specific guard bands derived from the inequality above.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Anti-Aliasing Filter Relaxation

### Given
A Butterworth anti-aliasing filter of order $N$ must attenuate out-of-band components by $A_s$ dB starting at $f_m$, with the stopband edge at $f_s/2$.

### Step 1 — Standard Design Equation
For a Butterworth filter:
$$N = \frac{\log_{10}\left(\sqrt{10^{A_s/10}-1}\right)}{\log_{10}(f_s / 2f_m)}$$

### Step 2 — Effect of Oversampling
Increasing $f_s$ (and thus $f_s/2f_m = \text{OSR}$) makes the denominator larger, **reducing the required filter order $N$**.

### Step 3 — Quantitative Example
If $A_s = 60$ dB and $f_s/2f_m = 1.1$ (near-Nyquist), $N \approx 33$ (impractical).
If $f_s/2f_m = 4$ (4× oversample), $N \approx 7$ (very practical).
**Conclusion:** Oversampling transforms a physically unrealizable analog filter into a simple, low-order one — or even eliminates the need for a sharp analog filter entirely if a gentle RC pre-filter + aggressive digital filter is acceptable.

---

## 3.4 Comprehensive Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Oversampling | Undersampling (Bandpass) |
|-----------|--------------|-------------------------|
| Sampling Rate $f_s$ | $f_s \gg 2f_m$ | $2B \leq f_s < 2f_c$ |
| Signal Type | Lowpass or bandpass | **Bandpass only** |
| Primary Benefit | Improved SNR, relaxed filters | Reduced ADC speed, direct RF digitization |
| Primary Risk | Higher data rate, more storage | Aliasing if guard conditions violated |
| ADC Cost | Lower speed, higher bits | Higher analog bandwidth required |
| Typical Applications | Audio codecs, $\Delta\Sigma$ ADCs, instrumentation | SDR, radar IF sampling, DSO |
| Key Formula | $\text{SNR} \propto 10\log(\text{OSR})$ | $f_s \geq 2B$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define oversampling. What is the oversampling ratio? List two advantages of oversampling in ADC systems.**

**Model Answer:**
Oversampling is the process of sampling an analog signal at a rate $f_s$ that is significantly greater than the Nyquist rate $2f_m$. The **Oversampling Ratio (OSR)** is defined as $\text{OSR} = f_s / (2f_m)$.

Two advantages:
1. **Improved Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** Each octave of oversampling adds ~3 dB SNR (uniform quantization) or ~6 dB SNR (with 1-bit noise shaping).
2. **Relaxed anti-aliasing filter design:** The transition band $f_s/2 - f_m$ becomes wider, allowing the use of simple, low-order analog filters.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is meant by undersampling? Under what conditions can a bandpass signal be undersampled without aliasing?**

**Model Answer:**
Undersampling (bandpass sampling) refers to sampling a bandpass signal at a rate $f_s$ that is **less than twice the highest frequency** $f_m$ of the signal, but **greater than twice the signal's bandwidth** $B = f_H - f_L$.

Conditions for alias-free undersampling:
1. The signal must be **bandpass** (not lowpass), with its spectrum confined to $[f_L, f_H]$.
2. The sampling rate must satisfy: $f_s \geq 2B$.
3. Additionally, the chosen $f_s$ must lie in the **guard band** interval:
$$\frac{2 f_H}{k+1} \leq f_s \leq \frac{2 f_L}{k}$$
for some positive integer $k$, ensuring that no spectral replicas overlap the desired band.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part B questions in ESE carry 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. **Answer ANY ONE** of the two full questions from the module.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Oversampling Focus [KTU Model Paper Pattern]

**Q.A (a)** An audio signal is bandlimited to **20 kHz** and is sampled at $f_s = 176.4$ kHz using a **16-bit uniform ADC**.
**(i)** Calculate the OSR.
**(ii)** Determine the theoretical SNR (in dB).
**(iii)** How much SNR improvement (in dB) is achieved over a Nyquist-rate ($f_s = 44.1$ kHz) sampler?

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Oversampling Ratio:**
$$\text{OSR} = \frac{f_s}{2 f_m} = \frac{176.4 \text{ kHz}}{2 \times 20 \text{ kHz}} = \frac{176.4}{40} = 4.41$$

**(ii) Theoretical SNR:**
Using $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76 + 10\log_{10}(\text{OSR})$:
$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 + 10 \log_{10}(4.41)$$
$$= 96.32 + 1.76 + 10 \times 0.6444$$
$$= 98.08 + 6.444 = \mathbf{104.52 \text{ dB}}$$

**(iii) SNR Improvement over Nyquist Rate:**
At Nyquist rate ($\text{OSR} = 1$):
$$\text{SNR}_{Nyquist} = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08 \text{ dB}$$
$$\Delta \text{SNR} = 10\log_{10}(4.41) = \mathbf{6.44 \text{ dB}}$$

Equivalently, $\text{OSR} = 4.41 \approx 2^{2.14}$, so this is roughly 2.14 octaves of oversampling $\Rightarrow 2.14 \times 3 = 6.42$ dB ✓ (matches).

---

**Q.A (b)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain how oversampling is used in a practical ADC system. Discuss the role of the **decimation filter** and state two engineering applications.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A typical oversampling ADC system consists of the following blocks in sequence:

1. **Analog Anti-Aliasing Filter:** A simple, low-order (often 1st or 2nd order) RC filter. Oversampling allows the transition band to be wide, so this filter need not be sharp.
2. **Oversampling ADC:** Samples at $f_s \gg 2f_m$. The high oversampling rate spreads the quantization noise power over a wide bandwidth, reducing in-band noise.
3. **Digital Lowpass Filter:** A high-order sharp digital filter (e.g., FIR or CIC) that removes out-of-band quantization noise while preserving the $[0, f_m]$ signal band.
4. **Decimator:** Reduces the sample rate from $f_s$ down to the desired output rate (e.g., $2f_m$) by discarding intermediate samples. To prevent aliasing during decimation, the digital lowpass filter must precede it.

**Role of the Decimation Filter:** It is **both** a noise-shaping filter and an anti-aliasing filter for the down-sampling step. Without it, decimation would alias the out-of-band quantization noise back into the signal band.

**Two Engineering Applications:**
1. **Studio-quality audio DACs** (e.g., 192 kHz / 24-bit systems using $\Delta\Sigma$ architecture).
2. **Precision instrumentation amplifiers** and digital voltmeters (DVMs) where high effective resolution (>20 bits) is needed from low-cost 16-bit ADCs.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Undersampling / Bandpass Sampling Focus

**Q.B (a)** A radar IF signal occupies the band **$[f_L, f_H] = [80 \text{ MHz},\ 120 \text{ MHz}]$**.
**(i)** Determine the signal bandwidth $B$ and center frequency $f_c$.
**(ii)** What is the **minimum** sampling rate that can be used without aliasing?
**(iii)** Find the **range** of permissible sampling rates (for $k = 1$) that allow alias-free bandpass sampling.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Bandwidth and Center Frequency:**
$$B = f_H - f_L = 120 - 80 = \mathbf{40 \text{ MHz}}$$
$$f_c = \frac{f_H + f_L}{2} = \frac{120 + 80}{2} = \mathbf{100 \text{ MHz}}$$

**(ii) Minimum Sampling Rate (Bandpass Nyquist):**
$$f_{s,\min} = 2B = 2 \times 40 = \mathbf{80 \text{ MHz}}$$

**(iii) Permissible Range for $k = 1$:**
Using $\frac{2 f_H}{k+1} \leq f_s \leq \frac{2 f_L}{k}$ with $k = 1$:
$$\frac{2 \times 120}{2} \leq f_s \leq \frac{2 \times 80}{1}$$
$$120 \text{ MHz} \leq f_s \leq 160 \text{ MHz}$$

**Verification:** Sampling at, say, $f_s = 160$ MHz, the replicas of the 80–120 MHz band appear at $f = 160 - [80, 120] = [40, 80]$ MHz (baseband) — clean, no overlap. ✓

---

**Q.B (b)** Explain the concept of **bandpass undersampling** with the help of a block diagram of a Software Defined Radio (SDR) receiver. What are the advantages of undersampling in this application?

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept:** In a bandpass undersampling system, a signal centered at a high RF/IF frequency $f_c$ (often hundreds of MHz or several GHz) is sampled at a rate $f_s$ much lower than $2f_c$, but still satisfying $f_s \geq 2B$. The analog signal is directly converted into discrete samples without an analog down-conversion stage.

**SDR Receiver Block Diagram (see Section 4.2):**
The signal flow is:
**Antenna → RF Bandpass Filter → Low-Noise Amplifier → Preselect Filter → Undersampling ADC → Digital Down-Conversion (DDC) → Digital Channel Filter → Baseband Output.**

The **Digital Down-Converter (DDC)** typically consists of a digital mixer, a numerically controlled oscillator (NCO), and a pair of decimation filters (CIC + FIR) that extract the desired channel and reduce the sample rate to baseband.

**Advantages of Undersampling in SDR:**
1. **Eliminates analog mixing stages:** No need for analog local oscillators, mixers, or anti-alias filters at IF — saving cost, board space, and power.
2. **Software reconfigurability:** The "channel" to be received is selected in the digital domain, allowing a single hardware platform to demodulate multiple standards (GSM, LTE, Wi-Fi, etc.) via firmware.
3. **Improved linearity and repeatability:** Digital processing avoids analog component drift, temperature variation, and I/Q imbalance issues.
4. **Reduced ADC analog bandwidth requirements** are traded off for *higher* sampling precision at a *lower* clock rate — typically more cost-effective.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Warning

> [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation**
> 1. **Confusing $f_s$ with $2f_s$:** Many students write the Nyquist rate as $f_s$ instead of $2f_m$. Always write $f_N = 2f_m$.
> 2. **Forgetting the logarithm in SNR:** $\text{SNR} \propto 10 \log_{10}(\text{OSR})$, **not** OSR directly. Writing "SNR increases by OSR" loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Applying undersampling to lowpass signals:** Undersampling is valid **only for bandpass signals**. A student applying $f_s < 2f_m$ to a lowpass audio signal will be penalized for fundamental conceptual error.
> 4. **Ignoring the guard-band inequality in bandpass sampling:** Simply stating $f_s \geq 2B$ is **insufficient** for full marks. The range $\frac{2 f_H}{k+1} \leq f_s \leq \frac{2 f_L}{k}$ must also be discussed.
> 5. **Mixing up OSR and bit gain:** Each 4× OSR gives ~1 effective bit. Students often write "OSR doubles → 1 bit gain" — this is wrong. Use the formula $\Delta n_{eff} = 0.5 \log_2(\text{OSR})$ for uniform quantization.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Sampling Regimes:** $f_s > 2f_m$ (oversample), $f_s = 2f_m$ (Nyquist/critical), $f_s < 2f_m$ (undersample — bandpass only).
- **OSR:** $\text{OSR} = f_s / 2f_m$ — the central metric for oversampling analysis.
- **SNR Formula (with OSR):** $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76 + 10\log_{10}(\text{OSR})$.
- **3 dB / Octave Rule:** Doubling $f_s$ gives ~3 dB SNR gain (uniform); ~6 dB with 1-bit noise shaping.
- **Bit Gain Rule:** $+0.5$ bit/octave (uniform); $+1.0$ bit/octave ($\Delta\Sigma$).
- **Anti-Aliasing Relaxation:** Oversampling allows low-order analog filters by widening the transition band $f_s/2 - f_m$.
- **Bandpass Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2B$, with guard-band inequality $\frac{2 f_H}{k+1} \leq f_s \leq \frac{2 f_L}{k}$.
- **Applications of Oversampling:** $\Delta\Sigma$ ADCs, audio DACs, precision DVMs, instrumentation.
- **Applications of Undersampling:** Software Defined Radio (SDR), digital oscilloscopes, radar IF sampling, direct RF digitization.
- **Key Trade-off:** Oversampling trades **ADC speed and data storage** for **resolution and filter simplicity**; Undersampling trades **analog complexity** for **digital processing load**.
- **Golden Rule:** Never undersample a lowpass signal — it will always alias. Always verify the bandpass guard condition before applying undersampling.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always include a **block diagram** (3 marks), a **derivation or formula derivation** (4 marks), and a **numerical example** (3 marks), with the remaining marks for application discussion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampling in the Frequency Domain and Sampling of Band-Pass Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT403 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sampling in the Frequency Domain and Sampling of Band-Pass Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Sampling Theorem (Time Domain Recap)

The **Sampling Theorem** (also known as the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**) is the foundational pillar of digital signal processing. It establishes the minimum rate at which a continuous-time (analog) signal must be sampled so that it can be perfectly reconstructed from its discrete samples without any loss of information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A band-limited signal $x(t)$ having no frequency components above $f_M$ Hz (i.e., $X(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > f_M$) can be uniquely determined by its values at uniform intervals less than or equal to $\frac{1}{2f_M}$ seconds apart. The minimum sampling rate $f_s = 2f_M$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**, and $\frac{1}{2f_M}$ is called the **Nyquist Interval**.

## 1.2 Sampling in the Frequency Domain — The Intuitive Picture

When a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is multiplied by an **ideal impulse train** $p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$ in the **time domain**, the resulting sampled signal $x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t)$ has a frequency-domain representation obtained via the **Convolution Theorem for Fourier Transforms**:

$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - kf_s)$$

where $f_s = \frac{1}{T_s}$ is the sampling frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Stroboscopic Effect):**
> Imagine a spinning bicycle wheel with a colored spoke. If you record a video of it at a very high frame rate (say 240 fps), you see smooth rotation. If you record it at a very low frame rate (say 10 fps), the spoke appears to move *backwards* or *stationary*. This is **aliasing** — the wheel's true rotation frequency has been "folded" into a false, lower frequency. The Sampling Theorem is the rule that prevents this illusion by insisting you sample at least **twice** as fast as the highest frequency of motion.

## 1.3 What is a Band-Pass Signal?

A **band-pass signal** is one whose energy is concentrated in a finite frequency band that does **not** include the origin (DC, $f = 0$). It has a lower cutoff $f_L > 0$ and an upper cutoff $f_U$, with a bandwidth $B = f_U - f_L$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** A speech signal filtered to occupy 300 Hz – 3400 Hz, an FM radio channel at 88–108 MHz, or a wireless communication sub-band. These are inherently **not** low-pass — they have a non-zero center frequency.

This raises a critical KTU question: **Do we really need to sample at $f_s > 2f_U$ (twice the highest frequency), or can we sample at a much lower rate?**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The answer is the heart of **Band-Pass Sampling Theorem**: A band-pass signal of bandwidth $B$ can be reconstructed from samples taken at a rate $f_s \geq 2B$, **provided** the sampling frequency is chosen to avoid spectral overlap. This is the single most important high-yield concept in Module 4.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sampling Process — Mathematical Model

The uniform sampling of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is mathematically modelled as multiplication by an **impulse train**:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = x(t) \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\delta(t - nT_s)$$

### Step-by-step Logic of the Frequency-Domain Operation

1. **Time-domain multiplication** of two signals $x(t)$ and $p(t)$ maps to **frequency-domain convolution** of their spectra $X(f)$ and $P(f)$.
2. The Fourier Transform of an impulse train $p(t)$ is itself an impulse train in frequency: $P(f) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty}\delta(f - kf_s)$.
3. Convolution with this impulse train **replicates** $X(f)$ at every integer multiple of $f_s$, scaled by $\frac{1}{T_s}$.
4. Therefore: $X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - kf_s)$.

### 2.1.1 The Two Critical Conditions

| Condition | Mathematical Statement | Consequence if Violated |
|---|---|---|
| **No Aliasing (Nyquist Criterion)** | $f_s \geq 2f_M$ | Spectral replicas overlap → **irrecoverable** information loss |
| **No Spectral Gaps (Reconstructability)** | $f_s \leq 2f_M$ (i.e., equality for minimum) | If $f_s > 2f_M$, replicas are separated by guard bands, easier filtering |

> [!NOTE]
> The Nyquist Rate is the *absolute theoretical lower bound*. In practice, engineers use a **guard band**, choosing $f_s = 2.2 f_M$ to $5 f_M$ depending on the application.

## 2.2 The Aliasing Phenomenon

When $f_s < 2f_M$, the shifted copies of $X(f)$ overlap. A frequency component at $f_0$ in the original signal appears (in the sampled spectrum) as if it were located at:

$$f_{alias} = \vert f_0 - kf_s \vert \quad \text{for the integer } k \text{ that brings the result into } [-f_s/2, f_s/2]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aliasing is irreversible.** Once frequencies overlap in the sampled spectrum, the original information is mathematically destroyed. It cannot be recovered by any filter.

## 2.3 Reconstruction via Ideal Low-Pass Filter

Provided the Nyquist criterion is satisfied, $X(f)$ can be recovered by passing $X_s(f)$ through an **ideal low-pass filter** of gain $T_s$ and cutoff $f_c = f_s/2 = f_M$:

$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$$

This is the famous **Whittaker–Shannon Interpolation Formula**.

## 2.4 Sampling of Band-Pass Signals — The Core Theory

Let $x(t)$ be a real band-pass signal with spectrum non-zero only in $[f_L, f_U]$ and $[-f_U, -f_L]$, where $0 < f_L < f_U$ and bandwidth $B = f_U - f_L$.

The **Band-Pass Sampling Theorem** (sometimes called the **Holloway / Kohlenberg Theorem** in its general form) states:

> A real band-pass signal of bandwidth $B$ can be reconstructed from its samples if the sampling rate satisfies $f_s \geq 2B$, provided that **no aliasing** occurs among the spectral replicas and the replicas do not overlap with the original.

### 2.4.1 Allowed Sampling Frequencies (KTU Favourite)

For a band-pass signal in $[f_L, f_U]$, the sampling frequency must satisfy:

$$f_s \geq 2B = 2(f_U - f_L)$$

To avoid overlap, $f_s$ must additionally satisfy:

$$f_s \leq 2f_L \quad \text{or} \quad f_s \text{ falls within carefully defined "guard" intervals}$$

More precisely, the **allowed ranges** for $f_s$ are:

$$\frac{2f_U}{k+1} \leq f_s \leq \frac{2f_L}{k} \quad \text{for integer } k = 1, 2, 3, \dots, k_{max}$$

where $k_{max} = \lfloor f_U / B \rfloor$ is the largest integer such that $f_L > B$ (i.e., the signal is not "low-pass-like").

### 2.4.2 Special Case: Quadrature / IF Sampling

In modern digital communication receivers (software-defined radio), the IF (intermediate frequency) signal is often a real band-pass signal centered at a high $f_c$. Sampling it at $f_s = 2B$ (the minimum) places the band around $f_s/4$, allowing the use of complex baseband processing.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sampled spectrum | $X_s(f) = \dfrac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - kf_s)$ | Replication every $f_s$ Hz |
| 2 | Nyquist Rate | $f_s^{\min} = 2f_M$ | For low-pass signal of max freq. $f_M$ |
| 3 | Nyquist Interval | $T_s^{\max} = \dfrac{1}{2f_M}$ | Maximum allowed sampling period |
| 4 | Bandwidth of BP signal | $B = f_U - f_L$ | |
| 5 | Band-pass min. rate | $f_s^{\min} = 2B$ | **Not** $2f_U$! |
| 6 | Allowed $f_s$ ranges | $\dfrac{2f_U}{k+1} \leq f_s \leq \dfrac{2f_L}{k}$ | $k = 1, 2, \dots$ |
| 7 | Reconstruction | $x(t) = \sum x(nT_s)\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\dfrac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$ | Whittaker–Shannon formula |
| 8 | Aliasing equation | $f_{alias} = \vert f_0 - kf_s \vert$ | Brings $f_0$ into $[-f_s/2, f_s/2]$ |
| 9 | Guard band criterion | $f_s \geq 2(f_M + f_{GB})$ | Engineering practice |
| 10 | LPF cutoff for reconstruction | $f_c = f_s/2$ | Must be $\geq f_M$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse $2f_U$ with $2B$. For low-pass signals both are the same ($f_L = 0$, $B = f_U$). For **band-pass signals**, the correct minimum is $2B$, *not* $2f_U$. This single distinction carries 7 marks in a typical 14-mark KTU question.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** Band-pass sampling eliminates the need for an analog down-conversion stage, reducing cost and component count in SDR (Software Defined Radio).
- **Audio CD:** Uses $f_s = 44.1$ kHz for $f_M = 20$ kHz audio — a classic low-pass case.
- **Radar / Sonar:** IF signals are inherently band-pass; band-pass sampling allows direct digital processing.
- **Biomedical Imaging:** MRI and ultrasound often use band-pass sampling to reduce data rates.
